source.title: Q1 capacity slab & launch readiness handoff
source.summary: Cross-team handoff to align Q1 capacity slabs, staged degradation plans, and oncall responsibilities prior to launch window. Decided on slab sizes for canary and GA, agreed on fallback routing priorities, and created action items for quant/kv testing and SLA verification.
topics: capacity slabs (canary/ga)
fallback routing & kernel fallback
KV cache priming and eviction
batching profiles and latency SLOs
oncall handoff and runbook gaps
action_items: Owner: Diego Morales - Item: Run end-to-end quantized inference benchmark across slab sizes (4/8/16k tokens) - Due: 2026-12-22
Owner: Lena Morales - Item: Validate rollforward/rollback wiring in staging with simulated OOMs and report traces - Due: 2026-12-21
Owner: Priya Nair - Item: Finalize launch gating checklist and stakeholder signoff matrix - Due: 2026-12-18
Owner: Samir Patel - Item: Add explicit KV cache eviction testcases to chaos harness - Due: 2026-12-20
Owner: Elena Ruiz - Item: Publish updated oncall escalation matrix and schedule 1-hour walkthrough with SREs - Due: 2026-12-17
next_steps: Diego to share benchmark spreadsheet and perf traces
SREs to run simulated degradation test in staging on Wed
Product to circulate final gating doc for signoff by Friday
Ops to confirm capacity slab reservations with finance
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-15 15:00 UTC
Duration: 72 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Diego Morales, Priya Nair, Samir Patel, Elena Ruiz, Jasper King, Lena Morales, Noah Berg

Summary: quick handoff before holiday freeze; align on Q1 slab sizes and who owns what for the launch runway, make sure the fallback + kernel fallback story is covered, and get oncall ready.

[00:00] Ava Chen: okay, hey everyone, thanks for making time, we're a little tight so we'll try to be sharp, um, quick status - this is the Q1 capacity slab and launch readiness handoff.
[00:22] Jasper King: yep, thanks Ava. From eng we want to make sure the canary will exercise kernel fallback and batching under real-ish load. There's still a few unknowns on quant profiles.
[00:45] Diego Morales: just to call out, the quantized kernels look fine on paper but we saw a skew in latency at 8k tokens under the 3080 backfill test, so we need to validate on the actual slab config. Could be a kernel selection thing or memory layout.
[01:10] Priya Nair: timeline wise we need canary in staging by Jan 6th, and GA in the week of Jan 20th. Stakeholders expect a clear gating doc by Dec 18.
[01:35] Lena Morales: from SRE, the oncall rota is mostly covered but we need the runbook updates for staged degradation - there were a couple of unclear steps around fallback routing.
[01:58] Noah Berg: and we also have finance needing the slab numbers for reserved capacity quotes by Dec 22.
[02:22] Ava Chen: ok so let's go slab-by-slab. Diego, what are your recommended sizes?
[02:35] Diego Morales: my take: for canary we should reserve a small slab equivalent to 4 x A100-80g nodes (we're mapping to GPU-equivalents), run the batching profile at 200 rps sustained with bursts to 600 for 5m to exercise autoscale. For GA we'd slice to 16 nodes or equivalent which gives headroom for unexpected KV cache misses.
[03:05] Jasper King: why not 8 for canary? We want to catch oom edgecases without burning too much quota.
[03:20] Diego Morales: 8 is safe but costs double; with 4 and targeted chaos we can recreate OOMs quickly. Also 4 forces kernel fallback earlier which is good to validate.
[03:45] Priya Nair: we need to be explicit in the gating doc about acceptable error rates and latency tail. We proposed p95 latency under 350ms and p99 under 1.2s for the main text-gen path, with a special class for long-form >8k token runs.
[04:10] Samir Patel: just a note - when kernel fallback triggers we often see a memory spike due to different allocator paths; that needs to be counted in the capacity math. Also the KV cache priming strategy matters: if priming fails we get more cold-start traffic which multiplies token compute.
[04:40] Ava Chen: good. Diego, can you add memory spike numbers to the slab calc?
[04:48] Diego Morales: yes, I'll add scenarios: cold KV hit rate 0.6, warm 0.9, and two quant profiles.
[05:12] [background crosstalk] Elena Ruiz: sorry, I'm adding the doc link in chat — deck: /docs/q1-slab-handoff (placeholder)
[05:30] [06:00] Lena Morales: on staged degradation - need to be precise: when we hit degraded capacity we want to first route low-priority tenants to lower-cost model variants, then run batching squeeze, then, if still over threshold, do per-route rate reductions. The order matters.
[06:30] Noah Berg: agreed. Also we need fallback region routing as a last resort; region fallback has bigger latencies but keeps availability.
[06:50] Priya Nair: we should capture acceptable functional degradation per product area, e.g., concise responses okay, hallucination-sensitive flows preserved.
[07:10] Diego Morales: technical detail: the route to fallback model variant requires model compatibility—some of our compressed quant variants don't support function-calling or structured outputs, so product needs to list which routes can accept a variant.
[07:40] Jasper King: that's important. We don't want to silently break function-calls in production.
[07:55] [08:10] Samir Patel: on KV cache: eviction policy right now is LRU with a size cap per slab; in some replay tests we saw cascading evictions under heavy prefix churn. We have an open PR with TTL-aware eviction that might help.
[08:40] Diego Morales: TTL-aware seems promising but it needs perf validation because the eviction metadata overhead increased wallclock here.
[09:05] Ava Chen: ok, let's note: Samir to add TTL-aware to chaos harness for perf validation.
[09:15] Samir Patel: noted.
[09:30] [10:00] Priya Nair: gating doc point — we need telemetry hooks for token-level cost, model-fallback metrics, and slab utilization. Ops needs that to make reserve-vs-burst decisions.
[10:25] Elena Ruiz: Ops will need a dashboard - Priya can you specify 5 must-have panels?
[10:38] Priya Nair: yes: slab utilization, request queue latency, KV miss rate, model fallback count, and error-class breakdown.
[10:55] [11:20] Lena Morales: and we need alert thresholds mapped to runbook steps, e.g., slab cpu>85% for 3m triggers batching squeeze.
[11:40] Diego Morales: small note on batching: continuous batching helps throughput but inflates p99 when request arrival is bursty; we're going to iterate the batch timeout from 8ms to 25ms in staging to find the knee.
[12:05] Jasper King: can we schedule a micro-benchmark run specifically for batch timeout tuning?
[12:15] Diego Morales: yes, I'll run a param sweep and post results.
[12:30] [13:00] Priya Nair: resilience — what about the kernel fallback reliability? We had one incident in October where fallback didn't engage due to a malformed kernel choice mapping. Has that been fixed?
[13:25] Samir Patel: root cause was an enum mismatch in kernel selector; fix merged but we need a canary that forces the codepath.
[13:40] Diego Morales: I prototyped a forced-fallback flag; we'll enable in staging and run a 30m soak.
[14:00] [14:30] Ava Chen: good. Who owns the soak and confirms traces?
[14:38] Diego Morales: I can own soak and initial trace collection, Lena will run the oncall sim and check alerts.
[14:50] Lena Morales: I can do that Wed morning.
[15:05] [15:40] Jasper King: on cost — finance asked for conservative and optimistic slab cost profiles. We should give both so procurement can structure reserved vs burst.
[15:58] Priya Nair: I'll prepare both profiles; optimistic assumes 20% KV hit-rate improvement from caching, conservative assumes no improvement.
[16:20] [16:50] Samir Patel: another risk: quant skew between training/export and serving kernels. We need to validate the saved quant artifacts produce identical logits up to tolerance.
[17:10] Diego Morales: yep I'll add a diff test to the benchmark pipeline that compares fp16 vs int8 outputs across 1000 sample prompts. We should log any top-1 mismatch and aggregate KL divergence.
[17:40] [18:05] Elena Ruiz: adding that as an item for Diego in the action items.
[18:20] [18:50] Priya Nair: runbook gaps — I found two vague steps: 1) who escalates to model team on hallucination regression; 2) rollback criteria for partial rollouts. We need precise SLAs on decision time.
[19:15] Jasper King: for escalation it's product -> SE -> model owner chain, but we need contact handles.
[19:30] Noah Berg: rollback criteria: if quality regression metric crosses threshold for 10 minutes on canary, auto-stop and alert p1. Product will specify metric and threshold.
[19:55] [20:20] Diego Morales: small nit - the telemetry agent sometimes drops traces when sampling too low, so make sure sampling policy covers fallback events.
[20:40] Lena Morales: will bump sampling for the canary to preserve those traces.
[21:00] [21:30] Ava Chen: ok let's do a quick read of the proposed gating checklist items: signoffs required, perf targets, canary slab size, GA slab size, runbook updated, chaos tests passed, quant verification, KV eviction validated.
[22:05] Priya Nair: reading out — signoffs: Product, SRE, Release, and Finance. Perf targets: p95<350ms, p99<1.2s. Canary: 4-node equiv; GA: 16-node equiv. Runbook updated: yes/no. Chaos tests: soak + OOM + KV churn. Quant verification: diff test.
[22:45] Jasper King: make sure to include fallback compatibility matrix per route.
[23:05] [23:40] Samir Patel: on the compatibility matrix — I can produce a draft mapping in two days, with notes on function-calling limitations.
[24:00] Diego Morales: I'll sync with Samir on which quant variants are allowed for each route.
[24:20] [25:00] Elena Ruiz: scheduling note — we should have a 60m dry-run with oncall on Dec 29 to walk through the runbook. Who's available?
[25:20] Lena Morales: I'll be oncall. Samir and Diego can join for the tech bits.
[25:40] Priya Nair: product will be there for signoff simulation.
[26:10] [26:45] Ava Chen: cool. Let's capture the known risks: kernel selector mismatch, KV eviction storms, quant skew, and telemetry sampling gaps. Anything else?
[27:10] Jasper King: region fallback readiness — we need a short test to ensure cross-region model variant routing doesn't break model variant lookups.
[27:30] Diego Morales: I'll add that to the soak matrix.
[27:50] [28:30] Priya Nair: stakeholder comms - we'll share the gating doc and a 2-slide summary with execs on Dec 18.
[28:50] Elena Ruiz: I'll draft the slides and circulate tomorrow.
[29:10] [29:40] Lena Morales: a practical question - who triggers capacity cleanup post-canary? We don't want to leave reserved slabs burning.
[29:58] Noah Berg: Release Lead (that's me) will run cleanup and confirm reservation changes with finance.
[30:20] [30:50] Diego Morales: about the chaos harness tests - I want to include a 'slow kernel' injection where kernel executes with artificial delay to emulate poor kernel selection.
[31:05] Samir Patel: good idea. Also include a TTL eviction flurry to test cascading eviction effects.
[31:30] [32:00] Priya Nair: can we define the exact dataset for the quality regression checks? We should lock a representative prompt-set by Dec 17.
[32:20] Jasper King: I'll propose the initial prompt-set and we can iterate.
[32:40] [33:05] Ava Chen: ok we're about halfway. Quick check - any blockers for the items we defined?
[33:20] Diego Morales: blocker is time for benchmarks - they take 24-48h for meaningful runs.
[33:35] Priya Nair: resource blocker: need finance confirmation for reserved slab for GA to start procurement.
[33:50] Noah Berg: finance has the request in flight, expect reply by Dec 22.
[34:10] [34:40] Elena Ruiz: ops note - we should tag the canary so billing and metrics are isolated.
[34:55] Samir Patel: will add a canary tag in the deployment manifest.
[35:10] [35:40] Diego Morales: question for product - long-form >8k token routes: do they get special handling in fallback?
[35:58] Priya Nair: yes, long-form gets a separate SLA and we prefer graceful degradation (truncate or summarize) rather than switching model variant that loses structure.
[36:20] Jasper King: add that to the compatibility matrix.
[36:40] [37:00] Lena Morales: oncall handoff detail - the first 12 hours after canary start should be a 'war room' period with SRE + SE online. We should have escalation contacts available.
[37:25] Ava Chen: accepted. Elena can schedule the war room.
[37:40] Elena Ruiz: will schedule and send invites.
[38:00] [38:30] Priya Nair: decision log item - we agreed to force kernel fallback in staging soak to validate paths.
[38:45] Diego Morales: noted.
[39:05] [39:40] Samir Patel: minor regression from the last canary: trace IDs were stripped in a proxy layer causing split traces; we've fixed proxy config but need to verify in staging.
[40:00] Lena Morales: will check trace continuity in the soak runs.
[40:25] [40:55] Jasper King: any legal/compliance flags?
[41:10] Priya Nair: none for this stage; data residency is addressed by region routing. For private deployments we'll have separate discussion.
[41:35] [42:05] Diego Morales: I'll upload perf traces to the spreadsheet and link it in the doc.
[42:25] Elena Ruiz: file link posted: /sheets/q1-slab-perf.xlsx (placeholder)
[42:40] [43:00] Ava Chen: near the end - let's recap action owners. Diego runs bench and quant diff; Samir adds TTL eviction to chaos; Lena runs oncall sim; Priya finalizes gating doc and prompt-set; Elena schedules war room and slides; Noah runs cleanup & finance coordination; Jasper drafts compatibility matrix with model-feature mapping.
[43:35] [44:10] Priya Nair: I'll send the signoff tracker after this call.
[44:25] Diego Morales: timeline check: all must be done to sign off by Dec 22 for finance and Dec 29 for dry-run.
[44:45] Ava Chen: agree. Any final risks?
[45:00] Jasper King: only that quant skew may require a rollback plan for certain routes.
[45:20] Samir Patel: also a reminder that kernel fallback perf regression might require temporary throttling to maintain SLOs.
[45:40] [46:00] Ava Chen: ok thanks everyone. We'll send minutes, action items, and the gating doc link within 24h.
[46:18] Diego Morales: quick follow - I may need more GPU quota to run the sweeps; filing a request with infra.
[46:35] Lena Morales: I'll coordinate oncall schedule for the dry-run.
[46:50] Elena Ruiz: I'll prepare the two-slide exec summary and share tomorrow EOD.
[47:10] Priya Nair: closing note - let's avoid feature creep in the gating doc; keep it minimal and verifiable.
[47:30] Ava Chen: agreed. Meeting adjourned.
[71:55] <automated transcription end - silence>

source.title: Ramp dress rehearsal and rollout checklist review
topics: traffic ramp schedule
canary gating and feature flags
capacity contingency
rollback and fallback behavior
SLO impact and observability
security/compliance signoffs
handoff to oncall and runbook updates
source.summary: Quick internal dress rehearsal to validate rollout checklist for Q4 private deployment ramp. We walked through the canary schedule, gating criteria, capacity safety nets, and oncall handoff. Outstanding items: final load test results, KMS signoff for Private image, and small updates to the runtime fallback playbook. Agreed to a tighter rollback SLA and an explicit KV cache warmup window.
action_items: Ethan: schedule 6h load run against staging with real sequence dist by 2026-11-05
Priya: update runtime fallback doc with new latency thresholds and publish by 2026-11-04
Sofia: add oncall escalation matrix to runbook and test pager flow by 2026-11-06
Jordan: finalize canary percent schedule and share with growth and infra by 2026-11-04
Maya: coordinate KMS/HSM checklist with Security + Legal by 2026-11-05
next_steps: Run staged load test and share raw metrics sheet
Lock transient capacity buffer and confirm dedicated pool allocations
Legal to confirm data residency and encryption notes for Private (action via Priyanka)
Dry-run pager and rollback with oncall in 48h
Prep customer-facing launch notes and escalation contact list
source.transcript:
[00:00] Alex Kim: ok everyone thanks for joining, we'll try to keep to 60 minutes, uh this is a dress rehearsal for the rollout checklist for the private region ramp. Priya, can you kick off with the canary plan?
[00:22] Priya Sharma: sure, yeah. So plan is 3 phases: 1% for 30m, 5% for 2 hours, 20% for 4 hours then ramp to full. gating is error rate <0.5% and p95 latency < 220ms. we also want to validate kernel fallback is not tripping under the batch shapes we saw in staging.
[01:01] Jordan Lee: small thing — can we add a prefix cache warmup window? last time cold KV cache caused the first 10 minutes to spike. propose a 15m warmup at 1% before we even start measuring gating.
[01:28] Priya Sharma: yeah that makes sense, ah, and there's the continuous batching thing — if we pre-warm with synthetic traffic the batching / sequence length dist will look different, so we should run with real patterns.
[01:46] Sofia Martinez: on that, we need the oncall flow spelled out: if kernel fallback happens, what telemetry do you want to see? right now we have an alert for any fallback > 0.1% but that was noisy. maybe make it thresholded by QPS and proportion of requests.
[02:10] Ethan Park: agree. also we should define a cap on autotune/quant during the ramp — we don't want background jobs changing kernel selection mid-canary. lock config for the window.
[02:28] Alex Kim: so add to checklist: lock kernel/quant config, schedule the warmup, and set fallback alert thresholds. Jordan, you ok to own the warmup slot item?
[02:41] Jordan Lee: yes, I'll own that.
[02:45] [00:03:12] Maya Chen: quick legal note — for private customers in EU we need explicit KMS key rotation alignment, Priyanka is on the call but not active here, Ben from Security might need to sign off.
[03:02] Alex Kim: thanks. Ben?
[03:05] Ben Holt: yeah so the ask is to produce the KMS usage doc and confirm HSM boundaries; we can do expedited review if Maya drops the checklist item.
[03:19] Priya Sharma: switching to capacity contingency — we recommend a 25% safety buffer on dedicated pools and an emergency burst policy that can spin up a small number of on-demand workers. we've got a rough cost estimate but need finance signoff.
[03:45] Ethan Park: 25% seems high but safe; maybe we can make it 15% plus a fast burst of 10% for 30m. that'll reduce the committed cost but still give headroom.
[03:59] Jordan Lee: ok capture that as variable: committed_buffer=15% burst_buffer=10% burst_duration=30m.
[04:13] Alex Kim: on rollback SLA — legal and customers want a clear window. We're proposing 15 minutes to full rollback once a serious degradation is detected. Is that operationally realistic?
[04:26] Sofia Martinez: 15 min is tight; remember DB migrations and cache drains. for this rollout we can require a soft-fail at 10m and hard rollback at 30m. but we can try to meet 15m if prep runs perfectly.
[04:44] Priya Sharma: we should add automatic fallback to a smaller model variant if the heavy model is causing tail latency. automatic fallbacks must preserve correctness checks — we need a metric for semantic drift.
[05:02] Alex Kim: semantic drift metric — good call. add to action items: define a lightweight accuracy set to run against fallbacks.
[05:12] [00:06:00] Jordan Lee: question about observability: do we have a per-route token-level cost breakdown in the dashboard for canary traffic? finance will want to estimate spend during ramp.
[05:28] Ethan Park: yes-ish, but it aggregates. need to expose a canary tag so we can slice traffic; we'll enable a canary header and feed it into the billing pipeline. will need one-off infra job.
[05:46] Alex Kim: add infra job to list.
[05:50] Maya Chen: Ops note — coordinate support rotations and ensure CSM is aware. we'll send a customer-facing incident contact doc.
[06:02] Priya Sharma: small gotcha — the qc tests in staging used a 2k max length; production will have some longer sequences. we haven't validated kernel selection for 4k sequences at high concurrency.
[06:20] Ethan Park: action: run targeted 4k concurrency test. I will add to the load run.
[06:28] Sofia Martinez: can someone remind me of the fallback signal chain? I want to see the alert > triage > decide > rollback steps.
[06:36] Jordan Lee: currently it's: fallback alert -> oncall page -> SE triage -> if >threshold then canary paused and ops notified -> if persistent then rollback initiated. we should enumerate exact metrics and owners.
[06:51] [00:10:15] Alex Kim: let's role-play one scenario: steady increase in p95 to 280ms over 20m but error rate stable. what do we do?
[10:23] Priya Sharma: we would look at kernel fallback logs and model variant metrics; if latency is from KV misses, we trigger more warm cache prefetch; if from attention kernel, we might fallback to cached KV variant or smaller model.
[10:41] Sofia Martinez: alerting wise, p95 breach alone should move to alert page but not auto-rollback. we want human in loop for latency-only issues.
[10:55] Ethan Park: make sure the tracing is hot — we need request-level trace ids in the first 2 minutes after the event to debug.
[11:05] Jordan Lee: noted.
[11:09] [00:12:00] Maya Chen: onboarding/legal — anything missing for compliance? Priyanka's review is pending, guess we should attach the compliance checklist to the runbook.
[12:22] Ben Holt: I'll do a quick pass on the KMS notes, but we need the final architecture doc. without that, we can't sign off.
[12:36] Alex Kim: ok Ben will review. Priya, any last infra caveats before we move to timeline?
[12:45] Priya Sharma: yeah, if we lock quantization/auto-tuning, kernel fallback becomes the main risk; make sure the fallback paths are exercised with the warmup. also, disable model hot-swap during the window.
[13:02] Jordan Lee: timeline: Nov 5 run load in staging; Nov 6 dry-run pager; Nov 7 canary start (1%), Nov 8 ramp to 5%, Nov 9 20% etc — flexible based on gating.
[13:24] Alex Kim: fine. last chunk: communications. who publishes the customer-facing notes and the internal runbook versioning?
[13:33] Maya Chen: I'll publish the public notes and the escalation list. Jordan will keep the runbook in Confluence and tag version.
[13:44] Alex Kim: cool. wrapup: quick recap and owners. Ethan, Priya, Jordan, Sofia, Maya — you all have items. we will reconvene after the load run for a go/no-go.
[13:57] [00:20:01] Priya Sharma: small callout — we should add a blameless postmortem template to the runbook for any issues arising during ramp.
[20:18] Sofia Martinez: agreed. also schedule a short retro 48h after rollout end.
[20:26] Alex Kim: ok thanks everyone. I'll send meeting notes, checklist, and the updated action items. meeting adjourned.
[20:38] [00:21:15] End of recording: 62 minutes

source.title: All-Hands: Compass Review and Ops Autopsies
source.summary: Biannual operational all-hands focusing on runbook changes after recent incidents, product roadmap alignment for Q3, and a short leadership AMA. Emphasis on incident autopsies, measurement of latency SLOs, and capacity commitments for Dedicated customers. Action items captured for SRE, Product, and People. A few competitive mentions and follow-ups on security posture were raised.
topics: Incident autopsy: May 7th KV cache regression
Latency SLO and tail-latency mitigation plan
Dedicated capacity demand forecasting and reserve policy
Hiring cadence and org adjustments for Eng Ops
Security posture and upcoming SOC / ISO readiness review
Leadership AMA
action_items: Samir Desai: produce incident post-mortem doc + timeline and share w/ eng by 2026-05-28
Priya Rao: prioritize top-two user-impacting product bugs into sprint and communicate to customers by 2026-05-26
Ethan Park: validate kernel selection telemetry dashboard and add tail-latency graphs by 2026-06-02
Imani Brooks: run manager calibration on hiring throughput and publish updated hiring forecast by 2026-06-04
Avery Chen: circulate internal capacity ask template for Dedicated sales to fill out by 2026-05-24
next_steps: Share incident deck and annotated timeline with company (link in slack #ops)
Schedule cross-functional tabletop for high-latency region scenarios (owners: SRE/Product)
Run a dedicated customer communication about Dedicated capacity guarantees for high-commit customers
Book SOC 2 readiness workshop with security vendor — target week of 2026-06-15
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-05-21
Start time: 09:00 PT
Duration: ~1h14m
Attendees: Maya, Ethan, Priya, Samir, Janelle, Luca, Imani, Avery (host)
---
Summary: quick review of incidents and ops; product alignment; AMA at the end; lots of Qs on capacity and SOC readiness

[00:00] Avery: all right, good morning everyone thanks for joining on time, uh we wanted to keep this tight — compass review, a couple autopsies and then Qs, we'll go 65, 70 minutes max.

[00:24] Maya: thanks Avery, um yeah I just wanted to open with two things: gratitude to SRE for the late nights last week and a quick note that execs will be tighter on runway forecasts this quarter, so we'll be asking product and ops to align on measurable tradeoffs.

[00:42] Samir: yeah appreciate that, I— I can give the incident overview, um quick spoiler: root cause was a KV cache invalidation edge-case combined with a quantized kernel path that hadn't seen enough load in staging.

[01:05] Ethan: yep, the kernel selector didn't fall back fast enough, and because of continuous batching cold-starts we observed tail spikes. This is, uh, partly on telemetry granularity as well.

[01:28] Priya: can you say more about "not enough load in staging" — was it test coverage or configuration drift?

[01:37] Samir: a mix — we had a config flip that toggled an optimization flag for quantized models; it was enabled in canary but not exercised end-to-end. There was also a small bug in the prefix cache eviction logic that only manifests with long-shot KV density. So it took traffic to surface.

[02:02] Janelle: hi quick question — how many customers were impacted, like high-level numbers? we need to know which renewals to proactively call.

[02:12] Samir: preliminary numbers: about 12 customers had elevated latency for 12-40 minutes, two of those were Dedicated customers with SLA exposure. We're still validating exact windows.

[02:38] Luca: was there an automated fallback to a lower-cost model variant? or did routing keep hitting the same pool?

[02:46] Ethan: routing didn't detect the perf regression early enough — we didn't have a model-variant fallback rule engaged for that route. We'll add a policy that defaults to nearest compatible runtime when tail-latency breaches X ms.

[03:05] Maya: okay and how does this affect our messaging to the market? two things — we can't overpromise latency guarantees while this is open, and we should be transparent to affected customers.

[03:22] Samir: Agreed — we'll draft the post-mortem and a recommended customer communication, plus a mitigation roadmap.

[03:40] Priya: operationally, I want Product to produce a small eval harness that replays long-KV sequences against the quantized path so this doesn't slip by test coverage.

[03:52] Ethan: yes, I can own that — add a telemetry flag to force-path exercise in staging nightly so the kernel path sees representative sequences.

[04:10] Imani: on the people side, there were a lot of all-hands questions about on-call load and burnout. We should make sure managers are coordinating rotation and that we have clear post-incident support.

[04:25] Maya: good call — Samir can you include an on-call health check in the post-mortem?

[04:33] Samir: yeah will do.

[04:40] (cross talk)

[04:50] Avery: shifting slightly — roadmap. Priya, can you give the three product bets we want the company to focus on this quarter?

[05:01] Priya: sure — 1) rollout of improved batching heuristics to reduce per-token cost without degrading p95 latency, 2) Dedicated capacity self-serve flow for easier reserved commits, 3) model-variant fallback policies and a routing policy UI in Console.

[05:26] Janelle: for Dedicated self-serve, do we expect sales to get immediate capacity answers? the ask has come up from customers to see committed throughput in UI.

[05:38] Priya: immediate-ish — initial phase will show estimated capacity and queue time, actual provisioning may have 24–48 hour lead time for certain hardware types. We need a clear expectation in the UI copy.

[05:55] Luca: capacity forecasting is a blocker for Eng Ops; we need a template that sales can use to capture committed RPS and model profiles.

[06:05] Avery: noted — I will circulate a capacity ask template by EOD Monday.

[06:12] Maya: on the security front, we have a SOC 2 readiness checkpoint in mid-June, right? where are we with docs and artifact pull?

[06:23] Ethan: security has a runbook, but we need two things: evidence for access reviews and the encryption/KMS inventory for Private deployments. I'm coordinating with Security to get a vendor in.

[06:38] Priya: product needs to freeze any API contract changes for Private customers until the audit window is complete.

[06:47] Maya: yep freeze. No breaking changes during the audit window.

[07:00] (30s silence while people scroll docs)

[07:30] Samir: quick ops note — we've been seeing a subtle increase in tail latency in eu-west: latency p99 is up 18% month-over-month. I'm proposing a regional tabletop to rehearse capacity failover.

[07:50] Priya: do we have a runbook that maps model families to fallback variants? if not, that's product backlog item one.

[08:00] Ethan: we have a partial map; it's not comprehensive, and the mapping logic currently requires a manual entry — needs automation.

[08:12] Janelle: competitor note — Alt-AI Labs announced a low-cost multi-tenant offering in EU last week; some customers asked how ours compares.

[08:25] Maya: ok keep an eye — marketing will publish a short comparison but we should not go into pricing wars.

[08:37] Avery: we're ~40 minutes in; let's move into leadership AMA. Please keep questions tight — we'll do lightning rounds.

[08:50] (Audience Q1 from Eng: remote first policy, hiring and budget)

[09:02] Imani: on hiring — we are pacing hires for top-of-funnel roles but tightening on non-customer-facing ICs this quarter. We'll publish a revised hiring cadence doc.

[09:15] Eng attendee (Speaker 7): will we still allow cross-team rotations for people on project X?

[09:22] Maya: yes rotations ok, but managers need to sign off and ensure deliverables are covered.

[09:30] (murmurs)

[09:45] Finance question (Speaker 9): what's the runway threshold that triggers an immediate hiring freeze?

[09:54] Maya: when runway dips below 9 months without committed growth plans, we will reassess hiring. That's the current threshold.

[10:08] (brief pause)

[10:20] Avery: back to ops autopsies — Samir you had a remediation timeline?

[10:27] Samir: immediate: revert flag in canary and add a forced exercise in staging by 2026-05-24. Short-term: telemetry and dashboard updates by 2026-06-02. Medium: automated routing fallback and variant map by 2026-06-30.

[10:52] Priya: for product roadmaps we will pull two weeks of dev time to ship the eval harness. I'll work with Eng leads to prioritize.

[11:05] Ethan: also planning to add kernel-path sampling so we can get representative traces from cold starts. This helps kernel selection heuristics.

[11:20] Luca: Eng Ops will prototype an emergency capacity quick switch for Dedicated customers — less friction to escalate.

[11:36] Janelle: sales will prepare a customer outreach script and account list; we'll prioritize calls for the two impacted Dedicated customers.

[11:48] Maya: good, let's make sure comms are coordinated.

[12:00] (wrap up)

[12:10] Avery: quick recap: Samir to post post-mortem and health-check; Priya to prioritize harness and product bugs; Ethan telemetry and kernel dashboards; Imani hiring forecast; Avery will send capacity template. We'll follow up in #ops and book a tabletop.

[12:32] Maya: thanks everyone for the candor and speed — appreciate the cross-functional energy.

[12:45] (meeting closes)

---
Notes on transcript quality: auto-transcript, some speaker labels generic and there are occasional misheard acronyms (e.g., KV cache heard as "K-V cash"), timestamps approximate.

source.title: OOM Simulations and Launch Contingency Tabletop
source.summary: Tabletop exercise to rehearse OOM scenarios against Dedicated pools and Hosted fallbacks ahead of the planned model rollout. We ran through simulated failure modes, prioritization for mitigation playbooks, and assigned short-term operational runbooks and longer-term infra tasks. Key outcome: sequence of tactical checkpoints (stress tests, improved observability, updated canary abort criteria) and explicit owners for each contingency step.
topics: OOM simulation scenarios
canary/rollback gating
KV cache warmup and prefix caching
burst autoscaler behavior
observability gaps (tracing + per-token latency)
postmortem playbook updates
next_steps: Jon to schedule three staged OOM stress runs by 2026-04-29
Priya to add per-model memory heatmaps to Grafana by 2026-05-05
Luca to prototype a lightweight fallback that serves a quantized variant by 2026-05-12
Sofia to update canary abort runbook and SLO thresholds by 2026-04-30
action_items: Jon Park - run 3x stress jobs (dedicated pool) and share logs - due 2026-04-29
Priya Nair - add memory heatmap dashboard and link to release page - due 2026-05-05
Luca Moretti - prototype 4-bit quantized fallback and document perf delta - due 2026-05-12
Sofia Reyes - update canary abort checklist and add automated slack alert - due 2026-04-30
Mina Kapoor - coordinate dry-run schedule with CSM and notify on-call roster - due 2026-04-26
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-04-22 14:00 PDT
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava (PM), Jon (SRE), Priya (Backend), Luca (Serving), Sofia (Release), Mina (Product Ops), Oscar (Infra Planner), Taryn (QA)

SUMMARY (auto): rehearsed OOM failure modes; agreed tactical tests and owners; found two observability gaps. Linked artifacts: deck: https://redwood internal/oom-tabletop-deck (internal), stress script repo: git://infra/stress-sim, perf spreadsheet: https://sheets.redwood/oom-results

00:00:12 Ava (PM): alright folks let's kick off, table top for oom sims, we have about an hour and fifteen. goal is to rehearse, um, and land owners for the rollout next month.
00:00:37 Jon (SRE): mm-hmm, quick context — last week's canary hit a memory spike when seq len jumped to ~9k for a small set of calls, the dedicated pool had a node that OOM'd and autobalancer shuffled, but the fallback path was slow so the user saw a time out.
00:01:05 Priya (Backend): to add, the KV cache wasn't fully warmed so the cache pressure amplified memory growth. might be a warmup issue more than kernel OOM but the symptom is node death.
00:01:30 Oscar (Infra Planner): for the exercise let's assume two scenarios: a) steady traffic with tail seqs causing steady mem creep, b) short burst (traffic spike) and autoscaler doesn't spin up fast enough.
00:01:56 Luca (Serving): noted. also a variant: inference kernel regression that increases per-token memory by 15% after a patch — that one is tricky cause it sneaks in.
00:02:22 Sofia (Release): so scenario listing: seq tail, autoscale lag, kernel regression, and cross-tenant noisy neighbor (shared host). we'll walk each.
00:02:47 Taryn (QA): i'll own reproductions in the staging harness; i need to know exact call shapes. can you attach sample requests?
00:03:10 Ava: yes — linked in the deck, but we'll also add an anonymized request set to the stress script repo, good call.
00:03:35 [00:03:37] Jon: scenario 1 - steady tail. step 1: detect rising mem per node. do we have an alert today?
00:03:55 Priya: we have node mem >= 85% alert but it fires late; we don't track per-model or per-queue mem well. current metric is host-level.
00:04:20 Jon: that's the observability gap — we need per-pod memory@percentile and per-model allocation.
00:04:42 Luca: we can emit per-actor alloc via our runtime shim, but there's an overhead. i estimate 5% perf hit if we sample at 1s.
00:05:05 Sofia: can we sample at 5s for production? tradeoff is coarser lens but less overhead.
00:05:28 Priya: 5s sampling seems reasonable for a first pass. Taryn, can QA validate sampling artifacts?
00:05:42 Taryn: yes, i'll validate the dashboards and ensure the CSV export lines up with load tests.
00:06:10 Jon: action item — add per-model memory heatmaps to grafana, show p50/p90/p99 by pod. Also, add an alert that triggers at p95 mem growth rate over 5m window.
00:06:30 Ava: agreed. next, teleport into the canary gating — what's the abort criteria today?
00:06:45 Sofia: today it's latency and error budget burn. there's no explicit memory regressions in the abort rules; we rely on manual eyeballing of grafana.
00:07:03 Luca: we should automate a canary abort trigger: any host OOM or p99 memory > threshold for two consecutive 5m windows should abort canary.
00:07:25 Jon: plus an early-warning slack alert to the on-call channel with a quick link to the trace and the recent allocations.
00:07:40 Priya: side note: traces often lack per-token memory markers; we can insert lightweight allocation markers at token boundaries but that changes traces.
00:08:02 Mina (Product Ops): how long to implement all that? we need short-term mitigations for launch day and long-term fixes.
00:08:15 Jon: short term: update canary abort rule and add two additional alerts, run staged stress tests to validate. medium term: per-model heatmaps and sampling. long term: runtime kernel audit and quantized fallback.
00:08:42 Ava: let's run a dry-run next week with the on-call rotated into the scenario. Jon, can you schedule with Mina?
00:08:55 Jon: yes — i'll schedule for next Tue and include the stress harness.
00:09:10 [00:09:12] Scenario drill: autoscale lag.
00:09:18 Oscar: our autoscaler has a 60s warmup for GPUs and a conservative burst policy; at 3x normal it's often too slow. we have a burst pool but it's limited by quota.
00:09:38 Luca: we can provision an emergency burst lane that accepts limited models with stricter token limits during launch window. it's effectively a low-memory flavor (shorter seq cap + batch cap).
00:10:02 Priya: that feels like a dedicated "spring" pool. question: routing logic — can we push traffic to the quantized variant automatically?
00:10:18 Jon: we already have model variants. routing policy can do fallback based on latency targets, but not yet automatic on memory alerts. we can add that rule.
00:10:35 Sofia: ensure we don't accidentally route high-paying customers to degraded variants. add route tag to preserve tenant priority.
00:10:48 Mina: note — legal asked about audit logs for any automated routing changes. we need to capture those decisions in the release page.
00:11:05 Ava: ok, action item: Sofia to add automated routing audit entries to release checklist.
00:11:22 [00:11:24] Scenario drill: kernel regression.
00:11:28 Luca: this is the scary one. if we ship a kernel with a regression that increases allocs, canary may pass at low traffic and fail at scale. two mitigations: tighter canary traffic profile that includes edge seq lengths and an A/B rollback plan.
00:11:52 Priya: also add a canary that ramps to higher sequence lengths artificially in staging. Taryn will set up the test harness.
00:12:10 Taryn: yes — i'll prepare a seq-tail suite and include model-level memory assertions.
00:12:28 Jon: we should also maintain a per-deploy memory baseline run in CI where we run a 30-min perf harness against the model binary. if the new build shows >10% mem increase we block.
00:12:48 Ava: can we run that in CI without blowing our quotas? if it's expensive we need to gate it to release candidate builds only.
00:13:04 Luca: we can sample smaller batches for CI but it's not perfect; better to have a nightly dedicated run on infra.
00:13:20 Oscar: scheduling a nightly baseline is fine, but we need capacity planning. i'll work with infra finance on a small nightly pool.
00:13:34 [00:13:36] Interruption — someone: "sorry, can you repeat the abort rule?"
00:13:39 Sofia: abort if host OOM OR p99 memory > threshold for two consecutive 5m windows OR observed allocation rate spike > X.
00:13:55 Priya: we should keep X small, like 20% increase over baseline in allocation rate.
00:14:05 Jon: add a two-step abort: auto-abort then manual review if there are false positives from noisy tenants.
00:14:18 Ava: good. next scenario: noisy neighbor.
00:14:24 Oscar: if another tenant suddenly consumes shared GPU memory (misconfig), we need per-tenant caps on node scheduling and stronger isolation.
00:14:38 Luca: node-level cgroups for GPU aren't clean, but we can prefer strict pod packing with anti-affinity. also a proactive eviction policy for bad actors.
00:14:55 Priya: we also need better labeling of tenants so ops can immediately identify noisy pods.
00:15:10 Mina: i'll add tenant labeling to the release checklist and ensure CSMs inform big tenants about expected behavior.
00:15:25 [00:15:27] Breakout mini-run: stress test plan review.
00:15:30 Jon: stress script will have 3 phases: slow ramp (1h), peak traffic spike (10m), and sustained tail traffic (45m). we will measure mem, p99 latency, allocations, and KV cache hit rate.
00:15:55 Taryn: QA will run the harness against staging, log results to the perf spreadsheet, and flag regressions.
00:16:12 Priya: detail — KV cache warmup: include a pre-warm phase where we prime the prefix cache to 80% hit before real traffic. compare warm vs cold runs.
00:16:30 Luca: add a metric for KV compacting events; if compactions spike during the run that's a red flag.
00:16:45 Jon: action item — Jon to run 3 runs: cold, warm, kernel-stress (with induced higher alloc) and post results.
00:17:02 Ava: make sure to include single-tenant and multi-tenant runs.
00:17:20 Oscar: also simulate quota exhaustion for the burst pool to measure fallback latency.
00:17:33 Priya: question on fallback: should fallback be to a smaller model or the same model quantized?
00:17:44 Luca: prefer quantized variant for behavior parity; smaller model hurts semantics. but quantization has perf tradeoffs; we'll measure.
00:17:58 Sofia: note on rollout communications — addition to the release notes: explicit fallback behavior and customer-facing guidance.
00:18:12 Mina: i'm going to draft a quick-run customer comms blurb for high-usage customers to set expectations.
00:18:25 [00:18:27] Mid-call drift into previous incident postmortem notes.
00:18:30 Jon: referencing the incident log from 2026-03-18 — root cause still partly unknown; we found artifact of a bad pointer in the kernel that sometimes caused an allocation leak. that was patched but we need regression tests.
00:18:55 Priya: we've added unit tests but not enough integration coverage. we need a synthetic that reproduces the leak.
00:19:10 Luca: the leak was tied to long-context LLM requests — we should add a long-context mode test.
00:19:26 Taryn: QA will build a long-context suite and run nightly.
00:19:40 Ava: ok action: Priya build synthetic leak repro and put in repo.
00:19:52 Priya: noted.
00:20:05 [00:20:08] Discussion: runbook ergonomics and on-call drills.
00:20:12 Jon: current runbook is 6 steps but it's ambiguous on who does step 3. we need explicit page with triage checklist and checklist for abort vs manual rollback.
00:20:30 Sofia: also we should build a short script that can automatically collect a minimal triage bundle (last 5m logs, top pids, allocation histogram) and upload to the incident channel.
00:20:48 Jon: i'll prototype that collector this week.
00:21:00 Mina: and schedule a 30-min on-call drill next Tue where the on-call runs the checklist with synthetic failures.
00:21:10 Ava: good — last 20 minutes: prioritize tasks for launch readiness.
00:21:20 [00:21:23] Prioritization table readout.
00:21:26 Ava: red = must-have before launch (canary abort automation, per-model mem alert, scheduled stress runs), amber = high priority (quantized fallback prototype, night baseline), green = nice-to-have (allocation tracing at 1s).
00:21:50 Priya: estimated effort: canary automation (2 dev days), per-model mem alert (3 dev days + 1 infra day), stress runs (2 infra days), quantized fallback prototype (1.5 eng weeks).
00:22:15 Oscar: infra quota approval might add lead time for nightly runs, start finance request now.
00:22:30 Ava: assign owners and dates — see action items.
00:22:42 [00:22:45] Quick heat-map demo by Priya (shared screen note): p99 mem spike observable when sequence > 6k.
00:23:05 Priya: this is noisy but you can see clear slope. if we cap seq to 4k in burst lane mem stays bounded.
00:23:20 Luca: tradeoff again: capping seq impacts customer experience for some customers. we need to notify them.
00:23:32 Mina: communications template will include opt-in for launch spike protection with degraded seq cap.
00:23:45 [00:23:47] Quick flag about observability gaps: missing token-level allocation markers in traces.
00:23:51 Jon: tracer insertion at token boundaries could help, will prototyping will take 2 sprints.
00:24:05 Ava: wrap up — let's recap owners and run cadence.
00:24:12 [00:24:14] Recap readout.
00:24:18 Ava: Jon runs stress jobs and delivers logs by 4/29. Priya adds heatmaps by 5/5. Luca prototypes quantized fallback by 5/12. Sofia updates canary abort runbook by 4/30 and adds audit logging. Mina coordinates dry-run next Tue and drafts comms. Taryn prepares test harness and long-context suite. Oscar starts quota request.
00:24:48 Sofia: any blockers?
00:24:52 Oscar: only finance approval for nightly infra and a short SLA exception for the burst pool.
00:25:05 Priya: we might need acceptance from product for degraded behavioral fallback; Mina will coordinate.
00:25:20 Ava: ok, thanks everyone. we'll post the minutes and action items to the release page. meeting adjourned.

POST-MEETING NOTES (auto):
- Observability gap: need per-model/pod mem metrics and alerts (owner: Jon).
- Canary abort rules to be automated and audited (owner: Sofia).
- Stress harness runs: cold/warm/kernel-stress (owner: Jon & Taryn).
- Quantized fallback prototype to be evaluated for perf/semantics tradeoff (owner: Luca).

Referenced artifacts:
- Deck: internal link (oom-tabletop-deck)
- Stress harness repo: git://infra/stress-sim
- Perf spreadsheet: https://sheets.redwood/oom-results
- Incident postmortem: https://incidents.redwood/2026-03-18-kernel-leak

END TRANSCRIPT

source.transcript:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- "staircase-spec-v0" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.

title: Inference Capacity Staircase & Rollbacks Playbook
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- \"staircase-spec-v0\" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.

topics: beta exit criteria
rollback/rollback criteria
incident ownership & escalation
canary percentages and pacing
observability gaps found in incident
preflight test matrix
action_items: Marco Ruiz: Draft rollback playbook for model variants (due 2026-03-03)
Sophie Li: Add SLO delta alerts and runbook links into runbook repo (due 2026-03-02)
Marta Nguyen: Add two additional e2e tests for KV cache hotpath (due 2026-03-01)
Anya Feldman: Update canary gating doc with exact metrics and thresholds (due 2026-03-04)
next_steps: Share updated rollback playbook with launches channel
Schedule dry-run of rollback on staging next Thursday
Priya to run last week's incident trace with kernel logs and share notes
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-02-27
Start: 10:00 PST
Duration: 75 minutes
Attendees: Jordan, Priya, Anya, Marco, Sophie, Khaled, Marta, Devon, Lina

Summary (auto): quick sync to close open gating questions and make sure incident learnings are captured in the preflight checklist.

00:00 Jordan: okay good morning everyone thanks for jumping in on short notice, we're tight on the window so we'll run through incident takeaways and the beta gating items, quick round the table for any blockers before we start?
00:18 Khaled: just one note from product side — customers are pushing for the next alpha beta cut, timeline hasn't moved, but they want assurances on rollback and data retention, um so that's top of mind.
00:35 Priya: SE flagged two perf spikes on long tail sequences, those happen when sequence > 2k tokens and quantized kernels switch, might be kernel fallback thrash.
:50 Marta: QA — we found a nondeterministic failure in the kv cache eviction under load, reproduces in staging only intermittently, so we added a flaky test but haven't stabilized it.
01:05 Jordan: perfect ok let's first walk through the incident review from Sat night — Sophie you were oncall, can you summarise timeline and primary failure mode?
01:20 Sophie: yeah, so saturday 22:40 PST we started to see error rate creeping on C2 pool, it correlated with a spike on prefix cache miss combined with the kernel selection path for int8 models, the runtime fell back to a slower kernel which increased CPU pressure and a small threadpool exhaustion, that caused backpressure into the router and we saw elevated 5xxs for ~18 minutes.
01:45 Devon: i want to add the router's request queueing was a factor — we had a config that permitted a larger inflight window which amplified the burst.
02:00 Lina: observability note — trace sampling dropped during the spike because of a misconfigured sampler cap, so we lost a full picture for 7 minutes, that's on me to fix.
02:15 Anya: how much of that is kv cache policy vs scheduler config, Sophie?
02:28 Sophie: it's both, the eviction policy was too eager when memory pressure rose; eviction churn caused more misses which forced more kernel loads; scheduler then tried to reassign and that created context switches.
02:45 Priya: also worth noting the quantization heuristics we push at load time sometimes choose an int8 codepath that isn't validated for sequence lengths beyond 1k, so in production we should have a safety net preventing that path for models with long tail usage.
03:05 Jordan: ok concrete learning items — need throttles upstream, sampler fix, kv cache policy change, and a rollback criteria for model variant changes. Let's pivot to gating for beta exit: Marco walk us through the current checklist.
03:25 Marco: sure — we currently require: 1) canary at 5% for 24h with no SLO regressions, 2) two independent e2e load tests, 3) security signoff SOC2 attestation pending, 4) observability traces with 99th pct token latency collection, 5) rollback playbook. Unknowns: acceptable SLO delta threshold, and what we consider 'no SLO regressions' for short tails.
04:00 Khaled: product wants hard numbers, not fuzzy language — we need a delta metric like < +5% P99 latency and < +2% error rate over baseline, else fail gate.
04:20 Anya: i propose we use relative thresholds per route — global numbers hide routes with critical latency sensitive ones. canary leadership: some routes need 2% threshold.
04:40 Priya: technical concern — the P99 can be noisy for small canaries (5%), we should use percentile smoothing and also token-level SLOs instead of per-request.
05:05 Jordan: good point — Lina, can observability support token-level P99 smoothing for the canary period?
05:18 Lina: yes, but we need to increase trace retention for the canary to get good percentiles, that will incur some cost, i estimate ~2K extra GB/day for the 24h canary, might need to approve budget.
05:35 Khaled: product accepts short-term cost if we get confidence, budget is ok for the one-off.
05:50 Marta: from QA — we need reproducible tests for the kv hotpath, adding two e2e scenarios that stress intermixed short and long sequences.
06:05 Marco: noted. Now about rollback — current draft says: immediate traffic reroute to previous pinned model version and forced cache flush, with an emergency runbook to abort any async batching. Is that sufficient?
06:25 Sophie: cache flush is tricky — flushing all prefixes increases load and can cause secondary failures; prefer targeted flush for affected prefixes + fallback to smaller batch sizes.
06:40 Anya: agree, also we should disable quantized int8 variant automatically on rollback to reduce complexity.
06:55 Priya: and include a KV cache warmup plan post-rollforward.
07:10 Jordan: ok so rollback playbook needs: targeted prefix flush guidance, automatic disabling of int8 variant, step to reduce batch size, and who flips the circuit in router. Marco please draft.
07:25 Marco: will do.
07:33 Jordan: ownership changes — we want a clear incident owner pattern: first responder SRE, then Eng Mgr for tactical, PM for customer comms, and AE to coordinate external comms if needed. Any objections?
07:48 Khaled: product asks to be in the loop for any customer-facing comms early; AE only for escalations.
08:00 Jordan: fine, we'll set notification playbook.
08:10 Sophie: action on sampler cap — Lina, can you drop a PR to fix sampling so we capture full traces during a spike?
08:20 Lina: yes I'll do that today.
08:28 Jordan: timeline for the preflight checklist finalization? We need final version by EOD Friday to run a dry-run next week.
08:36 Anya: i can finish gating doc by Wednesday EOD and include thresholds per route.
08:43 Marta: QA tests done by Monday will be tight but possible.
08:50 Priya: i'll validate kernel fallback paths in staging tomorrow and document heuristics that choose int8 path.
09:00 Jordan: excellent. Now quick walk through the incident postmortem bullet points so we can insert into the PM notes — Sophie read the short postmortem paragraph.
09:12 Sophie: incident started with a kernel fallback due to cache misses which caused elevated CPU, threadpool exhaustion; mitigated by scaling router and temporarily decreasing inflight window; mitigation was manual once oncall identified the kernel hotpath; root cause: interplay of eviction policy and quantized kernel heuristics; detection gap: trace sampling and missing token-level SLOs; remediation: change eviction policy, fix sampling cap, add canary guardrails for quantized variants.
09:45 Jordan: good, i'll paste that into the postmortem doc.
09:53 Devon: note for release eng — ensure the release pipeline has a preflight stage that runs the eviction scenario with load shape to validate.
10:05 Anya: we'll add that to the pipeline checklist.
10:12 Jordan: ok time check — 45 minutes left, let's make decisions on canary percentage and the SLO deltas.
10:20 Marco: proposal: start at 5% for 24h, if stable extend to 20% for 6h, require token-level smoothed P99 delta < +4% and request error rate < +1.5% across routes for the 5% stage; if a critical route exceeds +2% request error increase stop and rollback.
10:45 Khaled: product pushes that critical route threshold be stricter — 1% for payment/sign-in routes.
10:55 Priya: one more ops note — we should cap inflight requests per backend to avoid queue amplification, that's a quick config change.
11:05 Sophie: quick follow-up on runbook owners: i'll take first responder role for weekends, Anya covers weekday tactical, Marco covers decision, Jordan signs off comms.
11:18 Jordan: perfect — let's recap decisions and owners.
11:25 Jordan: decisions: canary 5%/24h -> 20%/6h progression; token-level smoothed P99 delta threshold +4% general, +1% critical routes; request error delta < +1.5%; rollback playbook must include targeted prefix flush and int8 disable; sampler cap fix deployed before canary; test matrix expanded to include kv hotpath e2e; incident ownership pattern adopted.
11:55 Jordan: next steps — Marco will draft rollback playbook, Sophie & Priya to validate kernel paths and batching configs, Lina will land the sampler PR, Marta to stabilize QA tests; Anya will update canary gating doc. We'll meet Thursday for the dry-run plan, and schedule the actual dry-run on staging next Thursday.
12:10 Jordan: any final questions?
12:18 Khaled: just confirm comms — Jordan will prepare a short note to stakeholders after we have draft playbook?
12:25 Jordan: yes i'll send it by EOD Friday. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript.
