source.title: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
topics: Publication checklist for known-issues articles
Standard 'When to escalate' language and thresholds
Redacting customer-specific details and internal-only links
Ownership + update responsibilities (Support vs SRE vs Platform vs Docs)
Cross-linking: known-issues index, Support macros, SDK troubleshooting
Status page vs docs banner vs direct customer comms
source.summary: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.
next_steps: Docs to apply final edits and merge PR-2841/PR-2842 once SRE/Platform approvals are recorded
Support Ops to update macros to link the known-issues index + specific articles for top ticket themes
SRE to sanity-check escalation thresholds in streaming + audit log articles against paging policy/SLO alerts
Platform to confirm final wording for quota/rate-limit header behavior under fallback and point to canonical metrics
Add/confirm 'Last updated' + 'Owner' fields on each article and set 30-day review reminders
action_items: Elliot Price: add standardized 'When to escalate' block to each article and push final doc edits by 2026-02-14 EOD PT
Sean Gallagher: review streaming + audit log workaround escalation thresholds and confirm they match paging guidance by 2026-02-15
Mina Haddad: provide Support-safe phrasing for model pinning + fallback behavior and which dashboard signal to reference by 2026-02-15
Dev Patel: update Support macros/snippets to link known-issues index + streaming/429 workarounds by 2026-02-16
Kira Thompson: remove remaining internal-only links (Grafana direct URLs) or move them behind 'Internal' callouts by 2026-02-14
Priya Shah: add 'Symptoms / Impact / Affected tiers / Workaround / Escalate when' headings to Dedicated autoscaling article for consistency by 2026-02-14
source.transcript:
Meeting: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
Date: 2026-02-14
Time: 08:05 AM PT (recorded)
Duration: 49 min
Attendees (Redwood): Elliot Price, Dev Patel, Priya Shah, Owen Phillips, Wei Chen, Kira Thompson, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Mina Haddad, Allison Grant

[00:00] Elliot Price: Alright, cool, looks like folks are joining. Um, goal today is quick publish review. We have five workaround drafts that are basically ready, but we need to make sure the “when to escalate” language is consistent, no customer-specific details, and ownership is clear. We’re trying to merge the docs PR today.

[00:22] Dev Patel: Yep. And for Support, the big thing is copy-paste readiness. Like, I don’t want a paragraph that says “it depends” with no trigger.

[00:30] Kira Thompson: Plus a few of the drafts still have raw dashboard links that are internal only. We need to either remove or label them internal.

[00:37] Sean Gallagher: And I just want to avoid us committing to paging thresholds that don’t match what on-call is actually doing.

[00:45] Elliot Price: Yep. So we’ll go one by one. I’ll share screen—starting with streaming timeouts and reconnect.

[01:02] Wei Chen: This is the one that’s like stream reset, truncated stream, disconnect mid-token.

[01:08] Elliot Price: Right. The headings are: Symptoms, Likely causes, Workaround, and When to escalate. Current “when to escalate” says: if more than 2% of streaming requests are disconnecting for 10 minutes, escalate to SRE.

[01:23] Rafael Mendes: Two percent might be low depending on customer volume. Also, Support won’t know % unless they have a dashboard.

[01:30] Allison Grant: Yeah, Support generally has a “quick view” dashboard but not everybody has the same access. We can phrase it as “if multiple customers report within 15 minutes” plus “if you can confirm elevated streaming disconnect metric”.

[01:43] Dev Patel: For P0/P1 mapping, I’d rather have: “P0 if widespread or enterprise impacted; P1 if single enterprise; P2 if single self-serve.” But the article should say what to do, not define priority again.

[01:55] Owen Phillips: We should anchor it to customer impact. Like: “Escalate immediately if enterprise or Dedicated customer experiencing repeated disconnects affecting production traffic; include request IDs and region.”

[02:07] Elliot Price: OK, so rewrite that block to be: “Escalate (page SRE) if: 1) more than one customer in same region within 30 minutes, or 2) enterprise/dedicated customer reports production outage, or 3) you confirm streaming disconnect metric elevated above baseline.”

[02:24] Sean Gallagher: And we should reference the incident process. If it’s multi-customer, we probably want to move it to #incidents and not keep it only in #support.

[02:32] Dev Patel: Yes, and the article can say “start a bridge if X” but don’t say “DM Sean” or something.

[02:38] Wei Chen: Also, the workaround section includes “disable HTTP/2”. That’s fine, but it needs to be framed as temporary and customer-controlled.

[02:47] Elliot Price: It currently says: “Temporary mitigation: disable HTTP/2; adjust keepalive; set client timeout >= 120s; implement retry with jitter.” Do we need to say “do not infinite retry”?

[02:58] Rafael Mendes: Please yes. Otherwise we get thundering herd.

[03:01] Allison Grant: Add idempotency note too. Like: only retry if your operation is safe, or use request idempotency key.

[03:07] Elliot Price: OK, I’ll add a bullet: “Retries: exponential backoff with jitter; cap attempts; avoid immediate retry loops; ensure idempotency.”

[03:15] Priya Shah: Can we also put a one-liner: “Include request_id in every support escalation”? That’s been a recurring issue.

[03:22] Dev Patel: Yes, and link to the TS helper PR and the SDK troubleshooting section.

[03:27] Elliot Price: Great. That’s streaming.

[03:33] Elliot Price: Next: 429 quota mismatch and rate limit headers.

[03:39] Mina Haddad: This one is sensitive because some header behavior changes under fallback and routing. We need to be careful with definitive language.

[03:47] Wei Chen: The draft says “Headers are always consistent across endpoints” which… is not true today.

[03:53] Mina Haddad: Exactly. We should say “Headers are intended to be consistent; if you observe inconsistencies, collect samples and escalate.” And point to the fix in flight.

[04:03] Dev Patel: For Support flow, we need a checklist: confirm account limits, confirm org/project, confirm which API key, confirm region, then compare telemetry.

[04:12] Owen Phillips: The biggest customer confusion is: they see 429 and assume monthly quota is exhausted, but it’s a per-minute rate limit or concurrency. And SDK upgrades sometimes change default concurrency.

[04:23] Elliot Price: So “Symptoms: sudden increase 429 after SDK upgrade.” “Likely causes: rate-limit enforcement, concurrency, burst behavior, retries.” “Workaround: reduce concurrency, respect Retry-After, parse headers.”

[04:36] Sean Gallagher: “When to escalate” should include: if 429 persists after lowering concurrency and verifying key, and especially if headers contradict the Console usage.

[04:44] Mina Haddad: And ask them to provide raw response headers from a single request. Redact auth. Like copy/paste.

[04:51] Kira Thompson: The draft currently includes a screenshot that has a real customer org slug.

[04:56] Elliot Price: We’ll remove the screenshot or replace with sanitized example.

[05:00] Dev Patel: Replace with text block. Screenshots are harder to keep clean.

[05:05] Elliot Price: OK. And we’ll add: “Escalate to Platform (Control Plane) if you suspect quota misclassification; link ENG-4825.”

[05:13] Mina Haddad: Yep. Ownership: Platform + Routing.

[05:17] Elliot Price: Cool.

[05:20] Elliot Price: Third: Dedicated autoscaling oscillation causing latency.

[05:27] Priya Shah: I wrote that one with Kira. It’s basically: identify oscillation pattern, then adjust min replicas, increase scale-down cooldown, reduce aggressive scale-in.

[05:37] Allison Grant: The dashboards—can we reference them generically? Like “Dedicated pool replicas over time” instead of direct Grafana link.

[05:44] Kira Thompson: I can put “Internal: dashboard link” in a callout and keep customer doc clean.

[05:49] Dev Patel: For customer-facing, we should avoid giving them internal knob names if it’s something only Redwood sets.

[05:56] Priya Shah: Some of it is customer-configurable if they have Dedicated console access. But not all. I can split: “Customer-configurable” vs “Redwood-managed.”

[06:06] Sean Gallagher: “When to escalate” for Dedicated should be faster because it’s a reserved capacity customer. Like if P95 latency is above their agreed SLO for 10 minutes, page SRE + Dedicated on-call.

[06:18] Rafael Mendes: Just don’t put exact SLO numbers in the public doc. Use “customer’s agreed SLO” or “contracted latency target.”

[06:25] Dev Patel: Also escalation should include: pool name, region, current min/max replicas, recent changes.

[06:33] Elliot Price: Great, we’ll add that as required context.

[06:37] Elliot Price: Fourth: Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations.

[06:44] Kira Thompson: This one is pretty technical. It includes safe rollback steps, and “collect diagnostics bundle.” We should ensure we’re not telling customers to do anything dangerous.

[06:53] Sean Gallagher: And we should be explicit: if you’re not comfortable, stop and escalate. Also mention maintenance window.

[07:01] Dev Patel: For Support, we need “what to ask for” and “what not to ask for.” Like no raw database dumps.

[07:07] Kira Thompson: The draft says: “Send installer logs and cluster events.” That’s fine. We can add: “Do not include secrets; follow redaction guidance.”

[07:15] Naomi—(not on list) Speaker 2: Uh, sorry, quick note—security wise, we should say “use secure upload link” not email attachments.

[07:22] Elliot Price: Good catch. We can add “upload via Support portal” language.

[07:27] Mina Haddad: Ownership here is Private Deployments/Platform. Also “when to escalate” is basically immediate if production upgrade is blocked.

[07:36] Rafael Mendes: For on-call, if it’s blocking a compliance customer during change window, treat it like P1. But again, keep it generic.

[07:44] Elliot Price: OK.

[07:46] Elliot Price: Fifth: audit log delivery latency.

[07:52] Wei Chen: This one has the expected SLO, like “typically under 5 minutes.” But we got feedback that enterprise expectation is tighter, or at least contract-specific.

[08:02] Sean Gallagher: Right now we’re still standardizing. I’d say: “Expected: near real-time; occasional delay during backfill.” And specify: “Escalate if lag > 30 minutes for enterprise or if sustained increasing lag.”

[08:16] Allison Grant: And also “if you can confirm pipeline backlog metric increasing.” But again Support may not.

[08:22] Dev Patel: Support needs a crisp line: “If customer reports missing audit events older than 30 minutes, open escalation.” Because they’ll ask, “is 12 minutes normal.”

[08:32] Rafael Mendes: 12 minutes can happen. So we can say: “Under 15 minutes: monitor; 15-30: investigate; over 30: escalate.”

[08:42] Sean Gallagher: I’m okay with that, as long as it’s “guidance” and not a contract guarantee.

[08:48] Elliot Price: OK, we’ll phrase: “Guideline thresholds for triage.”

[08:52] Elliot Price: Now, overarching thing: consistent “When to escalate” block.

[08:58] Dev Patel: Yes, please. Same shape everywhere: 3-5 bullets, include required context, and what channel to use.

[09:06] Owen Phillips: Also “If you’re waiting on customer, set ticket to Waiting on Customer and pause SLA timer”—but that’s Support process, not KB.

[09:14] Elliot Price: Right, KB is for Support/CS internal but still we want it safe. We can link to the triage checklist doc for the SLA timer stuff.

[09:24] Kira Thompson: The request from Docs was also: avoid too many cross-links that are dead ends. We should link only to the known-issues index, escalation playbook, and maybe the SDK troubleshooting page.

[09:35] Wei Chen: On the docs site, the landing page will be “Known issues & workarounds” with tags. PR-2841 adds that.

[09:42] Elliot Price: Exactly. PR-2842 adds the navigation links from Support docs to the playbook and SLA policy.

[09:48] Dev Patel: The support macros refresh is separate but I’ll update the top macros: streaming disconnect and 429 confusion to include the KB links.

[09:58] Sean Gallagher: Another point: in “When to escalate” we should explicitly say when to page SRE vs when to route to Platform. Like audit logs and SSO are Platform; widespread latency is SRE; dedicated pool oscillation is SRE + capacity.

[10:13] Mina Haddad: Routing / fallback confusion, like “pinned model version ignored,” that’s platform routing team, not SRE.

[10:20] Elliot Price: We don’t have an article for that one yet, but we can add an index entry that points to “under investigation” internally.

[10:27] Dev Patel: Careful: we don’t want to publish “under investigation” articles. The criteria doc says known issue requires a validated workaround.

[10:34] Elliot Price: True. We’ll keep that internal-only for now.

[10:37] Elliot Price: OK, let’s settle on standard wording. Proposed template:
- Escalate immediately if: (a) multiple customers impacted in same region/product, (b) enterprise/dedicated/private production outage, (c) security/compliance impact, (d) suspected platform-wide regression.
- Include in escalation: request IDs, region, model, time window, SDK + version, error message sample, steps tried.
- Start Slack bridge if multi-customer or time-sensitive, otherwise keep in ticket until trigger.

[11:09] Dev Patel: That works. Add “customer tier” too.

[11:13] Rafael Mendes: And “recent deploys” if known. Not always but helpful.

[11:18] Allison Grant: For dashboards, maybe call out the “Support quick view” dashboard name, but not a direct link.

[11:24] Kira Thompson: Or “ask SRE for the quick view if you don’t have access.”

[11:29] Sean Gallagher: That’s fine.

[11:31] Elliot Price: Alright. Now, ownership. I want each article to have an owner and a reviewer, plus “last updated.”

[11:40] Wei Chen: For streaming and 429, I can own the doc updates, but we need SRE/Platform as reviewers.

[11:47] Kira Thompson: I can own Dedicated autoscaling and Private installer articles. Reviewers: SRE for Dedicated, Platform for Private.

[11:56] Sean Gallagher: I’ll sign off on streaming and audit logs after edits.

[12:02] Mina Haddad: I’ll sign off on 429 wording especially around fallback and headers.

[12:07] Dev Patel: And Support will own the macros that link to them. We’re not the technical owner of the issue, but we’ll own the Support-facing guidance.

[12:16] Elliot Price: Great. Also, update policy: if a workaround becomes obsolete, we either mark it “Resolved” and keep short history, or archive.

[12:26] Owen Phillips: Can we add a “Temporary workaround” label? Customers get mad when they think it’s permanent.

[12:31] Wei Chen: Yeah, a callout box: “Temporary mitigation; may change after runtime patch.”

[12:37] Elliot Price: OK.

[12:40] Elliot Price: Quick question: status page vs docs banner. The comms alignment doc says: status page for material incident; docs banner for known issue that isn’t an incident but has high volume. We should align.

[12:54] Sean Gallagher: Yes, but banners need approval. Don’t have Support unilaterally post banners.

[12:59] Dev Patel: We’ll route through #incidents-comms or whatever the process is. But in the KB we can say: “If widespread, consider status page event.”

[13:08] Elliot Price: OK, but that’s a little too “processy.” Maybe leave it out of the KB and keep it in the escalation playbook.

[13:16] Kira Thompson: Agree.

[13:18] Elliot Price: Now, last pass for sensitivity. Any customer names in these drafts?

[13:24] Kira Thompson: I saw “Helix” in one place as an example of Private upgrade. I’ll remove.

[13:28] Wei Chen: “Aurora” appears in 429 doc as “customer reported.” I’ll replace with “example customer.”

[13:35] Dev Patel: Also remove any exact timestamps tied to tickets. Use relative time.

[13:41] Elliot Price: Great.

[13:44] Elliot Price: Let’s talk publish mechanics. PR-2841 is the hub + tagging. PR-2842 links from Support docs to SLA and playbook. PR-2843 is SDK troubleshooting for streaming. We want them to land together.

[13:59] Wei Chen: PR-2843 is in review from SDK folks. Might not merge today.

[14:04] Dev Patel: Not blocking the known-issues hub. But we can add a placeholder link to “Streaming troubleshooting (coming soon)”?

[14:11] Elliot Price: I’d rather not publish dead links. We can link to the KB for now and update later.

[14:16] Wei Chen: OK.

[14:17] Elliot Price: Alright, I’m going to scroll through each “When to escalate” section and we’ll do quick edits.

[14:25] (screen share; crosstalk)

[14:27] Elliot Price: Streaming article—current escalations: “If customer experiences more than 3 disconnects in 5 minutes.” That’s too specific.

[14:35] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, make it “repeated disconnects impacting production” plus “multiple customers.”

[14:41] Rafael Mendes: And add: “If error is ‘stream reset’ or ‘upstream closed’ and persists after retry/backoff.”

[14:48] Elliot Price: Ok.

[14:50] Elliot Price: Also add required fields: request_id, time window, region, model, SDK version, connection type (HTTP/2 vs 1.1), proxy.

[15:02] Dev Patel: Proxy/CDN is huge. Half the time it’s some corporate thing.

[15:06] Wei Chen: Add “Are you behind a proxy/load balancer?” question.

[15:11] Elliot Price: Done.

[15:14] Elliot Price: 429 article—current says “If headers inconsistent, open Jira.” We want: “Escalate to Platform and attach header samples.”

[15:24] Mina Haddad: And add “If customer is getting 429s with low traffic and no retries, that’s suspicious.”

[15:31] Dev Patel: Also emphasize: do not tell them to rotate keys as a first step. That’s a pain.

[15:36] Owen Phillips: Yep.

[15:38] Elliot Price: Dedicated autoscaling—“when to escalate” currently says “if latency high.” Too vague.

[15:45] Priya Shah: I’ll revise: “Escalate if sustained oscillation correlates with latency spikes for >10 minutes” and “if changes to min replicas don’t stabilize.”

[15:56] Sean Gallagher: Add: “If customer is missing contracted capacity or burst not activating, involve capacity/SRE.”

[16:04] Elliot Price: OK.

[16:06] Elliot Price: Private installer—“when to escalate” should include: migration stuck > 15 minutes, repeated crashloop, or rollback required.

[16:16] Kira Thompson: Yes, and we should add “collect installer logs, kubectl events, version of installer and target version.”

[16:25] Dev Patel: And “do not run random SQL.”

[16:27] (laughter)

[16:29] Elliot Price: Audit logs—triage thresholds: under 15 min monitor, 15-30 investigate, >30 escalate. Good?

[16:37] Sean Gallagher: Yes, with caveat “guideline.”

[16:40] Allison Grant: And include: “ask if they’re filtering by timestamp incorrectly.” Sometimes it’s timezone or cursor.

[16:47] Wei Chen: Good point. Add a “common confusion” note.

[16:52] Elliot Price: OK.

[16:54] Elliot Price: Now, question: Where do these live? We’re publishing under Support/CS docs in the redwood-docs help center or internal Confluence?

[17:04] Kira Thompson: These are going to the docs site (internal help center) but are visible to Support and some CS. Not public to customers, right?

[17:12] Dev Patel: Correct: internal. But still treat as customer-safe because it’s often shared.

[17:18] Elliot Price: Right, “customer-safe by default” and if internal-only, label clearly.

[17:25] Sean Gallagher: And no internal on-call phone numbers. (laughs)

[17:28] Elliot Price: Exactly.

[17:31] Elliot Price: Okay, last section: cadence for updates. I propose we set monthly review reminders. If issue is active, weekly.

[17:41] Dev Patel: For Support, if we see ticket volume spike, we’ll ping the owner to update.

[17:47] Mina Haddad: For Platform issues like 429 headers, it’ll change with ENG-4825 rollout. So update when release hits.

[17:55] Wei Chen: We can add: “Update triggers: fix shipped, mitigation changes, new affected regions/models.”

[18:02] Elliot Price: Good. We’ll include a short “Change log” line: “Updated 2026-02-14: clarified escalation thresholds.”

[18:12] Kira Thompson: Keep it minimal. Not a full changelog.

[18:16] Elliot Price: Cool.

[18:18] Elliot Price: Anything else blocking merge?

[18:22] Sean Gallagher: I just want to review the final “when to escalate” text for streaming and audit logs. Send me the diff.

[18:28] Elliot Price: Will do.

[18:30] Dev Patel: I’ll start on macro updates once the URLs are stable.

[18:34] Wei Chen: Please don’t change slugs after I paste them everywhere.

[18:37] Dev Patel: (laughs) Noted.

[18:39] Elliot Price: OK, recap:
- Elliot will apply template and redact examples.
- Kira removes internal dashboard links or labels.
- Sean and Mina provide sign-off.
- Dev updates macros.

[18:55] Owen Phillips: One more: the known-issues index needs “When to escalate” callouts per item, like a one-liner, not just the article. Otherwise people don’t click.

[19:05] Elliot Price: Great point. The index will have a short “Escalate when: enterprise impact / multi-region / data loss.” We’ll keep it consistent.

[19:14] Allison Grant: And tags: region, tier, product, last updated.

[19:19] Elliot Price: Yup.

[19:21] (short pause)

[19:23] Elliot Price: Okay, I think we’re good. I’ll push edits in the next couple hours and then ping for approvals.

[19:30] Dev Patel: Thanks all.

[19:31] Sean Gallagher: Thanks.

[19:32] Kira Thompson: Thanks.

[19:33] Meeting ended (recording stopped)

Note: Fireflies transcription may omit portions of screen-share review and some cross-talk; timestamps reflect approximate positions in recording.

title: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
preview: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.
text: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.

source.title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
source.summary: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language for enterprise vs non-enterprise (and how to handle “Waiting on Customer” without gaming the SLA). The group agreed to standardize a short “page template” (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys, customer tier, and what changed), require linking the SUP Jira ticket in the bridge, and to use a consistent cadence phrased as targets rather than guarantees. Follow-ups include edits to the Confluence SLA table wording, Jira automation for required fields + SLA timers, and incident-bot improvements to pin SUP ticket links in bridge channels.
topics: P0/P1 vs Sev mapping and definitions
Escalation triggers: error rate, latency SLO breach, number of impacted customers, enterprise contract risk
Slack bridge naming + required context + participants
When to move a bridge into #incidents
Update cadence language (30 min vs hourly) and 'Waiting on Customer' handling
Jira hygiene: required fields, SLA timers, linking Slack threads
Status page vs customer email vs docs banner alignment (high-level)
next_steps: Dev to update Confluence severity matrix page with clarified P0/P1 wording and examples (streaming disconnects, auth outage, region degradation).
Sean to send SRE-approved paging thresholds wording (what’s a page vs what’s a heads-up) for inclusion in the escalation playbook.
Rafael + Dylan to finalize the bridge template message (Slack workflow) including required fields and a “link SUP ticket” prompt (INT-332/INT-333 alignment).
Priya to update Support macros to include the standard request-id / region / model / SDK version ask and a 'what changed recently' line.
Allison to provide canonical dashboard links for Support-safe quick checks (latency, error rate, streaming health) to be referenced in the playbook.
source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
Date: 2026-02-12
Start time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 46 min
Meeting link: (internal) meet.google.com/xxx-xxxx-xxx

Attendees:
- Dev Patel (Support Ops)
- Owen Phillips (Support Incidents)
- Priya Shah (Support Workflow/KB)
- Sean Gallagher (SRE)
- Rafael Mendes (SRE On-call / Escalations)
- Allison Grant (Observability)
- Dylan Brooks (Internal Tooling / Jira Automation)

Auto-summary (generated):
- Agreed on consistent definitions for Support P0/P1 and how they map to incident Sev levels.
- Confirmed paging/escalation triggers should be tied to customer impact and SLO-style thresholds, not just “a loud customer”.
- Standardized Slack bridge conventions: naming, required context, who gets invited, and when to transition to #incidents.
- Clarified update cadence language: enterprise targets vs general targets; how “Waiting on Customer” interacts with timers.

Action items (captured):
- Dev: revise the Confluence severity matrix + triggers examples by EOW.
- Rafael/Dylan: propose Slack bridge template message and incident-bot prompt text by Monday.
- Allison: share Support-safe dashboard shortlist + permalinks.


Transcript

[00:00] Dev Patel: Alright, thanks everyone. This is basically the last alignment pass before we publish the refreshed escalation playbook. The pieces that keep coming up in threads are: what exactly is P0 versus P1 for Support, what triggers a page to SRE, and then the Slack bridge conventions and update cadence wording. I want to leave with, like, exact language we can paste.

[00:24] Sean Gallagher: Yep. And from SRE side, the main thing is we don’t want “page SRE” to mean “skip incident process” or “skip the on-call primary.” Like, it needs to route cleanly.

[00:35] Owen Phillips: Totally. Also we’ve had situations where Support opens a bridge, and then Engineering says “move to #incidents” but it’s not clear when that should happen. So we need a crisp rule.

[00:46] Rafael Mendes: And a template. Please. The number one thing that makes pages slow is we spend five minutes just extracting the basics. Request IDs, region, model, what endpoint, did anything deploy. That’s the stuff.

[00:58] Priya Shah: Plus tier. Like, Hosted free user versus Dedicated enterprise is a different play.

[01:05] Dev Patel: Yep. Let’s start with definitions. The Confluence draft currently says P0 is “service down or severe degradation for an enterprise customer” and P1 is “major impact but workaround exists.” The thread feedback was that’s too subjective.

[01:22] Sean Gallagher: Right. From SRE, we’d rather anchor to customer impact + scope. A single enterprise customer can be P0, but only if it’s actually blocking production and not like, one endpoint misused.

[01:33] Owen Phillips: So for Support: P0 should be “customer cannot use the product” or “data/security issue” with no immediate workaround. And it’s either multi-customer, or an enterprise contract / revenue risk.

[01:45] Rafael Mendes: And map that to incident Sev. Like, Sev0 is broad outage, Sev1 is major degradation, Sev2 is limited scope. We can do P0 -> Sev0/Sev1 depending on blast radius.

[01:57] Dev Patel: Okay. Let’s make it concrete. Streaming disconnects in a single region. Where does that land?

[02:05] Sean Gallagher: Depends on percent impact. If it’s, say, >5% of streaming requests failing in us-east for more than 10 minutes, that’s incident territory. If it’s one customer behind a proxy timing out, that’s not a Sev1.

[02:20] Priya Shah: But Support doesn’t always have the global percent. We see ticket volume or one loud escalation.

[02:26] Allison Grant: We can give Support a quick dashboard link to look at streaming reset rate by region. Even if it’s not perfect, it’s better than guessing.

[02:35] Dylan Brooks: If we add a required “impact estimate” field in Jira, we can prompt for “known customers affected” and “approx error rate from dashboards if available.”

[02:44] Dev Patel: Great. So triggers for paging SRE: we want something like: page when error-rate threshold is met OR enterprise P0 OR suspected platform-wide degradation.

[02:56] Rafael Mendes: Yeah. I’d phrase it as “page SRE when you believe it meets incident criteria OR when you need SRE action to mitigate within SLA.” Like, if it requires traffic shift, rollback, capacity intervention.

[03:10] Owen Phillips: Can we list examples? Like auth outage: definitely page. Region-level elevated 5xx for chat completions: page. Rate-limit header confusion: not page.

[03:22] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Also: security/compliance events. If customer says “data leak” or audit log tampering, page security on-call, not just SRE, but Support needs the path.

[03:33] Dev Patel: For this meeting, we’ll keep it SRE + Support, but we’ll link the security escalation section.

[03:38] Priya Shah: On update cadence, we’re still stuck on 30 minutes vs hourly for enterprise. The Sales team is like “promise 30,” and SRE is like “don’t promise what you can’t deliver.”

[03:52] Sean Gallagher: Yeah. Please don’t write it as a promise. Write it as “target cadence” and “if there’s no new info, provide a holding update.” That’s the key.

[04:03] Dev Patel: So for enterprise P0: first response within 15 minutes during coverage, and then updates every 30 minutes while actively investigating/mitigating.

[04:13] Rafael Mendes: That’s okay if we define what “update” means. It can be “still investigating, next checkpoint at X, current mitigation attempt is Y.” But not radio silence.

[04:25] Owen Phillips: And for P1: hourly is fine.

[04:28] Sean Gallagher: Agree.

[04:31] Dev Patel: What about “Waiting on Customer”? Support asked if SLA timers pause.

[04:38] Dylan Brooks: In Jira we can implement a status-based SLA pause. But it needs a rule, otherwise people will abuse it.

[04:45] Sean Gallagher: Please don’t make it a loophole. If it’s genuinely blocked—like we requested request IDs or logs—then it’s okay to pause the internal timer, but Support should still do a daily check-in or at least one reminder.

[05:02] Priya Shah: And send a clear ask: “we need request IDs from the last failed call in eu-west” not “send logs.”

[05:10] Rafael Mendes: Yes. Also, when it’s Waiting on Customer but the issue is likely systemic, don’t pause escalation. We should still monitor.

[05:20] Dev Patel: So the policy text could be: “When status is Waiting on Customer, first-response SLA is met but update cadence becomes ‘next response within one business day’ unless the issue is suspected platform-impacting.” Something like that.

[05:34] Owen Phillips: We should be careful. Enterprise customers interpret that as “we stop working.”

[05:39] Sean Gallagher: Phrase it as “we’ll continue to investigate, but progress may be limited until we receive X.”

[05:46] Dev Patel: Okay, noted.

[05:49] Dev Patel: Next topic: Slack bridge conventions. Right now the draft says create a bridge channel in the Support workspace, name it something like #sup-bridge-sup-1842-streaming-us-east, invite on-call, and link the Jira ticket.

[06:06] Rafael Mendes: The main thing is: please invite the on-call handle, not random individuals. Like @sre-oncall or whatever the PagerDuty user maps to.

[06:15] Owen Phillips: The concern is sometimes we don’t know which on-call to invite—SRE vs Platform vs Runtime.

[06:21] Sean Gallagher: That’s where the escalation directory comes in. But also: if it looks like infra symptoms (latency, 5xx, regional), start with SRE. If it’s control plane (SSO, org access), page Platform.

[06:34] Priya Shah: Can we make the bridge template include “suspected area” with a dropdown-ish prompt? Like “SRE/Platform/Runtime/Unknown.”

[06:42] Dylan Brooks: In Slack workflow yes. We can have a form. Then it posts the template.

[06:48] Dev Patel: Also, when to move to #incidents? There was a debate in #eng-oncall.

[06:55] Rafael Mendes: Here’s the rule I’d use: if it’s multi-customer OR meets Sev1+ thresholds OR requires cross-functional coordination beyond one team, move to #incidents. Otherwise keep in Support bridge.

[07:10] Sean Gallagher: Yeah. And “move” meaning: open an incident channel in the incidents workspace, incident bot starts, status page decision happens there. Support bridge can remain for customer-specific comms, but the source of truth becomes the incident channel.

[07:26] Owen Phillips: That’s helpful. So we can say: Support starts the bridge. SRE decides if it’s incident-worthy within, say, 10 minutes once they see metrics.

[07:36] Rafael Mendes: Ideally yes. But Support can also make that call if the dashboard is screaming.

[07:42] Allison Grant: I can add a quick checklist: “If error rate >X% for >Y minutes in a region” or “p95 latency >2x baseline” then treat as incident.

[07:53] Dev Patel: Let’s talk numbers. What do we put? We don’t want to lock ourselves to exact SLOs in a support doc.

[08:01] Sean Gallagher: Put relative thresholds and link to SLO doc for exact values. Like “sustained elevated 5xx above normal baseline” and “impacting multiple customers.”

[08:11] Rafael Mendes: But give at least one concrete example. Otherwise people interpret everything as baseline.

[08:17] Owen Phillips: Example: “>2% 5xx for chat completions for 10 minutes” would be a page.

[08:24] Sean Gallagher: That’s reasonable as a starting point. Add “or any availability alert firing.”

[08:30] Dev Patel: Okay.

[08:32] Dev Patel: Bridge template. Rafael, what are the required fields you want?

[08:37] Rafael Mendes: Minimum:
- SUP ticket link
- Customer name + tier
- Region
- Model + endpoint (chat/embeddings/rerank)
- Time window + first observed
- Request IDs (at least 2-3)
- What changed recently (SDK upgrade, config change, new deploy)
- Current workaround attempted (retry/backoff, switch region, disable HTTP/2, etc.)

[09:05] Priya Shah: We can also ask for SDK version and whether streaming is used.

[09:10] Rafael Mendes: Yes. And whether it’s server-side errors or client timeouts.

[09:14] Dev Patel: Great. Dylan, can we auto-post that checklist in every bridge channel?

[09:19] Dylan Brooks: Yes. INT-332 is exactly that. Slack workflow creates channel, posts template, pins SUP ticket link. We can also nag if the Jira issue isn’t linked.

[09:31] Owen Phillips: Incident bot pinning the SUP ticket—where are we on that?

[09:35] Dylan Brooks: There’s a PR in incident-bot to prompt for “related Jira tickets,” and we can special-case SUP- keys to pin. It’s basically done, needs review.

[09:45] Sean Gallagher: That’s good. The biggest pain is after the bridge, we lose the customer thread and then postmortem misses context.

[09:54] Dev Patel: Update cadence language: I want to settle the exact phrasing. Proposed: “For P0 Enterprise: updates every 30 minutes during active incident response; if no material change, send a checkpoint update.” For P1 Enterprise: hourly. For non-enterprise: “as available” feels too weak.

[10:13] Priya Shah: Maybe “every 2 business hours” for non-enterprise P1?

[10:17] Sean Gallagher: I don’t have a strong opinion there, but don’t set a cadence Support can’t hit. Better to set expectations by tier.

[10:24] Owen Phillips: For self-serve Hosted, we can say “best effort, usually within 1 business day” for non-urgent.

[10:31] Dev Patel: Right. But this meeting is mostly about escalations, so we can keep it to P0/P1.

[10:37] Rafael Mendes: Also important: internal update cadence versus customer update cadence. We can update internally every 15 minutes but customers every 30. Or vice versa.

[10:47] Dev Patel: Good call. The playbook should say: “Support owns customer comms; engineering owns internal technical updates; align before sending customer-facing RCAs.”

[10:57] Sean Gallagher: And no promising ETAs unless SRE confirms.

[11:01] Priya Shah: That’s in the comms template already.

[11:05] Allison Grant: One more thing: dashboards. If Support has access to a “quick view,” they can answer questions fast: is it us, is it regional, is it just one model. I’ll send a shortlist.

[11:19] Dev Patel: Great.

[11:21] (crosstalk)

[11:22] Owen Phillips: I want to make sure we cover Dedicated and Private. When do we page SRE versus Platform versus Private Deployments?

[11:30] Sean Gallagher: Dedicated performance / capacity is typically SRE + Capacity. Private installer issues are Platform/Private Deployments, but SRE can help if it’s infra on our side. For customer-managed clusters, we’re advisory.

[11:44] Rafael Mendes: Put it as: “If it’s Redwood-hosted infrastructure (Hosted/Dedicated control plane), page SRE. If it’s customer environment (Private), follow Private escalation path and only page SRE if there’s suspicion of Redwood services involved.”

[11:57] Dev Patel: Okay. And for Dedicated autoscaling oscillation: SRE or Platform?

[12:02] Sean Gallagher: It’s usually SRE + Platform runtime if it’s the autoscaler logic. But initial triage through SRE is fine.

[12:10] Priya Shah: Good. We’ll add that as an example in the severity doc.

[12:15] Dev Patel: Quick check: when Support creates a bridge, who is required participants? Draft says: Support lead, on-call engineer, optionally CSM and AE if enterprise.

[12:28] Rafael Mendes: Please don’t add AE unless there’s customer comms happening live. Too many people is noise.

[12:33] Owen Phillips: But CS sometimes wants to join to listen.

[12:36] Sean Gallagher: Let Support/CS join. Just keep engineering channel focused. The incident channel is where we can have a separate “customer-comms” thread if needed.

[12:47] Dev Patel: So we’ll write: “Invite only necessary roles; keep channel small; add AE/CSM as needed for customer updates.”

[12:55] Priya Shah: And required to pin: SUP ticket link, customer comms draft, dashboard links.

[13:02] Dylan Brooks: Pins can be automated: template post + Jira link + dashboard link list.

[13:08] Dev Patel: Nice.

[13:10] Dev Patel: Let’s circle back to P0/P1 wording. Sean, what wording do you approve? I need something Support can use in Jira.

[13:18] Sean Gallagher: Something like:
P0 (Support): “Production impact requiring immediate engineering response; no reasonable workaround; impacts multiple customers OR a single enterprise customer with contractual/SLA risk OR security/data integrity concern.”
P1: “Significant production impact with partial workaround or limited scope; engineering response needed but not necessarily incident process; may be single-customer.”

[13:44] Owen Phillips: And explicitly say: “P0 always triggers bridge creation; paging depends on trigger list.”

[13:51] Rafael Mendes: I’d say P0 should trigger a page if during on-call coverage. If it’s out-of-hours and not incident-level, maybe it’s a heads-up.

[14:02] Dev Patel: We can add “After-hours policy”: for enterprise P0, we page. For others, we create a bridge and escalate next business day unless it appears to be broad.

[14:13] Sean Gallagher: That aligns with reality.

[14:16] Priya Shah: Also should mention “don’t page multiple teams at once.” Choose one primary and ask them to pull in others.

[14:24] Rafael Mendes: Yes. One page, one owner.

[14:28] Dev Patel: Great.

[14:30] Dev Patel: Any gotchas on language around SLOs? We’re mapping Support SLAs to engineering SLOs, but we don’t want to publish internal numbers.

[14:39] Sean Gallagher: Keep internal thresholds internal, link to the mapping doc inside Confluence. In the support-facing playbook we can reference “engineering availability and latency SLOs” but not give the exact budget.

[14:52] Allison Grant: We can make the quick view show “is alert firing yes/no” without revealing the exact threshold.

[14:58] Dev Patel: That’s perfect.

[15:00] (pause)

[15:02] Owen Phillips: One more thing: sometimes Support gets asked “should we post status page?” We need a rule.

[15:09] Sean Gallagher: Status page decision is incident commander + comms owner. Support can recommend, but don’t unilaterally post.

[15:17] Dev Patel: We have a comms alignment doc. We’ll link it. For this doc, we just say “if multi-customer degradation suspected, move to incident process; status page evaluation occurs there.”

[15:29] Priya Shah: Good.

[15:31] Dev Patel: Alright, let’s do final recap and next steps.

[15:35] Dev Patel: 1) Definitions: P0/P1 anchored to production impact + scope + enterprise contract risk; map to Sev levels but keep that as reference.
2) Paging triggers: incident-level metrics, multi-customer impact, need SRE action (traffic shift, rollback), security/data integrity.
3) Slack bridge: standard naming, workflow posts template; required fields include request IDs, region, model, tier; invite on-call handle; keep channel small.
4) Transition to #incidents when multi-customer or Sev1+ suspected.
5) Update cadence: enterprise P0 target 30-minute customer updates during active mitigation; P1 hourly; phrase as targets; “Waiting on Customer” pauses internal timers with guardrails.

[16:18] Sean Gallagher: Sounds right.

[16:20] Rafael Mendes: Please, please make the request ID requirement bold. Like, if there’s no request IDs, we can’t do much.

[16:26] Priya Shah: Noted.

[16:28] Dylan Brooks: I’ll sync with Dev on the Slack workflow text and Jira required-field rules. INT-333 for the Jira automation: we can block moving to “Escalated” if region/model/request IDs are empty.

[16:42] Owen Phillips: That would help a lot.

[16:44] Allison Grant: I’ll send the dashboard permalinks today.

[16:48] Dev Patel: Great. I’ll update the Confluence pages and send for final sign-off. Thanks everyone.

[16:55] Sean Gallagher: Thanks.

[16:56] Rafael Mendes: Thanks.

[16:57] Call ended.

title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
preview: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language for enterprise vs non-enterprise (and how to handle “Waiting on Customer” without gaming the SLA). The group agreed to standardize a short “page template” (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys, customer tier, and what changed), require linking the SUP Jira ticket in the bridge, and to use a consistent cadence phrased as targets rather than guarantees. Follow-ups include edits to the Confluence SLA table wording, Jira automation for required fields + SLA timers, and incident-bot improvements to pin SUP ticket links in bridge channels.

source.title: Marketplace onboarding walkthrough (internal dry run) — subscription → org provisioning → first key
topics: AWS/GCP marketplace subscription confirmation and entitlement propagation
Console entrypoint for marketplace customers (org linking vs create new org)
Entitlement verification API behaviors and error states
First API key creation and first successful request path
Docs gaps: step-by-step, troubleshooting, and where security links live
Metering dimension/SKU mapping visibility and billing expectations
Telemetry: funnel events and what we can measure reliably
source.summary: Internal walkthrough of the end-to-end marketplace onboarding flow to validate the UX and docs before submission. Team identified confusion around org linking for existing Redwood orgs, unclear messaging during entitlement propagation delays (AWS especially), and missing docs for the "already subscribed" and "multiple subscriptions" edge cases. Engineering confirmed expected entitlement API error codes and noted where console should map them to actionable retry guidance. Docs team flagged needed screenshots and a clearer separation of AWS vs GCP steps, plus a single canonical "How marketplace billing maps to usage" section to reduce support tickets.
questions: Do we want to allow linking a marketplace subscription to an existing org without requiring org owner role, or keep strict owner/admin requirement?
Should the console show the purchased SKU name (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) or only show entitlements/limits (tokens/min, capacity) to avoid mismatch with marketplace labels?
What’s the maximum entitlement propagation delay we should message as ‘normal’ before we recommend opening a support ticket?
Do we need a dedicated troubleshooting page per marketplace (AWS vs GCP), or one shared page with provider-specific callouts?
action_items: Chloe Martin — Update console error copy for entitlement propagation delays: include expected wait time, refresh button, and ‘check again later’ guidance (due 2026-02-12)
Alex Martinez — Add a marketplace onboarding doc section: ‘Link subscription to existing org’ + required roles/permissions (due 2026-02-11)
Elliot Price — Add docs troubleshooting for common entitlement error codes (E_ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND, E_SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_ACTIVE, E_ORG_ALREADY_LINKED) with user-facing next steps (due 2026-02-13)
Cole Summers — Confirm final marketplace billing dimensions displayed in internal billing catalog match AWS/GCP submission fields; share mapping table link for Docs (due 2026-02-10)
Nadia Rahman — Verify telemetry events fire for retry/refresh clicks and for successful org link completion; propose dashboard for time-to-entitlement (due 2026-02-14)
Markus Klein — Provide ‘first call’ curl/SDK snippet specifically for marketplace users (includes where to find org slug + API key) to add to docs and example app (due 2026-02-12)
next_steps: Run the same dry-run in staging with a fresh AWS subscription and measure entitlement sync latency end-to-end
Update docs pages and console copy, then re-run internal dry-run focusing on edge cases (existing org + multi-subscription)
Finalize screenshots/diagram list for marketplace listing assets once the console entrypoint UI is stable
Send updated onboarding narrative to Partnerships for inclusion in AWS/GCP submission materials
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-02-07
Start time: 9:03 AM PT
Duration: 46 min
Title: Marketplace onboarding walkthrough (internal dry run) — subscription → org provisioning → first key
Attendees: Ben Carter, Chris Osei, Markus Klein, Chloe Martin, Ethan Park, Cole Summers, Nadia Rahman, Alex Martinez, Elliot Price, Kira Thompson

Auto-summary (Fireflies)
- Walked through AWS and GCP marketplace subscription flows and the Redwood console onboarding entrypoint.
- Identified gaps in error messaging for entitlement delays and missing docs for org linking edge cases.
- Confirmed expected entitlement verification API responses and discussed telemetry events.

[00:00] Ben Carter: Cool. Okay, we’re recording. This is our internal dry run for marketplace onboarding. Goal is: pretend we’re a buyer coming from AWS or GCP Marketplace, and we go subscription, entitlement, org provision, first key, first request. And we call out every “wait, what?” moment.

[00:19] Chris Osei: Yep, and we’ll capture action items. I’m taking notes. Let’s keep it kind of brutal.

[00:26] Chloe Martin: I can share my screen. I’m in staging console right now.

[00:31] Ben Carter: Great. Chloe, can you start with the “I just subscribed” path?

[00:37] Chloe Martin: Yep. So imagine the marketplace listing sends you to this URL, /onboarding/marketplace. We’ve got the provider selector at the top, AWS or GCP. Right now it defaults to AWS.

[00:49] Elliot Price: Quick flag, we don’t yet have the final vanity URLs. But yeah, conceptually.

[00:54] Chloe Martin: Right. So first step is “Connect your marketplace subscription.” There’s an input for “Account ID” and “Subscription ID.” In AWS it’s the customer identifier, and in GCP it’s the entitlement name.

[01:08] Markus Klein: As a customer, do I know what those are? That’s already, like, a thing.

[01:12] Alex Martinez: That’s my first docs gap. We need screenshots of where to find those in each marketplace portal.

[01:17] Ben Carter: Yeah, plus we need to handle “I don’t have it” and link to “contact procurement admin.”

[01:24] Chloe Martin: Exactly. If I paste in a dummy one, we call the entitlement verification endpoint. Ethan, do you want to confirm what we return when it’s not found?

[01:32] Ethan Park: Sure. In staging right now, the endpoint is returning E_ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND if we can’t match. But… in AWS, for the first like, couple minutes, it might not exist yet because the entitlement sync job hasn’t picked it up.

[01:47] Cole Summers: Or AWS hasn’t actually pushed it yet. That’s the “propagation delay” thing.

[01:51] Ben Carter: So the UX needs to say: “This can take X minutes,” not “you did it wrong.”

[01:57] Chloe Martin: Right now it says, literally, “Not found. Check values.” Which is… not great.

[02:04] Chris Osei: Action item. Chloe, update copy for entitlement delays, with refresh/retry and expected time window.

[02:10] Chloe Martin: Yep.

[02:12] Nadia Rahman: Also, when they hit refresh, can we log an event? Because we want to know how many people get stuck here.

[02:17] Chloe Martin: We added a click event, but I’m not sure if it’s wired on this screen yet.

[02:22] Nadia Rahman: I’ll verify.

[02:25] Ben Carter: Keep going. Let’s assume entitlement is found.

[02:28] Chloe Martin: Okay. When found, we show: “You’re entitled to Redwood Hosted — Marketplace.” And then we ask: “Do you want to create a new Redwood org or link to an existing org?”

[02:40] Markus Klein: That’s good. What happens if they already have an org, but they’re not the owner? Like an engineer set up an org last year.

[02:49] Chloe Martin: Right now we require org admin. If you choose “existing org,” you have to be signed in and have permissions.

[02:56] Alex Martinez: We need to document that, super explicitly. Otherwise support gets “I can’t link.”

[03:01] Elliot Price: And the doc should say “Ask your org owner to link the marketplace subscription.”

[03:05] Ben Carter: Do we want to allow non-admin to request linking? Like a “send request to admin” workflow?

[03:12] Ethan Park: Not in this iteration. That’s product work.

[03:15] Chloe Martin: Yeah, for now we show an error that says, “You don’t have permissions.” But it doesn’t say what to do.

[03:21] Chris Osei: Another action item: improve that error state with guidance.

[03:25] Chloe Martin: Yep.

[03:26] Chloe Martin: Okay, selecting “create new org.” We ask for org name, region preference, and then we show the “data processing region” dropdown.

[03:36] Kira Thompson: Region naming needs to match our residency language. Like “US” vs “us-east-1.” We should use customer-friendly names.

[03:42] Elliot Price: Also, docs need to clarify what region affects. Like model runtime region vs where logs are stored.

[03:48] Ben Carter: We should align with the security packet wording, otherwise we contradict ourselves.

[03:53] Ethan Park: From an implementation standpoint, this sets the control plane region and where inference is served by default. But you can still route per request depending on policy, for Hosted.

[04:03] Markus Klein: That’s too nuanced for onboarding. We should say “Default region for serving and logs” and then “contact us for constraints.”

[04:11] Chloe Martin: When you click “Provision,” it triggers org creation and also ties the entitlement to the org.

[04:18] Cole Summers: And from billing, that’s the moment we start metering under the marketplace account.

[04:22] Ben Carter: Wait, do we meter before linking? Or only after org link?

[04:26] Cole Summers: We meter usage. If there’s no org, there’s no usage. But technically the subscription exists. So yeah, practically after org is created/linked.

[04:33] Nadia Rahman: For telemetry, we should track time from “entitlement verified” to “org linked.” That’s our friction point.

[04:39] Chloe Martin: Here’s the provisioning status screen. It’s a spinner that says “Creating org… syncing entitlements…”

[04:46] Markus Klein: Please add an ETA or at least “usually < 2 minutes.” Otherwise people bounce.

[04:52] Chloe Martin: It currently has no time estimate, just “This may take a few minutes.”

[04:57] Ben Carter: We need the copy to be provider-specific. AWS entitlement delays are longer than GCP, in our experience.

[05:04] Ethan Park: Yep, AWS can be like 5–15 minutes in worst case, if their side is slow. GCP is typically faster.

[05:10] Elliot Price: We should not promise 2 minutes then.

[05:12] Ben Carter: Right, maybe: “Often under 5 minutes, sometimes up to 15.” And then a “If longer than 30, contact support.”

[05:20] Dev Patel: (joins late) Sorry, I’m late—support call ran over.

[05:23] Ben Carter: Hey Dev. Perfect timing. We’re on the “entitlement delay” messaging. What’s your preference for escalation?

[05:29] Dev Patel: If it’s over 30 minutes, open a ticket. But we should also ask them to confirm the subscription is ‘Active’ in the marketplace portal. Half the time it’s ‘Pending acceptance’ or whatever.

[05:39] Chloe Martin: Good point. We can add a link “Check subscription status in AWS/GCP portal.”

[05:44] Alex Martinez: Docs can include that as step zero. “Confirm subscription is active.”

[05:49] Chris Osei: Captured.

[05:52] Chloe Martin: Okay, provisioning completed. Now we land in “Create your first API key.”

[05:58] Markus Klein: This is good. Do we also show the model name to test against?

[06:02] Chloe Martin: We show a suggested model in a dropdown. It’s currently “rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct” in staging.

[06:09] Markus Klein: That might not exist in every region or SKU.

[06:12] Cole Summers: Also, for Dedicated and Private SKUs it’s different, not just a model list.

[06:17] Kira Thompson: Our docs need to branch here: Hosted marketplace SKU gets “call the API.” Dedicated gets “request capacity + provisioning steps.” Private gets “deployment kickoff.”

[06:25] Ben Carter: Yeah. This dry-run is mostly Hosted path, but we should at least note where Dedicated/Private diverge.

[06:32] Chloe Martin: After key creation, we show a code snippet: curl, Python, Node. It’s pulling the org slug and the base URL.

[06:41] Alex Martinez: Can we include a “copy to clipboard” and a “run in browser” button?

[06:45] Chloe Martin: Copy exists. “Run in browser” not yet.

[06:49] Markus Klein: For first success, a simple “Test request” button helps. Especially when their environment variables aren’t set.

[06:55] Ben Carter: Agree. But might be out of scope.

[06:58] Ethan Park: It’s also a security question: we’d be issuing a request from the console environment.

[07:03] Marcus Lin: (briefly) Just make sure it doesn’t leak keys in logs or analytics.

[07:07] Nadia Rahman: Right now we redact the key, but I’ll double-check.

[07:11] Chloe Martin: Okay, now we simulate first request. Markus, you want to drive? You can use the sample curl.

[07:17] Markus Klein: Sure. (typing) Okay, I’m getting a 401.

[07:22] Chloe Martin: Wait—did it include the header as “Authorization: Bearer”?

[07:25] Markus Klein: I pasted it. It says “X-API-Key” but I might’ve… oh I see, it’s “X-Redwood-Key” in this snippet.

[07:33] Alex Martinez: That’s a docs bug or a snippet bug. Our public docs use “Authorization: Bearer” for some endpoints and “X-API-Key” for others.

[07:41] Ethan Park: The gateway accepts both, but the snippet should be consistent.

[07:45] Ben Carter: This is exactly why we’re doing this. Action item to fix the snippet in console and docs.

[07:51] Chloe Martin: I’ll take it. The console snippet template is in the onboarding component.

[07:56] Markus Klein: Once I correct it, it works. I get a streaming response.

[08:01] Ben Carter: Great. Now, edge cases. Let’s do “existing org” link path.

[08:06] Chloe Martin: Switching to “Link existing org.” It lists orgs I have access to.

[08:12] Elliot Price: If you have many orgs, do we have search? Some enterprise folks have like 20.

[08:17] Chloe Martin: No search yet. It’s a dropdown.

[08:20] Ben Carter: Probably okay for now.

[08:22] Chloe Martin: Okay, selecting an existing org. Click “Link.”

[08:26] Chloe Martin: Here’s an error: “E_ORG_ALREADY_LINKED.” It’s literally the raw code.

[08:33] Alex Martinez: Oof. That’s not customer-friendly.

[08:36] Chloe Martin: Yeah, we need to map codes to messages.

[08:39] Ethan Park: That’s from the entitlement service. It means that org already has a different subscription linked.

[08:44] Cole Summers: Or the same subscription was already linked, and we’re idempotent but returning that code.

[08:49] Ben Carter: What should the user do? If it’s already linked, we should say “You’re all set,” and then route them to create key / usage.

[08:56] Chloe Martin: Agreed. Right now it’s blocking.

[08:59] Dev Patel: Support perspective: this is a top ticket. People try again and think it failed.

[09:04] Chris Osei: Action item: code mapping, E_ORG_ALREADY_LINKED should be success path.

[09:08] Chloe Martin: Yep.

[09:10] Nadia Rahman: And track that event. It’s a “repeat attempt” indicator.

[09:13] Ben Carter: Let’s talk multi-subscription. If a company buys Hosted via marketplace and later buys Dedicated via marketplace. How do we represent that?

[09:22] Cole Summers: Billing catalog supports multiple entitlements per org. But the UX doesn’t. It assumes one subscription.

[09:28] Ethan Park: There’s a ticket for multi-subscription edge cases. We’re not fully done.

[09:32] Markus Klein: We’ll hit this in enterprise. They’ll start with Hosted usage and then procurement will want Dedicated with a commit.

[09:39] Kira Thompson: Docs need to say “contact sales/support for upgrades” for now, unless we truly support it self-serve.

[09:44] Ben Carter: Right. We should not pretend self-serve upgrade works if it doesn’t.

[09:49] Chloe Martin: Okay, switching to GCP provider. Here, the input is “Entitlement ID.” Same general flow.

[09:57] Elliot Price: Do we have separate docs pages per cloud? The UI changes slightly.

[10:01] Alex Martinez: I think we should. Otherwise it’s too confusing.

[10:05] Ben Carter: On listing pages, they’ll want cloud-specific instructions anyway.

[10:09] Chloe Martin: For GCP, when entitlement is found, the response returns the “billing account id” and the “plan.” But I’m not showing it right now.

[10:17] Cole Summers: Please don’t show the billing account id in UI; it’s not needed and might freak people out.

[10:22] Chloe Martin: Got it.

[10:24] Ben Carter: Cole, can you confirm we’re aligned on the marketplace SKU naming and internal meters? We had that mismatch last week.

[10:30] Cole Summers: Mostly aligned. The Hosted dimension is tokens. Embeddings is separate. Rerank is separate. The mismatch was around a legacy “requests” dimension. We’ve removed it. But docs should say “billing is token-based,” not “request-based.”

[10:43] Elliot Price: Yep, we’ll update.

[10:45] Ben Carter: Another user story: procurement buys via marketplace, engineer gets notified, goes to console, but they don’t know they need to start at /onboarding/marketplace. How do they discover it?

[10:56] Chloe Martin: We added a banner on the normal onboarding page: “Purchased via marketplace? Start here.” But it’s only visible if you have no API key.

[11:06] Markus Klein: If they already have keys, they might not see it. But they still need to link subscription for billing.

[11:11] Ethan Park: That’s tricky. You can have keys and still be unlinked.

[11:14] Dev Patel: Then we need a console settings page: “Marketplace subscriptions” with link/unlink status.

[11:20] Chloe Martin: Not built yet.

[11:22] Ben Carter: For this launch, at minimum, docs should mention “If you already have an org, go to Settings → Billing → Marketplace” (if it exists) or give a direct URL.

[11:33] Chloe Martin: We can create a hidden route. But we need to decide.

[11:36] Chris Osei: Noting as open product gap. We can decide in go/no-go.

[11:41] Alex Martinez: Another docs gap: “Who can create API keys?” We should clarify roles and recommend creating a service account key.

[11:48] Marcus Lin: And we should mention key rotation best practices, without over-promising. Like “rotate regularly,” “store in secrets manager.”

[11:55] Elliot Price: We can link to the general API key management doc.

[12:00] Ben Carter: Cool. Let’s jump to troubleshooting. What happens if entitlement verification returns “subscription not active?”

[12:07] Ethan Park: E_SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_ACTIVE. That usually means the marketplace purchase is not accepted, or it’s cancelled.

[12:13] Chloe Martin: Right now we show “Not active.” No guidance.

[12:17] Dev Patel: Guidance should be “Check marketplace portal status,” “confirm you used the right account,” and “if you’re using a private offer, confirm it’s accepted.”

[12:28] Ben Carter: And include private offer activation timing. That’s another common question.

[12:34] Markus Klein: I’ve seen customers ask “We accepted the private offer, why are limits still the old tier?” That’s basically entitlement sync delay or wrong subscription link.

[12:43] Nadia Rahman: If we track entitlement changes, we can detect that mismatch and alert.

[12:48] Cole Summers: The meter mapping should update immediately once entitlements update, but the console might cache.

[12:54] Chloe Martin: Console caches org billing status for like 60 seconds. Not huge.

[12:59] Ben Carter: Okay. Let’s do a quick docs review: Elliot/Alex, what pages exist right now, and what’s missing?

[13:07] Elliot Price: We have a draft “AWS Marketplace onboarding” page and “GCP Marketplace onboarding” page. What’s missing is: (1) where to find the subscription identifiers, (2) existing org linking steps, (3) a troubleshooting section with these exact errors, and (4) clarifying billing dimensions: tokens vs embeddings.

[13:27] Alex Martinez: Also, we should have a simple diagram: Marketplace → Redwood entitlement service → Redwood org provisioning → API key → first call. People like a picture.

[13:36] Kira Thompson: And for Dedicated/Private SKUs, we need a “What happens after purchase?” page. Because it’s not immediate.

[13:43] Ben Carter: Right. That’s procurement-led, they expect an onboarding motion.

[13:48] Markus Klein: For Dedicated, it’s “we confirm region, GPU type, throughput, then we provision capacity.” That can be days.

[13:55] Chloe Martin: The console shouldn’t imply immediate usage for Dedicated/Private. We might need a different post-purchase screen.

[14:03] Ethan Park: That’s also gating; Dedicated requires internal provisioning.

[14:07] Ben Carter: Okay. We’ll log that as out-of-scope for Hosted launch but needs a clear message.

[14:13] Chris Osei: Noted.

[14:15] (crosstalk)

[14:16] Ben Carter: Let’s do a “happy path” recap as if we’re writing the listing “How it works” section. Step 1 subscribe, step 2 open Redwood console marketplace onboarding, step 3 verify entitlement, step 4 create or link org, step 5 create key, step 6 run example.

[14:32] Alex Martinez: We also need to mention “entitlement can take a few minutes.” That’s a step.

[14:37] Dev Patel: And “if over 30 minutes, contact support with these IDs.” We should define what IDs: subscription id, org id, timestamp.

[14:45] Chloe Martin: We can show a “Copy diagnostics” button on the error state. That would save support time.

[14:51] Ben Carter: That’s a great idea, but might be too much before launch.

[14:55] Chris Osei: Could be a stretch, but let’s capture it.

[14:58] Nadia Rahman: Even without “copy diagnostics,” we should log correlation ids and tell user “include correlation id.”

[15:05] Ethan Park: We already generate request id in the API response. But the UI needs to display it.

[15:10] Chloe Martin: Currently it doesn’t.

[15:12] Ben Carter: Alright. Another gap: security docs. We should link the marketplace security overview from onboarding.

[15:19] Elliot Price: Where do we want that link? In docs and in the listing. But in the console too, maybe under “Need security info?”

[15:27] Marcus Lin: As long as the wording is approved. Don’t say “HIPAA compliant” or anything.

[15:31] Ben Carter: We’ll stick to “SOC 2 report available under NDA” type language.

[15:36] Alex Martinez: The docs page should also include “shared responsibility” quick bullets. Marketplace reviewers expect it.

[15:43] Ben Carter: Great. Switching gears: instrumentation. Nadia, what funnel events do we have and what’s missing?

[15:51] Nadia Rahman: We have: marketplace_onboarding_viewed, entitlement_verify_clicked, entitlement_verify_success/failure, org_create_started, org_link_started, org_link_success/failure, api_key_created, first_request_doc_clicked (if they click docs). What’s missing is: refresh retries count, time-to-entitlement in backend vs UI, and a “gave up” signal. We can approximate “gave up” by no further events within 30 minutes.

[16:19] Ben Carter: Perfect. That ties to our success metrics.

[16:23] Cole Summers: Also ensure billing dimension mapping is logged at org link time; it helps debugging mismatches.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Careful with PII, but dimension names are fine.

[16:34] Chloe Martin: One more UX thing: on the “verify entitlement” step, we should allow sign-in later. Right now it forces you to sign in before even checking entitlement.

[16:44] Markus Klein: Customers might want to verify without creating an account first.

[16:48] Ben Carter: But if we verify without sign-in, what do we do next? We still need an org.

[16:53] Alex Martinez: We can verify and then say “Sign in to continue.” That’s less friction.

[16:59] Ethan Park: As long as we’re not exposing too much entitlement info without auth.

[17:04] Chloe Martin: We can show just “Verified” without details.

[17:08] Ben Carter: Okay, capture as maybe nice-to-have.

[17:12] Chris Osei: Noted.

[17:14] (brief pause)

[17:16] Ben Carter: Let’s do a quick run-through of the listing FAQ questions we keep hearing: “How long until I can use it?”, “How does billing work?”, “Can I use my existing org?”, “What if I have multiple subscriptions?”, “Who do I contact for support?”, “Where are security docs?”

[17:33] Dev Patel: On support: marketplace customers should still use support portal, but we should also mention “for procurement issues contact your cloud provider.” People get confused.

[17:42] Ben Carter: Right. And we should include the cloud provider’s refund policy is not controlled by us.

[17:47] Cole Summers: Also credits/trials: we probably can’t do free credits through marketplace easily. Need to be careful.

[17:54] Ben Carter: Yeah, no mention of free credits in marketplace listing unless confirmed.

[17:59] Elliot Price: Docs can say “For trials, contact sales.”

[18:03] Markus Klein: That’s fine.

[18:05] Chloe Martin: Quick check: On the API key page, we don’t explain rate limits or quotas for marketplace SKU. Should we?

[18:12] Ben Carter: We should at least link to “Usage limits.” Customers will ask “why am I throttled.”

[18:18] Alex Martinez: We can add a callout “Your quota is set by your plan. View in Settings.”

[18:23] Chloe Martin: Okay.

[18:25] Ben Carter: Alright, we’re at about 20 minutes. Let’s go through action items and confirm owners.

[18:31] Chris Osei: I’ve got: Chloe updating entitlement delay messaging + mapping raw error codes to friendly messages, Alex/Elliot adding docs for identifiers + org linking + troubleshooting, Cole confirming final billing dimensions + sharing mapping table, Nadia verifying telemetry for retries and building dashboard proposal, Markus providing marketplace-first quickstart snippet.

[18:55] Ben Carter: Great. Any other gaps we didn’t capture?

[18:59] Kira Thompson: Dedicated/Private divergence needs a doc page. Even if simple.

[19:04] Elliot Price: Yep, I’ll coordinate with Kira on that. But we should confirm scope.

[19:09] Ben Carter: Put it as a follow-up. We can do a minimal “After purchase” page for each SKU.

[19:14] Ethan Park: Engineering-wise, the biggest risk is entitlement sync reliability. But we’re working on retries and idempotency.

[19:22] Nadia Rahman: If the sync job fails, we need alerting thresholds. Otherwise, customers are stuck.

[19:27] Ben Carter: That’s part of platform work. Ethan, can you sync with Priya/PagerDuty on that?

[19:32] Ethan Park: Yep.

[19:33] Ben Carter: Cool. Let’s wrap with go/no-go criteria: (1) no raw error codes in UI, (2) docs pages published with identifiers + troubleshooting, (3) snippet headers consistent, (4) telemetry confirms we can measure time-to-first-token, and (5) entitlement delay messaging is accurate.

[19:53] Chris Osei: I’ll post notes in the project channel.

[19:55] Ben Carter: Thanks all. Ending recording.

(Recording ends; meeting continued briefly off-record)

title: Marketplace onboarding walkthrough (internal dry run) — subscription → org provisioning → first key
preview: Internal walkthrough of the end-to-end marketplace onboarding flow to validate the UX and docs before submission. Team identified confusion around org linking for existing Redwood orgs, unclear messaging during entitlement propagation delays (AWS especially), and missing docs for the "already subscribed" and "multiple subscriptions" edge cases. Engineering confirmed expected entitlement API error codes and noted where console should map them to actionable retry guidance. Docs team flagged needed screenshots and a clearer separation of AWS vs GCP steps, plus a single canonical "How marketplace billing maps to usage" section to reduce support tickets.
