source.title: Lifecycle growth sprint — retargeting launch checklist
source.summary: Internal working session to finalize retargeting launch details for the lifecycle growth experiments sprint: audience definitions and suppression rules (active opps + enterprise domains), UTM naming conventions, landing page mapping, creative variant set, spend caps, and measurement expectations. Team aligned on a conservative ramp, a hard exclusion list refresh cadence, and how to interpret activation vs retention signals for retargeting.
topics: Audiences (site visitors, signup-no-activation, activated-but-no-return)
Suppression/exclusions (active sales opps, enterprise domains, regulated accounts)
UTM conventions + landing page mapping
Creative kit and message hierarchy (developer-first, latency/cost/reliability)
Spend caps, pacing, and stop/go thresholds
Attribution + activation measurement guardrails
next_steps: Tyler to publish final UTM conventions + required fields in a short doc and pin in #marketing.
Isabelle to automate weekly export/refresh of active opp + sensitive account suppression list (Mon 9am PT).
Jordan/Stephanie to confirm exclusion logic for “active opp” stages and enterprise domain list criteria.
Tessa/Kenji to deliver final creative exports (static + 2 copy variants per audience) and naming scheme.
Ben to create final launch checklist in Linear/PM ticket, including spend cap and ramp plan, and schedule a 15-min go/no-go for launch day.
action_items: Tyler Benson — Finalize UTM schema (utm_source/utm_medium/utm_campaign/utm_content/utm_term) + examples for LinkedIn and Google; share in Slack — Due 2026-01-28 EOD
Isabelle Laurent — Set up suppression list pipeline (active opps + do-not-target accounts) and confirm it hits ad platforms before daily sync — Due 2026-01-29
Jordan Blake — Confirm exact CRM stages to treat as “active opp” for suppression; include re-opened opp rule — Due 2026-01-29
Stephanie Nguyen — Validate enterprise domain patterns and “strategic named accounts” list inclusion — Due 2026-01-29
Tessa Morgan — Provide final creative kit exports + file naming convention; include ‘developer-first’ variant set — Due 2026-01-28
Kenji Nakamura — Final copy QA for ad headlines/descriptions; ensure messaging guidelines compliance — Due 2026-01-28
Ben Carter — Confirm spend caps and ramp (Day 1/Day 2/Day 3), and add stop-loss rules to the launch plan — Due 2026-01-28
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-01-28
Start time: 10:03 AM PT
Duration: 46 min
Attendees: Ben Carter, Tyler Benson, Camila Reyes, Soojin Lee, Tessa Morgan, Kenji Nakamura, Jordan Blake, Stephanie Nguyen, Isabelle Laurent

Auto-summary (Fireflies)
- Purpose: Retargeting launch checklist and final approvals
- Key outcomes: Final audiences + exclusions agreed; UTMs standardized; ramp plan confirmed; creative set reduced to 2–3 variants per segment

10:03 Ben Carter: Alright, thanks everybody. This is the last retargeting launch checklist before we turn spend on. Goal today: lock audiences, the suppressions with Sales, UTMs, landing pages, and the spend cap and ramp. If we leave with owners, we’re good.

10:04 Tyler Benson: Yup. And just a reminder, if UTMs are inconsistent we’re dead in attribution. So I’m gonna be annoying about it.

10:04 Camila Reyes: Please be annoying. Also, conservative ramp. We don’t need hero numbers on day one.

10:05 Ben Carter: Cool. Let’s start with audiences. Current draft is three buckets: (1) site visitors, (2) signup-no-activation, (3) activated-but-no-return. Jordan, Stephanie, we’re still aligned on exclusions for active opps and strategic accounts?

10:05 Jordan Blake: Yes, but we need to be precise on “active.” Marketing keeps asking for a simple definition and Sales keeps giving like seven caveats.

10:06 Stephanie Nguyen: The short version: if there’s an open opportunity in any late stage, or it’s assigned to an AE and in active eval, don’t retarget. I’d rather under-target than have a customer ask why they’re seeing ads while negotiating.

10:06 Isabelle Laurent: From CRM side, I can export a suppression list keyed by company domain and account id. The tricky part is if the signup uses Gmail, then it doesn’t match the company domain.

10:07 Tyler Benson: For Gmail signups we can suppress via HubSpot company association when it exists, but that lags. So I’d treat Gmail users as eligible unless they’ve been associated to an enterprise account, then suppression kicks in.

10:07 Jordan Blake: That’s fine, as long as once they are associated, they’re out. Also, include re-opened opps. We have accounts that churn the deal and reopen in 30 days.

10:08 Ben Carter: Okay. Audience 1: site visitors. Tyler, what’s the lookback window?

10:08 Tyler Benson: Proposed 30-day lookback for “docs + pricing page visitors,” and a tighter 14-day for “console login page visitors” because that tends to overlap with active users. We don’t want to annoy current users.

10:09 Camila Reyes: Wait, console login page visitors could include existing customers. Can we exclude “active users last 7 days” from all audiences?

10:09 Tyler Benson: Yes, with product events. We can do a suppression: any user with api_request_succeeded in last 7 days, exclude from retargeting.

10:09 Ben Carter: That’s consistent with the guardrail—don’t pay to reach people who already activated.

10:10 Soojin Lee: Plus messaging. If someone’s already using us, and they see “Get your first request running,” it feels off.

10:10 Ben Carter: Audience 2: signup-no-activation. Definition: signed up, no first successful request within 7 days. Also likely no 100k tokens. Are we using “no API key created” as sub-segment?

10:11 Tyler Benson: I’d keep it simple at platform level: “signup event fired, no request success event.” But we can do creative routing: if no api_key_created, ad copy points to “Create your API key.” If they have a key but no request, point to the hello-world snippet.

10:11 Kenji Nakamura: That means we need two copy sets for the segment.

10:12 Tessa Morgan: And two landing pages or same landing page with anchors?

10:12 Ben Carter: Ideally same landing page for measurement. Tyler, can we route them to the same quickstart page but with different UTM content and maybe a hash?

10:12 Tyler Benson: Yes. Use the same canonical URL. If you add a hash like #create-key it won’t hit analytics consistently. Better: same page, and the page itself has the key creation step above the fold.

10:13 Camila Reyes: Agree. Don’t get clever and break tracking.

10:13 Ben Carter: Audience 3: activated-but-no-return. Definition: they did first successful request and/or hit 100k tokens, but no activity in last, what, 14 days?

10:14 Tyler Benson: For early retention, I prefer 7-day inactivity window after activation. But for retargeting we should not be too aggressive. Proposed: activated, then no active usage for 10 days. Lookback 60 days for membership.

10:14 Soojin Lee: Copy should be “pick up where you left off” and highlight reliability/performance, not “try Redwood.”

10:15 Ben Carter: Great. Now exclusions. We have three layers: (a) active opp suppression list from CRM, (b) regulated/sensitive accounts list, (c) product activity suppression “active in last 7 days.” Isabelle, can you refresh the CRM suppression weekly?

10:15 Isabelle Laurent: Weekly is easy, daily is harder because approvals. But I can do Monday 9am PT weekly and also a manual refresh before end-of-quarter pushes.

10:16 Jordan Blake: Weekly is acceptable if we also allow “manual add” for hot accounts.

10:16 Ben Carter: We’ll do a “hot add” channel. Like #plg-retargeting-suppressions. Tyler or Isabelle can apply within 24 hours.

10:16 Tyler Benson: I’ll own the Slack channel and process. But we need strict rules: no random ‘I don’t like them’ adds.

10:17 Stephanie Nguyen: Criteria: named account, active negotiation, or procurement. I’ll document.

10:17 Ben Carter: Cool. UTMs next. Tyler, can you walk us through the final convention? We got into a debate in Slack about utm_campaign vs utm_content.

10:18 Tyler Benson: Yeah. Here’s the final:
- utm_source: linkedin | google | x | meta (lowercase)
- utm_medium: paid_social | paid_search | display
- utm_campaign: lifecycle_growth_q1_2026 (fixed) + audience suffix, like lifecycle_growth_q1_2026_signup_no_activation
- utm_content: creative_id and message angle, like c01_latency_devfirst, c02_cost_tokens, c03_reliability_sla
- utm_term: optional, for search only (keyword)
And we do not use spaces. Dashes or underscores only.

10:19 Ben Carter: And landing pages mapping?

10:19 Tyler Benson: Three landing targets max:
1) /docs/quickstart (for site visitors and signup-no-activation)
2) /console/onboarding (for activated-but-no-return, but only if they can authenticate; otherwise it’s a dead end)
3) /pricing (only for site visitors who hit pricing page previously; otherwise it causes bounce)

10:20 Camila Reyes: I’m nervous about sending cold-ish users to console onboarding.

10:20 Ben Carter: We can keep it docs-first for all segments for now. Activated-but-no-return gets docs page with “next steps” and “observability + usage dashboard” callout.

10:21 Soojin Lee: Yeah, and make sure the headline doesn’t over-promise. Approved phrasing is “production-grade inference” and “predictable latency/cost,” not “best latency.”

10:21 Kenji Nakamura: Noted. Also, do we mention “Dedicated” at all?

10:22 Ben Carter: For retargeting, no. This is Hosted API activation. If they raise signals, sales gets handoff later.

10:22 Jordan Blake: Thank you.

10:22 Ben Carter: Creative kit. Tessa, where are we?

10:23 Tessa Morgan: We have a full set, but I want to reduce it. Proposed final exports:
- Two statics for each audience (so 6 total), plus one universal “developer-first” variant.
- Each has two copy angles: latency/perf and cost predictability.
- We’ll avoid too many claims. No numbers unless verified.

10:24 Kenji Nakamura: And I’ll do a copy QA pass. Some of the early versions said “drop-in replacement” which Soojin hates.

10:24 Soojin Lee: It’s not that I hate it, it’s that it creates expectation mismatch. “Drop-in” implies zero changes.

10:25 Ben Carter: Right. “Fast to integrate” is safer.

10:25 Tyler Benson: Also please name files with creative IDs that match utm_content. Like c01, c02. Otherwise we can’t reconcile.

10:26 Tessa Morgan: Got it. c01 through c07.

10:26 Ben Carter: Spend caps and ramp. Camila, we have approval up to what, $12k for the week?

10:26 Camila Reyes: Cap is $12k total for week one across platforms. But I want a daily cap and a stop-loss if CPA is insane.

10:27 Tyler Benson: Proposed ramp:
- Day 1: $800 total
- Day 2: $1,200
- Day 3: $1,800
- Day 4 onward: up to $2,500/day if CPA stays under threshold
Threshold: $120 cost per activated signup (first successful request within 7 days). Secondary: $180 per 100k tokens milestone.

10:28 Ben Carter: And if we don’t have enough conversion volume day 1-2?

10:28 Tyler Benson: Then we optimize to leading indicators: landing page CTR and doc quickstart completion events, but we do not declare winners.

10:29 Camila Reyes: Yes. No premature decisions. Also, we need to watch for support load. If ads drive junk signups that open tickets, we pause.

10:29 Ben Carter: Good call. We’ll add “support tickets per 100 signups” as a guardrail, even if it’s directional.

10:30 Jordan Blake: One more sales concern: if retargeting pulls in bigger accounts, you need to route them to sales-assisted quickly, but don’t retarget them once the AE is engaged.

10:31 Ben Carter: We’ll stick with suppression. Handoff signal is separate: if they hit usage threshold or request security docs, it becomes sales-assisted and then suppressed.

10:31 Isabelle Laurent: I can add “requested security review” as suppression if it’s logged.

10:32 Tyler Benson: That’s in HubSpot as a property sometimes. Not always reliable.

10:32 Stephanie Nguyen: We’ll take best-effort and do manual adds for now.

10:33 Ben Carter: Measurement: Tyler, confirm attribution window.

10:33 Tyler Benson: 7-day click, 1-day view. But for reporting to this sprint, we’ll focus on click-through conversions for activation to avoid inflated view-through. We can show view-through as a footnote.

10:34 Camila Reyes: Please. Otherwise we’ll fool ourselves.

10:34 Ben Carter: Anything else on compliance? CAN-SPAM is email, but ads still need sensitivity. Olga isn’t on this call; any red flags?

10:35 Soojin Lee: Messaging should avoid implying we know what they’re building. No creepy personalization. Also we should not target regulated accounts; Finchbank style accounts should be excluded.

10:35 Jordan Blake: And healthcare. Quantico Health, etc.

10:36 Isabelle Laurent: I’ll include the sensitive accounts list from Sales Ops.

10:36 Ben Carter: Okay. Last piece: platform mix. Are we doing LinkedIn + Google only, or also X/Meta?

10:37 Tyler Benson: Week one: LinkedIn and Google Display. X is too noisy for this, Meta has policy friction for B2B dev audiences and we don’t have enough creative sizes ready.

10:37 Tessa Morgan: Thank you.

10:38 Kenji Nakamura: I’ll tailor copy lengths for LinkedIn vs Google banners.

10:38 Ben Carter: Great. Let’s do a quick recap of decisions.

10:39 Ben Carter: Decisions:
1) Audiences: site visitors (30d; pricing/docs), signup-no-activation (signup + no request success in 7d), activated-but-no-return (activated + inactive 10d, lookback 60d).
2) Exclusions: active opp suppression weekly + manual hot adds; regulated/sensitive list; exclude active product users last 7d.
3) UTMs: Tyler’s schema, consistent naming, creative IDs map to utm_content.
4) Landing pages: default docs quickstart; no console-only landing for now.
5) Ramp/caps: start $800 day 1, ramp to $2.5k/day contingent on CPA; stop-loss if CPA way above threshold.

10:40 Camila Reyes: And reporting is click-through for primary, view-through as secondary.

10:40 Tyler Benson: Yes, and I’ll put it in the pinned doc.

10:41 Jordan Blake: Please also confirm the definition of “activated signup” for CPA. First successful request, not just key creation.

10:41 Ben Carter: Correct. First successful request within 7 days of click.

10:42 Kenji Nakamura: Who’s approving final copy before launch?

10:42 Soojin Lee: I can do a quick pass today, but keep it minimal. I don’t want to restart the whole thing.

10:43 Tessa Morgan: I’ll send exports in two hours.

10:43 Isabelle Laurent: I’ll start the suppression export right after this. I need the exact stage list from Jordan.

10:44 Jordan Blake: I’ll send it in 30 minutes.

10:44 Tyler Benson: And I’ll create the Slack channel for suppression requests.

10:45 Ben Carter: Perfect. Launch go/no-go: tomorrow 9:30 AM PT for 15 minutes, just to confirm creative loaded, UTMs validated, suppression list in place.

10:45 Camila Reyes: Works.

10:46 Ben Carter: Alright, thanks everyone. I’ll post notes and action items. End.

topics: Week 1 onboarding email v2 A/B early results
Segmentation: new users vs stalled (no API key / no first request)
Activation measurement (first successful request; first 100k tokens)
Deliverability + suppression rules
Week 2 console onboarding nudges rollout plan
Feature flags / holdout cohort
Instrumentation + funnel dashboard sanity checks
source.summary: Team reviewed early Week 1 results for onboarding email v2 A/B (opens/clicks and preliminary activation lift), agreed to keep test running through end of week while fixing a small tracking discrepancy. Product/Eng walked through Week 2 console nudge rollout: targeted checklist-style nudges (API key creation + first request) behind feature flags with a holdout group, plus QA and dismissal behavior fixes before ramping exposure.
action_items: Tyler Benson - confirm email A/B sample size math and update readout chart with confidence intervals; add note on weekday effect - Due 2026-01-22
Logan Wright - sanity-check activation events post-dedupe (signup, api_key_created, first_request_succeeded) in dashboard; verify region completeness - Due 2026-01-22
Paula Mendes - finalize stop/go thresholds for Week 2 nudge experiment (CTR, step completion, downstream activation) and post in PM-651 - Due 2026-01-23
Dylan Brooks - ensure nudge feature flag + 10% holdout assignment is wired for new signups cohort; confirm persistence of dismissal state - Due 2026-01-24
Kenji Nakamura - deliver final copy for 2 nudge variants (API key + first request) aligned with messaging guidelines; include CTA labels - Due 2026-01-23
Soojin Lee - quick messaging pass on email follow-up (Day 3) to emphasize reliability/latency without over-claiming; approve by EOD - Due 2026-01-22
Ben Carter - send recap + next week launch checklist link and escalation path (deliverability + support) - Due 2026-01-21
next_steps: Keep email v2 A/B running through Friday unless complaints/bounces exceed guardrails; decide winner in Monday readout.
Ship console nudge framework behind feature flag Monday; start at 10% exposure Tuesday, ramp to 50% by Thursday if metrics are stable.
Validate funnel dashboard numbers daily while new experiments roll out (watch for event drops/spikes and support tickets).
source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai - Transcript

Title: Weekly Growth Experiments Readout (Lifecycle sprint) - Jan 21
Date: 2026-01-21
Start time: 10:07 AM PT
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Ben Carter, Camila Reyes, Tyler Benson, Soojin Lee, Marissa Cole, Paula Mendes, Dylan Brooks, Logan Wright, Tessa Morgan, Kenji Nakamura

---

[00:00] Ben Carter: Cool, alright. I think we’re good to start. This is the weekly readout for lifecycle growth sprint. Goal today is quick pass on Week 1 email A/B early results, any deliverability red flags, and then align on next week’s console nudge rollout plan.

[00:18] Camila Reyes: Sounds good.

[00:20] Ben Carter: Tyler, do you want to start with the email numbers? Keep it like, five minutes.

[00:26] Tyler Benson: Yep. Let me share what I’ve got. So this is the onboarding email v2 experiment, Day 0 send, triggered on signup. We split 50/50: Variant A is “Ship your first LLM request in 5 minutes” subject, Variant B is “Your Redwood API key + quickstart” subject. Audience is net new signups, excluding enterprise domains list and anyone with existing workspace members.

[00:53] Tyler Benson: Early sample size is 3,180 delivered total since Monday. Opens: A is 52.4%, B is 48.1%. Click-through (unique): A 9.6%, B 11.2%. The click rate is favoring B, but opens favor A. So not a clean winner yet.

[01:13] Ben Carter: And activation?

[01:15] Tyler Benson: Yeah, so for activation proxy, we’re looking at “first successful request within 24 hours” as the early cut. A is 21.0%, B is 22.6%. It’s directionally better on B, but it’s early and the confidence is… not great. I don’t want to over-call it.

[01:33] Logan Wright: Quick note, I’m seeing some weirdness in EU-West events. Like, the signup to api_key_created step is lower than normal. It might be the same discrepancy we saw last week.

[01:42] Ben Carter: Okay, that’s important. Is that in the dashboard build, or the raw events?

[01:46] Logan Wright: Raw events in the warehouse look fine for US, but EU has like… missing request_succeeded events intermittently. Could be ingestion lag or a sampling thing. I’ll dig today.

[01:58] Paula Mendes: Just flagging, if it’s region specific we need to annotate the readout so we don’t interpret A/B lift incorrectly. Especially since we’re ramping next week’s console stuff.

[02:08] Tyler Benson: Yeah, agreed. Right now I’m using a blended view. If EU is undercounting “request_succeeded” it can bias activation down.

[02:16] Ben Carter: Great. Let’s talk guardrails. Deliverability: complaint rate, bounces.

[02:21] Tyler Benson: So far, soft bounce 0.7%, hard bounce 0.18%. Complaint rate 0.03%. Unsub is 0.4%. All within what we said. No big spikes.

[02:32] Soojin Lee: Any replies? Like, “stop emailing me” type.

[02:35] Tyler Benson: Some, but nothing alarming. Mostly “can you help me find pricing” or “how do I get a model list.” Support tagged two tickets but they’re more product questions.

[02:45] Ben Carter: Okay. Copy feedback? Soojin, anything you want changed before Day 3 email goes out?

[02:51] Soojin Lee: The only thing: I want us to be careful with “fastest inference” claims. We can say “low latency” and “predictable performance” without sounding like we’re taking a swing at OpenAI or Anthropic. Also, the line about “no infra required” is fine, but we should pair it with “Hosted API” language so it’s not confusing vs Dedicated.

[03:10] Ben Carter: Cool. Kenji, Tessa, any design constraints showing up?

[03:14] Tessa Morgan: Email template is holding up on mobile. The one thing is the code snippet block is a little dense on smaller screens. It’s readable but tight. We might need to shorten the snippet or add a “copy” affordance later.

[03:27] Ben Carter: That’s more like iteration. For now, I’d rather not touch template mid-test.

[03:31] Tessa Morgan: Agreed.

[03:33] Ben Carter: Okay, decision: keep the A/B running through end of week. Tyler you’ll add a note about region discrepancy. Logan to validate. Next, console nudges for next week. Marissa, can you walk us through what’s shipping and how we’re measuring?

[03:48] Marissa Cole: Yep. So Week 2 is the in-console onboarding nudge framework. We’re starting small: two nudges, basically checklist items, on the main dashboard page for new Hosted API users.

[04:01] Marissa Cole: Nudge 1: “Create an API key.” CTA goes to the API keys page. Success is api_key_created event.

[04:08] Marissa Cole: Nudge 2: “Send your first request.” CTA opens the minimal snippet (TypeScript + curl) and links to quickstart. Success is first_request_succeeded event.

[04:18] Marissa Cole: Eligibility: newly created workspaces within last 14 days, and only if they have not yet completed that step. So if they already created a key, we don’t show the key nudge.

[04:28] Dylan Brooks: Implementation detail: it’s behind a feature flag, “lifecycle_growth_nudges_v1”. We can target by cohort assignment at signup. Also we have a holdout group.

[04:39] Paula Mendes: Holdout percentage?

[04:41] Dylan Brooks: We proposed 10% holdout for the experiment measurement. Remaining 90% sees nudges, but we can ramp exposure with a separate rollout gate.

[04:50] Ben Carter: And the ramp plan is like 10% Tuesday, 25% Wednesday, 50% Thursday, then maybe 90% Friday if no issues?

[04:57] Dylan Brooks: Yeah, exactly. We can throttle at the flag level.

[05:01] Paula Mendes: What’s our stop condition? Like, if CTR is high but activation doesn’t move, do we keep?

[05:08] Marissa Cole: For Week 2, the primary is completion rate per step and downstream activation within seven days. But since it’s early, we’ll watch leading indicators: CTR on the nudge, time-to-api-key, time-to-first-request. Also make sure we don’t break the API key page.

[05:23] Kenji Nakamura: On copy, I’ve got variants. One is more direct: “Create your API key (required)”. The other is softer: “Create an API key to start sending requests.” I worry the “required” word feels scold-y.

[05:37] Soojin Lee: Yeah, I’d avoid “required.” Better: “Step 1 of 2” vibe. Make it feel like progress.

[05:43] Ben Carter: Agree. We should keep the value prop consistent: quick path to first token.

[05:48] Logan Wright: On measurement, we need to make sure “nudge_viewed” and “nudge_clicked” events are standardized, otherwise CTR is sketch.

[05:56] Dylan Brooks: The PR has “nudge_impression” and “nudge_click” with nudge_id, page, workspace_id, user_id. There’s also “dismissed”. But I’m not 100% on dedupe.

[06:09] Logan Wright: Yeah, dedupe is important because impressions fire on page render and we might be double-firing in React transitions. I’ll check.

[06:18] Paula Mendes: And we need downstream attribution. Like, if they click but complete the step later from another surface, we still count completion, but we should look at median time-to-complete.

[06:29] Marissa Cole: Yup. We’ll do both. Also: dismissal behavior. We decided “dismiss” hides the nudge for seven days, not forever, right?

[06:39] Dylan Brooks: Right now it’s persistent dismiss, meaning it stays dismissed until step is completed or user clears state. We can change that, but it’s not in the current build.

[06:49] Marissa Cole: I’d prefer seven days, because new users often dismiss stuff and then need it later.

[06:54] Ben Carter: What’s the risk if it’s persistent dismiss for first iteration?

[06:58] Kenji Nakamura: UX risk is they dismiss accidentally and then lose the guidance. Also could reduce effectiveness and we’d misread it as “nudges don’t work.”

[07:09] Dylan Brooks: We can do a quick patch to store a timestamp and re-show after seven days. That’s doable but it’s a little change.

[07:17] Camila Reyes: If it’s small, I’d rather fix now than ship something that underperforms for a dumb reason.

[07:23] Ben Carter: Okay, Dylan, can you scope it? If it’s < half day, do it. If not, ship and we note it as limitation.

[07:31] Dylan Brooks: I’ll check with Chloe after this.

[07:34] Ben Carter: Any other edge cases? I saw a note that a nudge might show on the wrong page.

[07:39] Marissa Cole: That was in staging. It showed on the API keys page itself, which is weird. The idea is it only shows on the main dashboard.

[07:47] Dylan Brooks: That was a route matching issue. It should be fixed, but QA needs to validate.

[07:52] Leila (Speaker 1): I’m not on camera, but I can run QA passes Monday morning on staging and again after prod deploy. Just send me the checklist.

[08:01] Ben Carter: Great, thanks. Paula, anything on guardrails? Like support load, compliance.

[08:06] Paula Mendes: For console nudges, biggest guardrail is “do not block core flows.” So nudges must be non-modal and not intercept clicks. And we should watch support tickets for “can’t create API key” immediately after ramp.

[08:19] Marissa Cole: They’re non-blocking. Just a component.

[08:22] Tyler Benson: Also, attribution: if we are changing quickstarts in parallel, we should note confounding. The docs PR is going out this week, right?

[08:31] Ben Carter: Yeah, docs updates are minor link alignment. But yes, we’ll annotate.

[08:36] Soojin Lee: On messaging across surfaces: email says “run this snippet,” console says “send your first request.” That’s consistent. Just keep the “low latency, predictable cost” line as optional, not the headline.

[08:48] Ben Carter: Okay. Before we wrap: open questions on email. Tyler, do you want to adjust the triggers or suppression yet?

[08:55] Tyler Benson: Not until we get past this first week. But one question is the stalled segment: “signed up, no API key after 24 hours.” That’s a separate trigger. I’m holding it until we’re confident we’re not spamming.

[09:08] Camila Reyes: I’d rather do that after we ensure preferences/unsubscribe is clean everywhere. We had that request to add preferences link in console too.

[09:16] Dylan Brooks: There’s a PR in flight to add email preferences in console settings. Not shipping next week, but soon.

[09:24] Ben Carter: Great. Logan, when can you confirm the EU tracking thing?

[09:28] Logan Wright: Today. I’ll post in the channel by end of day. If it’s ingestion lag, we may just exclude EU from early readout.

[09:35] Ben Carter: Okay.

[09:37] Marissa Cole: One more thing: what’s the “definition of completed step” for first request? Is it request_succeeded with any model, or only from the console snippet?

[09:47] Paula Mendes: Any successful Hosted API request. We don’t want to artificially tie it to our snippet. But we should capture “source_surface” if possible.

[09:57] Dylan Brooks: We can add “source=console_snippet” if they launch request from embedded runner, but most users will do it locally. So likely not.

[10:07] Ben Carter: That’s okay. Primary is overall activation. Secondary is time-to-first-token.

[10:13] Tyler Benson: On TTFT we might need to define it carefully. Are we doing “time from signup to first streamed token” or “to first response token”? Because some users aren’t streaming.

[10:24] Paula Mendes: Good catch. For now, TTFT as time from first request start to first token is product performance; but onboarding sprint is more about “time to first successful request.” Let’s not mix.

[10:35] Ben Carter: Right. We’ll use “time-to-first-request” and “time-to-first-100k tokens” as the sprint metrics. Platform TTFT is separate.

[10:44] Camila Reyes: Okay, recap? Ben, you want to close.

[10:48] Ben Carter: Yep. Decisions: email A/B continues through Friday, no copy/template changes midstream. Soojin reviews Day 3 messaging today. Logan investigates EU tracking and dedupe. Next week: console nudges ship behind flag with 10% holdout, ramp exposure starting Tuesday if QA is green. Dylan to confirm dismissal behavior patch feasibility, and Kenji to finalize copy variants by Friday.

[11:15] Ben Carter: Tyler will update readout with confidence intervals and a note on region data quality. I’ll send recap email and link to the launch checklist / escalation path. Anything else?

[11:24] (crosstalk)

[11:26] Marissa Cole: All good.

[11:27] Dylan Brooks: Good.

[11:28] Ben Carter: Cool. Thanks all.

---
End of transcript.

title: Weekly Growth Experiments Readout (Lifecycle sprint) - Jan 21
preview: Team reviewed early Week 1 results for onboarding email v2 A/B (opens/clicks and preliminary activation lift), agreed to keep test running through end of week while fixing a small tracking discrepancy. Product/Eng walked through Week 2 console nudge rollout: targeted checklist-style nudges (API key creation + first request) behind feature flags with a holdout group, plus QA and dismissal behavior fixes before ramping exposure.

Product/Eng walked through Week 2 console nudge rollout: targeted checklist-style nudges (API key creation + first request) behind feature flags with a holdout group, plus QA and dismissal behavior fixes before ramping exposure.

source.title: Lifecycle growth experiments sprint kickoff (Hosted API activation/retention)
source.summary: Kickoff for 4-week lifecycle growth experiments sprint focused on self-serve Hosted API activation + early retention. Team aligned on primary activation KPIs (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), experiment tracks (email drip v2, in-console nudges, paid retargeting), instrumentation/event taxonomy, and guardrails (deliverability, CAN-SPAM/GDPR, sales opp exclusions, and experiment integrity). Owners confirmed and week-by-week deliverables reviewed.
topics: Sprint goals + hypotheses (activation lift, reduce time-to-first-token, improve D7/D30)
Email onboarding drip v2: segmentation, trigger logic, A/B plan, deliverability ramp
Console onboarding nudges: placement, eligibility, feature flags + holdout, success metrics
Retargeting: audiences, UTMs, spend caps, exclusion rules for active opps/enterprise domains
Measurement: event taxonomy, funnel dashboard, stop/go thresholds, data quality checks
Risks/guardrails: compliance (CAN-SPAM/GDPR), suppression lists, support load, attribution conflicts
action_items: Ben Carter — circulate finalized sprint brief + weekly timeline and link hub — due 2026-01-14 EOD
Paula Mendes — confirm activation event definitions (signup → api_key_created → first_request_succeeded → first_100k_tokens) + required properties — due 2026-01-16
Logan Wright — draft funnel dashboard spec + sanity checks (dedupe, region gaps) — due 2026-01-17
Tyler Benson — confirm email send ramp plan + suppression rules + unsubscribe handling checklist — due 2026-01-16
Soojin Lee — provide messaging guardrails + approve initial subject line set (latency vs cost vs reliability emphasis) — due 2026-01-15
Marissa Cole — list first-wave console nudge candidates + “completion” definitions — due 2026-01-17
Dylan Brooks — confirm feature flag + cohort assignment approach including holdout group mechanics — due 2026-01-17
Jordan Blake — deliver retargeting exclusions (active opps, sensitive accounts, enterprise domains) + weekly refresh process — due 2026-01-16
Michael Grant / Olga Petrov — review retargeting + lifecycle email data usage and confirm GDPR/CAN-SPAM guardrails (lookback windows, retention, opt-out) — due 2026-01-20
Allison Grant — propose alert thresholds for activation drop after launches + owner rotation — due 2026-01-21
next_steps: Week 1: lock KPI definitions + event spec, finalize email v2 copy, launch first email A/B
Week 2: ship console nudge framework behind flags + run first nudge experiment
Week 3: launch retargeting with agreed audiences/UTMs + monitor spend/CPA + iterate creatives
Week 4: consolidated readout with keep/iterate/kill decisions and follow-on backlog
source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting: Lifecycle growth experiments sprint kickoff (Hosted API activation/retention)
Date: 2026-01-14
Start time: 10:03 AM PT
Duration: 58 min
Attendees: Ben Carter, Camila Reyes, Soojin Lee, Tyler Benson, Marissa Cole, Paula Mendes, Dylan Brooks, Logan Wright, Allison Grant, Jordan Blake, Michael Grant, Olga Petrov

Auto Summary (generated): Team kicked off a 4-week sprint to improve self-serve activation and early retention for Redwood Hosted API through lifecycle emails, console nudges, and retargeting. Alignment on success metrics (first successful request and first 100k tokens in 7 days) and guardrails (deliverability, compliance, sales exclusions).

[00:00] Ben Carter: Alright, I think we’re at time. Thanks everyone for jumping on. This is the kickoff for the lifecycle growth experiments sprint. It’s four weeks, cross-functional, and the goal is pretty tight: improve self-serve activation and early retention for Hosted API.

[00:17] Camila Reyes: And just to frame it, this is the Q1 theme for activation/retention. So we’re treating this as like, learn fast, ship small, instrument everything.

[00:28] Ben Carter: Exactly. I’ll run through goals, what we’re testing, owners, and then I want us to spend time on measurement and guardrails because last time we… we had some metrics noise.

[00:43] Paula Mendes: Yeah, and also the “what counts as activation” question. We should settle that now or we’ll be arguing in Week 4.

[00:50] Ben Carter: Yup. So primary KPI: activation rate. Specifically percent of new signups that complete first successful request and first 100k tokens milestone within 7 days.

[01:05] Logan Wright: Just to clarify, 100k tokens is total tokens across requests, not a single request, right?

[01:10] Ben Carter: Right. Cumulative. And we’ll define what token source counts—like completion vs prompt—Paula we’ll sync.

[01:18] Paula Mendes: We can do total_billed_tokens, which is prompt+completion after normalization. There’s some edge cases with streaming but it’s fine.

[01:28] Ben Carter: Secondary goals: reduce time-to-first-token, time-to-first-success. And improve D7 and D30 active usage.

[01:38] Marissa Cole: For console nudges I want to be explicit: we can optimize for “step completion” and then check downstream activation, but we shouldn’t declare victory on CTR alone.

[01:49] Ben Carter: Totally. We’ll treat CTR as an input metric.

[01:54] Soojin Lee: Also messaging. If we’re changing copy across email and console, I want one consistent value prop. We keep oscillating between latency and cost.

[02:04] Camila Reyes: Can we decide on a default? My vote is: developer-first, reliable, fast. And cost is a proof point, not the headline.

[02:14] Soojin Lee: Works. We can still test subject lines but keep body consistent.

[02:20] Ben Carter: Great. Ok, experiment tracks. Track one: onboarding email drip v2. Tyler and Soojin are the core folks there with me.

[02:30] Tyler Benson: I’m mostly on ops—triggers, send schedule, deliverability ramp, and making sure we don’t spam people who already activated.

[02:38] Ben Carter: Track two: in-console nudges, Marissa as product owner, Dylan for eng, Paula on telemetry.

[02:46] Dylan Brooks: And we’ll use the new reusable nudge component, behind a feature flag. We can do cohorts plus holdout.

[02:54] Ben Carter: Track three: retargeting, I’m owner but Jordan we need sales guardrails and exclusion logic.

[03:02] Jordan Blake: Yeah, we’re supportive but you can’t retarget active opps. Also certain regulated accounts, and enterprise domains we already have in pipeline. We’ll provide a suppression list flow.

[03:15] Ben Carter: Perfect. Now measurement approach. Paula, do you want to walk event taxonomy? Then Logan on dashboards.

[03:22] Paula Mendes: Sure. The funnel we care about is: signup_created → api_key_created → first_request_succeeded → first_100k_tokens. And we also want time deltas: signup-to-key, key-to-first-success, and first-success-to-100k.

[03:39] Paula Mendes: We need to be strict on dedupe. I’ve seen duplicate signup events from some flows, especially if the console reloads.

[03:47] Dylan Brooks: Yeah, we’ve got a known issue there. Not sure it’s fixed yet.

[03:52] Logan Wright: If that’s not fixed, Week 1 results will be garbage.

[03:56] Paula Mendes: It’s fixable. But we should include a dedupe key—like user_id + event_type + day bucket or a unique request id.

[04:06] Dylan Brooks: We can do event_id on client side, plus server reconciliation for the API events. It’s not perfect but ok.

[04:14] Allison Grant: From observability side, I’d like basic guardrail alerts. If activation drops like 20% after a launch, someone gets paged—well, not paged, but Slack alerted.

[04:25] Ben Carter: Yes. Slack alert in #growth-alerts or something.

[04:29] Camila Reyes: Also we need stop/go thresholds. Like, if we see complaint rate above X or support tickets spike, we pause.

[04:38] Tyler Benson: On deliverability, standard is keep complaint rate under 0.1% and bounce under 2%. We can be even more conservative since we’re ramping.

[04:49] Michael Grant: And compliance-wise, unsubscribe has to be honored within 10 business days, but practically immediate. And don’t pre-check marketing consent for EU.

[05:00] Olga Petrov: +1. For GDPR, ensure lawful basis. For purely product onboarding emails it’s usually legitimate interest, but retargeting is more sensitive. We should keep lookback windows short and respect consent signals.

[05:13] Ben Carter: Great call. Let’s park retargeting compliance in a section.

[05:18] Soojin Lee: For email, are we calling these “product updates” or “getting started”?

[05:22] Tyler Benson: We should label as onboarding / getting started, and the footer has the physical address and unsubscribe link.

[05:30] Ben Carter: Ok, email track details. The hypothesis: a better-timed, more segmented drip reduces time-to-first-token and increases activation.

[05:41] Ben Carter: Segments: new signups (no API key), stalled (API key but no request), first-success (request succeeded but hasn’t hit 100k), and maybe “activated but no return” for a later email.

[05:56] Tyler Benson: Timing draft: 15 minutes after signup if no key, then 24 hours later if still no key, then after key creation but no request we send a “run your first request” email.

[06:08] Soojin Lee: Subject line test: “Make your first request in 2 minutes” vs “Fast, reliable LLM inference—your API key is ready”. Something like that.

[06:18] Ben Carter: We’ll A/B subject line and CTA framing. But body content: one code snippet, one link to quickstart, and one link to usage dashboard.

[06:28] Marissa Cole: On console side, we should align the “next best action” steps with that exact email CTA so people don’t get confused.

[06:36] Ben Carter: Yes. That’s a major pain point—email says do X, console says do Y.

[06:42] Marissa Cole: Proposed first-wave nudges: (1) create API key, (2) run a hello-world request, (3) enable streaming, (4) view usage dashboard, maybe (5) set up rate limits.

[06:58] Dylan Brooks: Rate limits might be too advanced for first week. But we can include it later.

[07:03] Marissa Cole: Agree. Keep it simple.

[07:06] Paula Mendes: We need to define completion for each nudge. Like “create API key” is a backend event, not just clicking the button.

[07:13] Marissa Cole: Yes, completion should be backend-confirmed where possible.

[07:19] Logan Wright: On dashboarding, I can build funnel with breakdowns: cohort, region, acquisition source, and experiment bucket. But we need consistent bucket assignment.

[07:30] Dylan Brooks: Feature flag service can assign a bucket at signup time. For email, we can store it in user profile. For console, same bucket ID.

[07:39] Ben Carter: And include a holdout group. I want 10% holdout for each track.

[07:45] Tyler Benson: Email holdout means no onboarding emails at all? That’s… bold.

[07:49] Camila Reyes: For two weeks maybe, then we can ramp down holdout if it’s clearly beneficial.

[07:55] Ben Carter: Yeah, we can do limited holdout window.

[08:00] Allison Grant: For integrity, don’t change three things at once for the same cohort if you can help it. Or at least tag exposures separately.

[08:09] Ben Carter: Exactly—exposure events. “email_sent”, “email_clicked”, “nudge_viewed”, “nudge_dismissed”.

[08:18] Paula Mendes: And dedupe for nudge viewed—if the component re-renders it’ll fire again. We need to throttle.

[08:26] Dylan Brooks: We can persist a local flag and a server-side “impression count”. But yeah, these events get noisy.

[08:35] Ben Carter: Retargeting. Audience definitions: (1) site visitors who viewed docs/pricing but no signup, (2) signup but no activation, (3) activated but no return.

[08:49] Jordan Blake: For (2) and (3), absolutely exclude any domain with an open opp in HubSpot. Also exclude a list of “do not target” accounts.

[08:59] Ben Carter: Understood. We’ll have an exclusion job that refreshes weekly.

[09:04] Tyler Benson: And UTMs. We need consistent naming so attribution doesn’t become a mess.

[09:09] Ben Carter: Yes. Something like utm_source=linkedin, utm_medium=paid_social, utm_campaign=plg_retargeting_q1, utm_content=variantA.

[09:20] Soojin Lee: Landing pages. Are we sending to docs quickstart or to console sign-in?

[09:26] Ben Carter: For no-signup audience, quickstart. For signup-no-activation, send to “run your first request” page or console getting started.

[09:36] Marissa Cole: If we don’t have a good console getting-started page yet, please don’t send paid traffic to a half-baked page.

[09:43] Ben Carter: Fair. We can use the updated quickstart page.

[09:47] Camila Reyes: Spend cap? We need guardrails.

[09:50] Ben Carter: We’ll propose a conservative cap, monitor CPA and downstream activation. Not trying to scale, trying to learn.

[10:00] Logan Wright: For retargeting performance, we should measure not just click-through but activation lift for those users vs similar cohort. We can do geo or time-based holdout if platform holdout is hard.

[10:14] Tyler Benson: Attribution windows. If they click an ad and activate 5 days later, does it count? We need a standard window.

[10:21] Ben Carter: Let’s do 7-day view, 7-day click window for reporting, but primary KPI is still actual activation regardless of channel.

[10:31] Michael Grant: Please be careful with “site visitors” audiences in EU. Consent banners matter.

[10:36] Olga Petrov: Yes, and we should log consent status and only build audiences where we have consent.

[10:42] Ben Carter: Ok. Guardrails section. Email: complaint/bounce thresholds, unsubscribe compliance, suppression rules.

[10:51] Tyler Benson: Suppress anyone who hits first-success from further “do first request” emails. And suppress support@ type addresses. And if someone unsubscribes, they’re out of all lifecycle sends.

[11:05] Soojin Lee: Also tone. No guilt-trips. More “here’s the exact snippet” and “reply if stuck”.

[11:12] Camila Reyes: Console: avoid blocking flows. Nudges must be dismissible.

[11:18] Marissa Cole: Yeah, a nudge should never prevent API key creation. It’s guidance, not a gate.

[11:24] Dylan Brooks: We’ll ensure dismissal persistence. Though I’ve seen issues where it comes back after refresh. We’ll test.

[11:34] Allison Grant: Please add monitoring for errors in the API key creation route after you ship the nudge.

[11:40] Ben Carter: Noted.

[11:42] Ben Carter: Experiment design. Week 1 deliverables: KPI definitions + event spec + email copy + launch A/B.

[11:52] Paula Mendes: And confirm that the “first request” event is truly server-confirmed success, not just client attempted.

[11:58] Dylan Brooks: We can use API gateway logs to confirm 2xx with model response. But there are edge cases when response is empty.

[12:07] Paula Mendes: That’s still success. We can define success as request completed without error.

[12:13] Logan Wright: For TTFT, do we measure end-to-end or just model time?

[12:17] Ben Carter: For onboarding, end-to-end, but we might also track model latency. Primary is time-to-first-token at client.

[12:26] Paula Mendes: That requires client instrumentation. We don’t have it universally.

[12:31] Dylan Brooks: We can estimate TTFT from server side time-to-first-byte for streaming endpoints, but it’s not exactly the same.

[12:38] Ben Carter: Ok—let’s call it “server TTFT proxy” and be honest.

[12:43] Camila Reyes: This is why we’re calling this sprint “experiments” and not “launches”. Learning matters.

[12:50] Ben Carter: Exactly.

[13:02] (crosstalk)

[13:03] Ben Carter: Let’s do a quick owner map so it’s unambiguous.

[13:07] Ben Carter: Email: me + Tyler + Soojin. Console nudges: Marissa + Dylan + Paula. Measurement/dashboards: Logan + Allison with Paula. Retargeting: me + Tyler on UTMs, Jordan on exclusions, legal review from Michael and Olga.

[13:27] Jordan Blake: On exclusions, I’ll need Isabelle from CRM to help automate. But I can own the rules.

[13:34] Ben Carter: Good. We’ll add her async.

[13:38] Soojin Lee: Copy review process—can we avoid 15 people editing a Google doc?

[13:43] Ben Carter: Yes. Soojin is final copy approver. Others comment, not edit.

[13:49] Camila Reyes: Thank you.

[13:52] Tyler Benson: Deliverability, we should ramp sends. Like 10%, then 25%, then 50% as metrics look good.

[14:00] Ben Carter: Agreed.

[14:02] Allison Grant: And for the dashboard, if there’s a known data discrepancy, we should flag it rather than silently shipping a chart.

[14:09] Logan Wright: I can add a “data quality” panel, like missing events by region, duplicate rate.

[14:15] Paula Mendes: That would be amazing. We have region gaps sometimes.

[14:20] Ben Carter: Ok, timeline. Week 1 email A/B, Week 2 console nudge experiment, Week 3 retargeting, Week 4 consolidated analysis.

[14:30] Marissa Cole: For console, I want a small footprint. One nudge placement max in the first test. Not five.

[14:36] Ben Carter: That’s fair. We can start with “Create API key” and “Run first request” in a checklist style, but maybe one at a time.

[14:45] Dylan Brooks: Technically easier to ship one placement and swap content.

[14:49] Soojin Lee: Also avoid popups. Developers hate popups.

[14:53] Marissa Cole: It’s more like an inline card. Dismissible.

[14:58] Ben Carter: Great.

[15:05] (Discussion continues on specifics; minor transcription gaps)

[16:12] Camila Reyes: Let’s talk risks. Biggest risks I see: deliverability issues, compliance missteps, and measuring the wrong thing.

[16:23] Tyler Benson: Deliverability risk: domain reputation. We should make sure DKIM/SPF/DMARC are clean and monitoring is on.

[16:31] Michael Grant: Also, don’t mix transactional and marketing sends from the same stream without clear labeling.

[16:38] Ben Carter: We’ll keep onboarding in its own category. Tyler will confirm with tooling.

[16:44] Olga Petrov: Data retention. If we’re creating audiences based on behavioral events, define retention and access control.

[16:52] Ben Carter: We’ll document retention and who has access.

[16:56] Jordan Blake: Sales risk: retargeting a customer who’s in a sensitive negotiation. It can blow up trust.

[17:03] Ben Carter: Understood. Exclusions are hard requirements.

[17:09] Allison Grant: Support load risk. If emails drive people to run code and they hit errors, support might spike.

[17:16] Marissa Cole: We should add a “getting started troubleshooting” link. Maybe the SDK error improvements help.

[17:22] Ben Carter: Yes. We’ll coordinate with DevEx docs.

[17:26] Soojin Lee: Another risk is overpromising latency. We should keep claims grounded: “predictable performance” not “fastest on earth”.

[17:34] Camila Reyes: Yeah, no benchmark chest-thumping in onboarding.

[17:39] Ben Carter: Cool.

[18:02] Ben Carter: I’m going to jump to decision points. One: activation definition includes first successful request plus 100k tokens in 7 days. Everyone ok?

[18:12] (multiple voices): Yeah / works / ok.

[18:15] Ben Carter: Two: holdout groups. 10% holdout by track. Email holdout is time-boxed.

[18:23] Tyler Benson: I’m okay if it’s time-boxed.

[18:26] Ben Carter: Three: retargeting audiences must exclude active opps and enterprise domains list.

[18:33] Jordan Blake: Yes.

[18:35] Ben Carter: Four: compliance review required before retargeting launch.

[18:40] Michael Grant: Yes.

[18:41] Olga Petrov: Yes.

[18:45] Ben Carter: Great.

[19:10] Logan Wright: Small thing—if we’re measuring “first 100k tokens”, new users might not hit it if they’re just testing. So the metric can be too strict.

[19:18] Ben Carter: That’s why it’s a milestone. Activation is first request, and the 100k is the “meaningful usage” milestone.

[19:26] Camila Reyes: We can report both: first request in 7 days, and 100k in 7 days.

[19:31] Ben Carter: Yes, we’ll report both.

[20:04] Marissa Cole: For console, I want to avoid nudging streaming as a step unless they’re using chat. Embeddings users don’t care.

[20:12] Paula Mendes: We can segment by endpoint used. Like if they created an embeddings key vs chat.

[20:17] Dylan Brooks: Might be hard. But we can segment by first API call type.

[20:23] Ben Carter: Let’s start simple: nudge for first request is universal.

[20:28] Marissa Cole: Agreed.

[21:02] Tyler Benson: On email triggers, we need to make sure “signup” event is accurate. If there are duplicates, someone might get two welcome emails.

[21:09] Dylan Brooks: Yeah, that’s the earlier point. We’ll prioritize the fix.

[21:14] Allison Grant: Please don’t ship until that’s fixed.

[21:17] Ben Carter: We’ll gate launch on a quick validation.

[22:03] Ben Carter: Ok, I think we’re good. Last five minutes: comms and cadence. Weekly readout meeting, same time next week.

[22:12] Camila Reyes: And a short Slack update in #marketing and #product-growth after each launch.

[22:18] Ben Carter: Yep. And we’ll use the readout template: hypothesis, setup, results, decision, follow-ups.

[22:28] Soojin Lee: Please include screenshots of the email and the nudge so people stop asking “what did we send”.

[22:34] Ben Carter: Done.

[23:02] Ben Carter: Recap next steps: I’ll send a link hub today. Paula to finalize event spec. Logan to draft dashboard spec. Tyler to finalize send schedule and deliverability ramp. Soojin to approve messaging. Marissa to pick first nudge and completion definition. Dylan to confirm feature flags and holdout.

[23:22] Ben Carter: Jordan to send exclusions rules and list. Michael and Olga to do compliance review for retargeting.

[23:31] Jordan Blake: I’ll do it by Friday.

[23:33] Ben Carter: Great. Thanks all.

[23:36] Camila Reyes: Thanks everyone.

(Recording continues with informal chat and calendar scheduling; transcript ends.)

title: Lifecycle growth experiments sprint kickoff (Hosted API activation/retention)
preview: Kickoff for 4-week lifecycle growth experiments sprint focused on self-serve Hosted API activation + early retention. Team aligned on primary activation KPIs (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), experiment tracks (email drip v2, in-console nudges, paid retargeting), instrumentation/event taxonomy, and guardrails (deliverability, CAN-SPAM/GDPR, sales opp exclusions, and experiment integrity). Owners confirmed and week-by-week deliverables reviewed.
