source.title: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
source.summary: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, observability, runbooks, game days) and out-of-scope items (multi-cloud migration, serving runtime rewrite, SLA language). Open questions focused on trigger hierarchy (authoritative signals vs corroboration), anti-flap behavior, approval model (auto vs ack-required), and how to safely run controlled production failovers with limited blast radius.
topics: Current state + reliability goals (reduce detection + failover time, prevent cascading overload)
Signal hierarchy: reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn, capacity exhaustion, control plane health
Region Health Service: inputs, TTL/jitter, failure modes, API contract to routing
Failover controller: dry-run mode, audit events, approvals, safe per-tenant limits
Rollback semantics: hold-down timers, anti-oscillation, rate limits, when to revert vs stay failed over
Operational readiness: dashboards, alert thresholds, annotations, incident-bot/status comms
Game day planning: scenarios, success criteria, rollback triggers, go/no-go checklist
action_items: Miles Thornton: draft first cut of trigger hierarchy + corroboration rules (reachability vs error burn vs latency burn) for review in architecture channel (due: 2026-01-12)
Allison Grant: propose dashboard panels + required annotations for game day (reachability, burn, capacity, policy flips) and identify gaps in current metrics (due: 2026-01-13)
Ravi Menon + Mina Haddad: confirm deterministic evaluation semantics needed in policy engine (tie-breaking, dampening windows, confidence score fields) (due: 2026-01-14)
Victor Alvarez: confirm network/reachability probes approach + provider caveats (DNS TTL, anycast behavior) and what’s safe for production test (due: 2026-01-14)
Vanessa Ortiz: provide capacity headroom snapshot for us-east and eu-west and define “safe to failover” threshold targets for Hosted vs Dedicated (due: 2026-01-13)
Benji Okafor: outline guardrails for automation (max flips/hour, per-tenant blast radius, approvals) and propose default settings for initial rollout (due: 2026-01-14)
Peter Holtz: review gateway retry/timeouts + circuit breaker behavior during failover to avoid thundering herd; propose changes needed before production drill (due: 2026-01-15)
Tomáš Novák: inventory existing runbooks and list top mismatches with desired automation flow (enable/disable, rollback, comms) (due: 2026-01-12)
Devon Price: define incident-bot hook requirements (summary format, links to dashboards, audit trail fields) (due: 2026-01-13)
Talia Benson: schedule design review + set target date for staging rehearsal and production go/no-go review (due: 2026-01-09)
next_steps: Architecture review of trigger hierarchy + rollback rules early next week (goal: converge on ADR draft).
Stand up initial Region Health Service contract (even if stubbed) so routing/policy work can integrate early.
Finalize game day scenario + success criteria and confirm go/no-go checklist owners.
source.transcript:
Meeting: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
Date: 2026-01-08
Time: 10:03am PT
Duration: 57 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees (Redwood): Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Miles Thornton, Mina Haddad, Ravi Menon, Ethan Park, Allison Grant, Victor Alvarez, Vanessa Ortiz, Benji Okafor, Peter Holtz, Tomáš Novák, Devon Price, Talia Benson

---

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’re at time. This is the kickoff for the regional failover automation upgrade. We’re recording, just as a heads up. Goal today is scope alignment, owners, timeline, and to capture the open design questions we need to resolve quickly.

[00:18] Sean Gallagher: Thanks, Talia. This is part of the Reliability Program push this half. The headline is: region failover is currently a bit… artisanal. A lot of it is manual, and some of it is automated but noisy and hard to trust.

[00:33] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, “artisanal” is generous. We have routing policies that can shift, but the triggers aren’t consistent, and there’s not a single health view we trust. On-call ends up correlating five dashboards and Slack rumors.

[00:48] Mina Haddad: From routing side, we basically have three different paths depending on Hosted vs Dedicated, and depending on which gateway path you’re on. That’s where it gets confusing and risky.

[01:02] Miles Thornton: And the big thing is the trigger pipeline itself. It’s mixing signals that should be authoritative with signals that are like… secondary. We want something deterministic and explainable.

[01:16] Talia Benson: Great. Let’s start with scope. Sean, do you want to restate goals and what’s in vs out?

[01:23] Sean Gallagher: Sure. Goals: reduce customer-visible impact during regional impairment. That means faster detection and faster, safer failover. Also prevent cascading overload when we swing traffic. Second, standardize trigger hierarchy and rollback behavior so we don’t oscillate. Third, prove it with controlled failover tests, game days. Fourth, improve observability so we can tell “region is down” vs “one service is having a bad day.”

[01:50] Sean Gallagher: In-scope: Hosted API and Dedicated multi-region routing + policy evaluation. Region health aggregation service. Automation controller. Circuit breakers and load shedding during failover. Dashboards, alerts, runbooks. Incident-bot and status comms support. Controlled tests in staging and prod with limited blast radius.

[02:12] Sean Gallagher: Out-of-scope: multi-cloud migration, rewriting the serving runtime. Also not changing contract/SLA language here.

[02:21] Ethan Park: Plus, for Private deployments we can provide guidance patterns, but we’re not building bespoke automation per customer. Just to be explicit.

[02:31] Talia Benson: Perfect. Let’s do quick current-state pain points so we’re all aligned.

[02:36] Rafael Mendes: Biggest pain: false positives. We’ve had cases where tail latency in one dependency spiked and it looked like “region is toast,” but failing over actually made it worse because the other region didn’t have headroom.

[02:52] Vanessa Ortiz: And capacity signals today are kind of disconnected. You can see fleet utilization in one place, but it’s not gating failover decisions. We’ve had situations where we’d be “green” from 5xx but actually at 92% GPU and then a failover doubles traffic and you get a cliff.

[03:10] Peter Holtz: The gateway retries exacerbate that. When we do a traffic shift, clients retry, we retry internally, and suddenly the effective QPS is way higher than the incoming. That’s the thundering herd, and it can turn a partial impairment into a full incident.

[03:28] Allison Grant: From observability: we don’t have a single timeline. We don’t annotate policy flips in dashboards consistently. And the metrics for “why did we fail over” are basically… someone posts a message.

[03:43] Devon Price: Incident-bot can help but we need structured events, otherwise it’s just parsing random text. We can post summaries when automation recommends something, and when it executes, but only if we emit a consistent audit event.

[03:58] Talia Benson: Cool. So the workstreams are basically: signals, policy engine/controller, capacity/network integration, and observability/runbooks/game day. Let’s go deeper on signals first.

[04:10] Miles Thornton: The proposal, broadly: authoritative signal is reachability. Like, can we reach the region and get valid responses. Then error burn (5xx) with SLO burn rate. Then tail latency burn. Then capacity exhaustion. There’s a debate whether capacity should be “authoritative” in a different way—like it should prevent failover even if reachability is bad, or it should trigger preemptive shedding.

[04:36] Ravi Menon: On the deterministic evaluation: we need an ordering and also tie-breakers. Like, if reachability is degraded but intermittent, we need corroboration. What is enough? Two of three probes? And what’s the window? If you look at a 30-second reachability blip, we should not flip the world.

[04:55] Victor Alvarez: For reachability, I’m assuming we’re talking about synthetic probes from multiple regions? Because if the control plane is the only one checking, you can get a biased view. And DNS can lie. Like, you can have local resolver caching and think the endpoint is fine.

[05:12] Miles Thornton: Exactly. We want perf canary style probes from outside the target region. Some from within as well, but cross-region is key.

[05:22] Mina Haddad: Also, define “reachability.” Is it just TCP connect? Or is it “HTTP 200 from /healthz with model runtime ready”? Because you can be reachable and still not serve.

[05:36] Ethan Park: Control plane health needs to be included too, maybe as a separate signal. If region control plane is impaired but data plane is serving, we should be careful about changes. I don’t want automation fighting a degraded control plane.

[05:53] Rafael Mendes: Right. There’s also the “do not fail over” scenario. Like if both regions are unhealthy or if we’re in a planned test window. We need explicit guardrails.

[06:06] Benji Okafor: Guardrails bucket: (1) anti-flap: hold-down timers, minimum dwell time before rollback, rate limit on flips. (2) blast radius: per-tenant or per-tier gating. (3) approvals: maybe required on-call ack initially, with an option to go full auto later.

[06:28] Talia Benson: Let’s pin that. Approval model is a big decision. Sean, what’s the expectation from leadership for Q1?

[06:36] Sean Gallagher: For the first production game day, we want a “safe automation” posture: dry-run first, then on-call ack to execute. After we have evidence and tuning, we can talk about removing approvals for some conditions.

[06:54] Tomáš Novák: That needs to be in runbooks. On-call needs to know: when do I trust the recommendation and click approve, vs when do I dig deeper. Today it’s all tribal.

[07:08] Allison Grant: And we need the evidence attached to the recommendation. If the controller says “fail over us-east,” I need to see which signals fired: reachability probe fail from eu-west, 5xx burn above X, etc. Otherwise it’s a black box.

[07:26] Ravi Menon: +1. The policy engine needs to emit a “decision explanation.” Even a simple list: primary trigger, corroborating triggers, confidence score, and the dampening/hold-down state.

[07:40] Miles Thornton: Yeah, and we need deterministic semantics. No “sometimes it picks latency.” We should be able to replay decisions.

[07:51] Devon Price: Replay means you need recorded inputs. If we have a simulation endpoint that accepts a time range or a blob of signals, we can reproduce what happened in postmortem.

[08:05] Talia Benson: Great. Let’s talk about Region Health Service concept.

[08:10] Ethan Park: The idea: a single service that aggregates health signals per region. Inputs: synthetic reachability, SLO burn, latency percentiles, capacity headroom, maybe control plane indicators. Output: a region health state (Healthy / Degraded / Unhealthy) plus reasons and timestamps. It should have caching with TTL and jitter so it doesn’t stampede during an incident.

[08:36] Miles Thornton: And critically, it needs to degrade gracefully. If one input stream is missing—like probes are down—we shouldn’t crash or assume healthy. We should mark “unknown” and require corroboration.

[08:51] Victor Alvarez: For network symptoms, also define what’s a region impairment vs a path impairment. We’ve had cases where certain ISPs can’t reach us-east but other paths are fine. That’s tricky: do we fail over globally or only for impacted clients?

[09:10] Mina Haddad: We probably can’t do per-ISP today without a bigger routing change. For now, we do region-level decisions with caution. But we should at least surface the asymmetry, so on-call sees “probe failures from X only.”

[09:27] Allison Grant: Observability can handle that if we label probe source region. But we need to be careful with metric cardinality. If we add source, tenant, model, reason all together, Prometheus is going to explode.

[09:42] Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity labels too. Dedicated vs Hosted, tier, region, pool type. It’s a lot.

[09:50] Allison Grant: Yeah. So we should pre-aggregate to a small set of dimensions: region, product_surface (Hosted/Dedicated), and a bounded “reason enum.” Avoid free-form.

[10:05] Rafael Mendes: Let’s capture that as a requirement: bounded reason codes for metrics and audit logs.

[10:12] Talia Benson: Moving to controller behavior. Benji, can you outline what you’re thinking for safe rollout?

[10:20] Benji Okafor: Sure. Controller runs every N seconds, reads Region Health and policy configs, computes recommended action. Modes: (a) observe-only/dry-run (write audit event, no changes), (b) ack-required (pause until on-call approves), (c) fully automatic for certain routes. Rate limiting: max flips per hour per tenant and per region. Hold-down timer: once we fail over, we stay failed over for at least X minutes unless there’s a “hard recovery” signal. Rollback conditions need to be explicit.

[10:54] Ravi Menon: For rollback, we should avoid bouncing if the region goes “healthy” for 30 seconds and then back to “unhealthy.” That’s where dampening windows come in.

[11:06] Miles Thornton: I’d like an ADR on rollback semantics. There’s a classic approach: “fail fast, recover slow.” Aggressive to fail over when reachability is bad, conservative to roll back.

[11:20] Peter Holtz: Also: during failover, we need to enforce retry budgets. Otherwise we might “successfully fail over” but the clients keep retrying the old region endpoint and we see a spike anyway.

[11:34] Mina Haddad: Depending on how traffic steering works (DNS vs policy routing), some clients will stick. DNS TTL changes help but aren’t instant. That’s a big caveat for expectations.

[11:49] Victor Alvarez: Exactly. Some resolvers ignore TTL. And anycast can have weird convergence. So “failover time” should be defined as “routing policy changed” vs “client traffic shifted.” We need to measure both.

[12:08] Sean Gallagher: Good point. For SLO purposes, customer impact matters, so we should track “time until error rates normalize” not just “policy flip time.”

[12:21] Talia Benson: Let’s shift to game day planning since it’s a forcing function. Rafael, what’s the target timeframe?

[12:29] Rafael Mendes: Tentatively mid-January for a controlled production test, but only if we hit readiness. Otherwise staging rehearsal first. We need a go/no-go checklist.

[12:41] Tomáš Novák: For go/no-go: runbooks updated, dashboards in place, alert noise tuned, and clear rollback authority. Last thing we want is ambiguity during the test.

[12:55] Devon Price: And comms plan: internal only? status page? Customer support needs a heads up, at least so they can answer tickets.

[13:07] Sean Gallagher: For the first one, we’ll do limited blast radius. Potentially only a subset of tenants, maybe internal traffic plus a couple opted-in. We still need support briefed because someone will notice.

[13:23] Mina Haddad: Routing can support a scoped policy based on tenant tier or allowlist. That’s doable.

[13:31] Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity wise, if we’re doing us-east to eu-west, we need to confirm eu-west headroom at the time of test. And also dedicated pools are trickier. We can’t just shove dedicated traffic without pre-drain.

[13:49] Ethan Park: Dedicated needs a drain workflow. I want a step that ensures we’re not breaking data isolation assumptions or crossing residency boundaries.

[14:01] Victor Alvarez: Data residency: some enterprise contracts require staying in-region. If we fail over across geos, we need to respect that. So controller needs tenant constraints.

[14:15] Benji Okafor: That’s part of blast radius constraints. Policy engine should be aware: “this tenant cannot route out of EU,” etc. For now, we can encode it as config.

[14:28] Allison Grant: Observability requirement: we need a panel that shows “traffic shifted by region over time” and “policy flip events” on the same chart. Otherwise debugging is painful.

[14:41] Ravi Menon: Also a chart for “automation recommended vs executed.” In dry-run we should be able to see, did it *want* to fail over but got blocked by guardrails?

[14:55] Miles Thornton: That’s huge for tuning false positives. If dry-run says “fail over 12 times,” we know we have noise.

[15:05] (crosstalk)
[15:05] Peter Holtz: Sorry—one more. Gateway behavior during failover: we should return retry-after on overload. That at least nudges clients to back off.

[15:15] Mina Haddad: That’s not just gateway, also SDK guidance. But for this project, we can standardize the response.

[15:24] Talia Benson: Okay, I’m going to steer us to responsibilities. Quick round: who owns what.

[15:31] Rafael Mendes: SRE owns the overall delivery and on-call readiness. I’ll take that.

[15:37] Miles Thornton: I’ll own architecture for trigger hierarchy and rollback semantics, and help with Region Health design.

[15:44] Mina Haddad: Routing team owns integration into smart routing and making sure policy flips are scoped correctly.

[15:51] Ravi Menon: Policy engine evaluation changes: deterministic ordering, dampening, tie-breakers. I’ll lead that from routing policy side.

[15:59] Ethan Park: Control plane integration: how the controller talks to routing/policy config, and safe execution. My team.

[16:07] Allison Grant: Observability: dashboards, alerting, annotations, metric design. I’ll coordinate with Sergio/Aisha offline.

[16:15] Victor Alvarez: Network reachability probes and DNS/anycast caveats. I’ll drive that and coordinate with infra.

[16:23] Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity signal definition and thresholds, and “safe to failover” criteria per region. I’ll own.

[16:31] Benji Okafor: Guardrails, safe rollout strategy, approvals, rate limits, per-tenant limits. I’ll own.

[16:39] Peter Holtz: Gateway/circuit breakers, timeouts/retry budgets, overload semantics. I’ll own.

[16:46] Tomáš Novák: Runbooks, paging hygiene, and making sure the operational flow is coherent for on-call. I’ll own.

[16:54] Devon Price: Incident-bot and tooling integration for posting summaries and capturing audit trail links. I’ll own.

[17:02] Talia Benson: Great. Now timeline. We have a 10-12 week target window (Q1 to early Q2). Phase 1 is baseline + requirements. That’s basically now through next week. Phase 2 design: ADRs. Phase 3 build: integration. Phase 4 validate: staging drills then controlled prod. Phase 5 rollout/hardening.

[17:28] Sean Gallagher: One thing: we should define success metrics now. “Detection to failover time” target, and “error budget burn reduction” target. Otherwise we can’t prove it’s better.

[17:42] Rafael Mendes: Agree. I’ll pull baseline numbers from the last few incidents. Even if rough: manual time to mitigate, number of false triggers.

[17:55] Allison Grant: For metrics, we should decide measurement windows. Burn rate alerts often use 5m/1h windows. That choice affects noise.

[18:08] Miles Thornton: That ties into trigger hierarchy: reachability should be fast, but 5xx burn might lag. We should explicitly model “fast signals” vs “slow corroboration.”

[18:25] Ravi Menon: If reachability is hard-fail (like 80% probe failure), we can fail quickly. If it’s partial, require 5xx or latency corroboration.

[18:39] Victor Alvarez: We should also account for probe failures due to the probe system itself. If probes are down, we can’t declare a region dead.

[18:50] Ethan Park: That’s where “unknown” health states matter. The controller should treat missing data as “cannot conclude,” not “healthy.”

[19:03] Benji Okafor: But also, don’t block forever. If we have two strong signals and one is missing, allow action.

[19:10] Miles Thornton: Exactly: required corroboration set depends on the primary signal.

[19:16] Talia Benson: Let’s capture the key open questions. I’m going to list them. Interrupt if I miss something.

[19:23] Talia Benson: 1) What is the exact trigger hierarchy and what requires corroboration?

[19:29] Miles Thornton: Plus dampening windows. Like, how long does a signal need to be bad.

[19:33] Talia Benson: 2) What are the rollback semantics—fail fast, recover slow—what timers and thresholds.

[19:40] Ravi Menon: And rate limits. Max flips per hour.

[19:44] Talia Benson: 3) Approval model: ack-required in prod initially, criteria for full auto.

[19:51] Sean Gallagher: Yes.

[19:52] Talia Benson: 4) How do we incorporate capacity signals so we don’t fail over into overload?

[19:59] Vanessa Ortiz: And what is “headroom” definition. Tokens/sec vs GPU utilization vs queue time.

[20:05] Talia Benson: 5) How do we measure failover success given DNS/anycast limitations.

[20:11] Victor Alvarez: Right.

[20:13] Talia Benson: 6) Metrics and audit events: what fields, bounded labels, and how to annotate dashboards.

[20:20] Allison Grant: Include “reason enum” and “confidence” but bounded.

[20:24] Talia Benson: 7) Dedicated constraints: drain workflow, isolation/residency boundaries.

[20:30] Ethan Park: Yep.

[20:32] Talia Benson: Cool.

[20:34] (brief pause)

[20:36] Sean Gallagher: One more: communications. For game days and real events. We need an internal playbook and status page template. Not writing all of it today, but hook points.

[20:49] Devon Price: If controller emits an audit event, incident-bot can post a short message in #incidents and #eng-sre, maybe include: “recommended action: failover us-east -> eu-west, primary trigger reachability, corroboration 5xx burn, blocked by approval.”

[21:09] Rafael Mendes: That would help on-call so much.

[21:12] Talia Benson: Great. Let’s talk briefly about “dry-run mode” definition so engineering has a crisp acceptance criteria.

[21:20] Ravi Menon: Dry-run should compute exactly the same decisions as real mode, but it must not execute policy flips. It should emit a structured event with the decision and evidence.

[21:32] Miles Thornton: And it should include “what would have happened” including rollback. Otherwise you only see half the story.

[21:41] Benji Okafor: Also, dry-run should show which guardrail would block execution. Like, “blocked: would exceed max flips per hour.”

[21:53] Allison Grant: From dashboard perspective, we can chart dry-run recommended flips vs executed flips.

[22:00] Talia Benson: Okay. Since we have 30-ish minutes left, I want to make sure we leave with immediate tasks and meeting cadence.

[22:08] Talia Benson: Proposal: twice-weekly standup for this workstream, plus one architecture review next week for triggers/rollback. Game day prep meeting once we have the scenario.

[22:20] Rafael Mendes: Works for me.

[22:22] Mina Haddad: Same.

[22:24] Sean Gallagher: +1.

[22:26] Talia Benson: Any risks we should flag now?

[22:31] Victor Alvarez: DNS TTL changes are risky. Some clients ignore TTL. Also, lowering TTL can increase DNS query volume and we need to ensure our resolver setup is fine. We should do it gradually.

[22:48] Peter Holtz: Gateway changes can impact latency. If we tighten timeouts too much, we could increase 5xx. Need careful tuning and maybe per-route flags.

[23:01] Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity signal correctness. If we base headroom on average utilization, we’ll miss bursty behavior. We should incorporate queue depth or “admission control” metrics.

[23:14] Allison Grant: Metric cardinality, as mentioned.

[23:17] Ethan Park: Control plane reliability. If the controller depends on a service that is degraded during incidents, that’s a failure mode. Keep dependencies minimal.

[23:30] Miles Thornton: Also, the biggest risk: we tune for one scenario and break another. That’s why game days plus replay/simulation matters.

[23:43] Talia Benson: Great. I’m going to recap action items now, then we can open floor.

[23:48] Talia Benson: Miles drafts trigger hierarchy + corroboration rules by Monday. Allison drafts dashboard spec and annotations. Ravi and Mina confirm deterministic policy semantics. Victor confirms probe approach and DNS caveats. Vanessa provides capacity headroom snapshot and “safe to failover” thresholds. Benji outlines guardrails and defaults. Peter reviews gateway retry/timeouts and overload semantics. Tomáš inventories runbooks gaps. Devon defines incident-bot hook requirements. And I’ll schedule design review and set target dates.

[24:25] Talia Benson: Anything missing?

[24:28] Tomáš Novák: Can we explicitly assign “go/no-go checklist owner”? That’s usually SRE.

[24:34] Rafael Mendes: I’ll own go/no-go checklist and circulate a template.

[24:38] Talia Benson: Added.

[24:41] (crosstalk)
[24:41] Mina Haddad: Quick one: we should decide the initial blast radius approach early. If we need allowlists, that changes implementation.

[24:51] Ravi Menon: Yeah, we can do per-tenant gating. But we should decide: is it based on tenant ID, tier, or routes/models.

[25:02] Sean Gallagher: For the first game day, let’s do allowlist by tenant + route. We can refine later.

[25:10] Talia Benson: Okay. Rafael, Mina, can you sync offline and propose initial allowlist approach?

[25:16] Rafael Mendes: Yep.

[25:18] Mina Haddad: Yep.

[25:20] Talia Benson: Cool. Open floor for any other questions.

[25:26] Speaker 1 (unattributed): Are we considering “partial impairment” scenarios, like only embeddings endpoints are failing?

[25:33] Miles Thornton: Good question. Ideally yes, but it complicates. We can model health by route class: chat/generate vs embeddings. Region health could have subcomponents.

[25:48] Mina Haddad: The routing layer already has per-route policies, so we can target failover for a subset, but region-level events are simpler. For game day #1, keep it simple: a primary Hosted route.

[26:04] Allison Grant: If we do per-route, dashboards need to filter by route class.

[26:10] Talia Benson: Let’s keep game day #1 focused, but capture per-route health as a follow-up requirement.

[26:18] Peter Holtz: Also streaming. If we redirect a streaming request mid-flight, clients can drop. We need expectations: do we reconnect, do we return an error, how do we make it idempotent.

[26:33] Sean Gallagher: Streaming is a known sharp edge. Not sure we solve fully in this tranche, but we should at least not make it worse.

[26:44] Rafael Mendes: For the game day, we should include a streaming client test so we see the behavior.

[26:49] Mina Haddad: Agreed.

[26:52] Talia Benson: Noted.

[26:55] Victor Alvarez: One more: cloud provider status signals. Do we ingest them? They can lag, but they’re useful context.

[27:05] Miles Thornton: I’d treat provider status as informational, not a trigger.

[27:10] Sean Gallagher: Agree. Not authoritative.

[27:14] Allison Grant: We can display it on dashboards as a link.

[27:18] Talia Benson: Great.

[27:20] (pause)

[27:22] Ethan Park: Clarifying the control plane role: do we expect the controller to directly flip routing policies, or does it request a change via a workflow service with approvals?

[27:35] Benji Okafor: I prefer workflow with audit trail. Even if it’s internal. That gives us a place to enforce rate limits and approvals.

[27:44] Ravi Menon: And log the “who/what/why.”

[27:48] Devon Price: That’s also what incident-bot can link to.

[27:51] Sean Gallagher: We’ll do it with workflow. Safety first.

[27:56] Talia Benson: Okay.

[28:00] Tomáš Novák: Can I request we add a very explicit “kill switch” in the runbook. Like, if automation is wrong, on-call can disable it fast.

[28:09] Rafael Mendes: Yes. Feature flag + manual override documented.

[28:14] Mina Haddad: We can expose a “disable automation for region” switch.

[28:18] Talia Benson: Good.

[28:21] (crosstalk)

[28:22] Sean Gallagher: Alright, if there’s nothing else, we can end early. But I think we should keep a few minutes for questions.

[28:31] Speaker 2 (unattributed): What’s the definition of “region-level event” vs “partial degradation” for SLO reporting?

[28:39] Sean Gallagher: That’s part of the SLO doc workstream, but for this project: region-level event is when the majority of traffic in a region cannot be served within SLO (availability/latency). Partial degradation could be a specific route or a dependent service. We’ll define it more formally.

[28:56] Allison Grant: From measurement: we’ll need a multi-region availability SLO definition. Like, if one region is down but traffic shifts, the overall service might still meet SLO. But customers can still see blips.

[29:10] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. That’s why we track failover time and the transient error window.

[29:17] Talia Benson: Great. I’ll capture that as a requirement for the SLO owners.

[29:24] Talia Benson: Okay, I’m going to end the recording at 30 minutes, but we can stay on for a few more. (Fireflies note: recording continued.)

[29:35] Sean Gallagher: Thanks everyone.

[29:37] Recording continues...

[29:40] (informal discussion)

[29:41] Miles Thornton: Mina, on deterministic evaluation, do we want strict priority order always, or can we do weighted scoring?

[29:49] Mina Haddad: For now strict priority. Weighted scoring becomes impossible to reason about during incidents.

[29:56] Ravi Menon: Agree. Deterministic + explainable.

[30:02] Allison Grant: Please. No ML for this.

[30:05] (laughter)

[30:07] Talia Benson: Alright, we’re good. I’ll send notes and the action items list. Meeting adjourned.

---
Notes/links mentioned (internal):
- Draft trigger hierarchy (Miles)
- Dashboard/alerts spec (Allison)
- Game day go/no-go checklist template (Rafael)
- Incident-bot structured event format (Devon)

title: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
preview: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, observability, runbooks, game days) and out-of-scope items (multi-cloud migration, serving runtime rewrite, SLA language). Open questions focused on trigger hierarchy (authoritative signals vs corroboration), anti-flap behavior, approval model (auto vs ack-required), and how to safely run controlled production failovers with limited blast radius.
