source.title: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
topics: Taxonomy goals: low-cardinality + usable in live incidents
Decision tree: 5xx vs latency vs streaming vs auth vs capacity vs control plane
Ambiguous incidents: overload vs routing vs quota vs client behavior
Owner mapping disputes: Fleet vs Routing; Control Plane vs Support vs Security
Remediation SLA expectations vs severity/tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
Telemetry/observability incidents and how to prevent label explosion
Rules for multi-cause incidents and contributing factors
source.summary: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.
action_items: Mei Lin - Pull 10 recent '429/overload' incidents and propose classification examples (capacity vs quota vs routing) for the taxonomy page - Due 2025-12-06
Mina Haddad - Write one-paragraph definition for 'Routing/Fallback misbehavior' and list the allowed subtypes (policy loop, health signal mismatch, wrong fallback target) - Due 2025-12-05
Vanessa Ortiz - Provide concrete signals/alerts that indicate true capacity shortage vs routing overload (GPU saturation, provisioning lag, regional constraints) - Due 2025-12-05
Allison Grant - Confirm metric label allowlist for incident_type/subtype and propose 'unknown/unclassified' handling to prevent cardinality spikes - Due 2025-12-09
Ethan Park - Update owner-mapping config draft to reflect final primary owners and include 'consulted' teams + runbook link placeholders - Due 2025-12-09
Kira Thompson - Add runbook link mapping for the three most common types (Overload, Auth/RBAC, Routing/Fallback) and send to Dylan for incident-bot integration - Due 2025-12-10
Aisha Bello - Draft initial remediation SLA matrix row for Dedicated Sev1 overload and sanity-check with Product stakeholders (Lauren Bishop) - Due 2025-12-11
Fiona McCarthy - Add a short 'handoff trigger' note for suspicious auth failures (dual-track security vs reliability) - Due 2025-12-11
Dylan Brooks - Propose incident-bot UX wording for 'primary incident type' vs 'contributing factors' fields - Due 2025-12-12
next_steps: Rafael to circulate updated taxonomy + owner mapping draft for async signoff (SRE/Platform/Infra/Runtime).
Integrate final owner mapping into control plane config so incident-bot can resolve owners automatically.
Update decision tree with 3 example walkthroughs (capacity event, control plane outage, streaming disconnect spike).
Schedule a 30-min follow-up to review remediation SLA matrix deltas for Dedicated/Private.
source.transcript:
Meeting: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
Recorded by Fireflies.ai

Date: 2025-12-03
Time: 4:00 PM - 4:58 PM PT
Duration: 58 minutes

Attendees:
- Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Allison Grant (Observability)
- Mei Lin (SRE, data)
- Mina Haddad (Smart Routing)
- Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Capacity)
- Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane)
- Sofia Ivanova (Serving Runtime)
- Aisha Bello (Dashboards/SLO)
- Dylan Brooks (Tooling integration)
- Kira Thompson (Runbooks)
- Fiona McCarthy (Security Ops)

Auto-summary (generated):
- Goal: lock incident taxonomy v1 edge cases + ownership mapping for disputed categories.
- Key decisions: Keep taxonomy small; capacity shortage -> Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior -> Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement -> Control Plane primary.
- Risks: metric label cardinality; confusion between client-side streaming issues and gateway/runtime issues.


[00:00] Rafael Mendes: Alright, I think we’re recording. Um, thanks everyone. The goal today is not to rewrite the whole taxonomy. It’s to do the annoying part, which is the tricky incidents and the ownership disputes so we can ship v1 without like, fifteen “it depends” footnotes.

[00:18] Rafael Mendes: Quick reminder, constraints: low-cardinality, stable. If we can’t explain it in thirty seconds on-call, it’s not a v1 category. Okay.

[00:30] Mei Lin: I can share the spreadsheet view if you want, but I pulled the top clusters from the last two quarters. The big ambiguous bucket is still overload, like 429s, 5xx, and it’s split between “true capacity” and “routing got weird” and “quota.”

[00:46] Allison Grant: And the fourth one is “we didn’t have metrics” which is my least favorite category but it’s real.

[00:52] Rafael Mendes: Yup. Let’s start with overload because that’s where we keep fighting. Mina, Vanessa, I’ll put you both on the spot.

[01:02] Mina Haddad: Love that. Okay, the problem is we get paged for elevated 5xx and then people say “routing did it” because the router is the thing that’s visible. But half the time it’s just no GPUs.

[01:18] Vanessa Ortiz: Exactly. If the fleet is saturated, routing is doing the correct thing, it’s just making the best of a bad situation. So in taxonomy terms I think the primary type should be “Capacity/Fleet shortage” when the mitigation is capacity actions.

[01:33] Rafael Mendes: So propose a rule: pick the primary incident type based on the primary mitigation lever.

[01:39] Mina Haddad: That works if we’re honest about what we did. If the mitigation was “flip routing policy to a less expensive model” or “disable fallback,” then it’s routing.

[01:49] Vanessa Ortiz: Yep. If the mitigation was “add capacity, move load, unblock provisioning, increase reserved pool,” then it’s fleet.

[01:58] Rafael Mendes: Great. So we capture that as: Capacity shortage vs Routing/fallback misbehavior are different types. But overload symptoms appear in both.

[02:10] Sofia Ivanova: Can I add runtime? Because there’s also overload-like symptoms when the scheduler is stalling or KV cache thrash is causing timeouts. It looks like capacity but it’s actually runtime regression.

[02:21] Rafael Mendes: Good call. So then the decision tree should ask: are GPUs saturated and healthy, but throughput dropped? That might be runtime.

[02:30] Allison Grant: The decision tree needs to not be “go check 17 dashboards.” It should be like three questions with links.

[02:37] Kira Thompson: If we’re mapping runbooks, we can put “check GPU saturation panel” and “check router fallback reason counters.” Like two panels.

[02:46] Dylan Brooks: For incident-bot auto-suggest, we can look at which alert fired. If the alert is “fleet_provisioning_lag” then suggest capacity shortage. If it’s “router_fallback_reason=policy_loop” then suggest routing misbehavior.

[03:00] Rafael Mendes: Okay. Let’s land the owner mapping first. For top-level “Capacity/Fleet shortage,” primary owner Fleet. Secondary consulted Routing. Agree?

[03:10] Mina Haddad: Yes, as long as there’s a subtype that is clearly routing. Like “fallback policy loop” should not get assigned to Fleet.

[03:18] Vanessa Ortiz: Totally.

[03:21] Rafael Mendes: For “Routing/Fallback misbehavior,” primary Smart Routing, secondary Fleet, consult Control Plane if policy/config writes involved.

[03:30] Ethan Park: Thank you, yes. Because a lot of the routing policies are stored in control plane and if writes fail people blame routing, but it’s actually control plane.

[03:41] Rafael Mendes: Great, which leads to the second disputed bucket: control plane outages and config issues.

[03:49] Ethan Park: I think top-level type “Control plane outage/degradation” should exist. Subtypes could be “config write failures,” “policy evaluation errors,” “control plane authz failure,” and “regional control plane dependency down.”

[04:05] Allison Grant: Please no region as a label. Keep it out of the taxonomy label set.

[04:09] Ethan Park: Yes, region is not part of the type. That’s just metadata.

[04:14] Rafael Mendes: Owner for “Control plane outage/degradation” is Platform Control Plane. Secondary SRE. Consult Routing when it manifests as routing changes not taking effect.

[04:27] Mina Haddad: Yes, because we can validate whether the router is reading stale policy or if the policy service is dead.

[04:33] Mei Lin: The support ticket SUP-20106 is basically that: customer couldn’t update routing policy due to control plane errors.

[04:41] Rafael Mendes: Good, add that as an example in the definitions.

[04:45] Aisha Bello: On SLAs: control plane outages are weird because customers care differently. Hosted customers mostly care about inference, not policy writes, unless it blocks mitigation.

[04:56] Rafael Mendes: We’ll handle SLA matrix after owners. But yes, SLA can be different by tier.

[05:03] Fiona McCarthy: Also, control plane incidents sometimes overlap with security if it’s authz or token validation failures. We need a boundary.

[05:12] Rafael Mendes: That’s actually our third bucket: Auth/RBAC failures.

[05:19] Fiona McCarthy: My proposal: reliability taxonomy has “Auth/RBAC failure” for availability impact from auth services, misconfigured RBAC, expired certs, SSO/SAML integration issues, that kind of thing. If there’s suspicious behavior, credential stuffing, weird patterns, then we dual-track as security incident. But we don’t block reliability mitigation.

[05:38] Dylan Brooks: For incident-bot we can have a checkbox like “security review required?” But that’s maybe v1.1.

[05:45] Rafael Mendes: For v1, we’ll document handoff triggers. Owner mapping: Auth/RBAC failures primary Security Ops or Platform?

[05:55] Fiona McCarthy: It depends on the auth system. If it’s our internal token service or RBAC config, that’s Platform Security or Control Plane.

[06:03] Ethan Park: For RBAC in control plane, it’s my team. For corporate SSO, maybe IT, but that’s not in scope.

[06:09] Rafael Mendes: Okay, we’ll set primary “Security Ops” as consulted/triage, but the owning team depends on component. That violates “single primary owner” though.

[06:20] Allison Grant: Taxonomy wants a single primary owner per type. But maybe type is more specific: “Auth service outage” vs “RBAC config regression.”

[06:28] Fiona McCarthy: I can live with: “AuthN/AuthZ outage (customer-facing)” primary Control Plane, consulted Security Ops. Then Security has separate security incident category if needed.

[06:39] Rafael Mendes: Great. So keep security aligned but not the primary for reliability categories. We’ll put a big note: “If suspicious, page Security Ops and open sec incident.”

[06:53] Rafael Mendes: Next: Streaming disconnects. This one keeps bouncing between Edge/Gateway, Runtime, and “client.”

[07:02] Sofia Ivanova: From runtime side, we see streaming disconnect as “client canceled” a lot. It’s not always client; sometimes our gateway resets connections. But the metric names are confusing.

[07:13] Allison Grant: They are. Half the logs say “context canceled” whether it’s client or we did it.

[07:18] Kira Thompson: We should make the taxonomy definition be symptom-based but with checks: if disconnects spike after SDK upgrade, likely client. If disconnects correlate with gateway deploy or load balancer issues, likely edge.

[07:31] Dylan Brooks: The bot can ask: “Did this start after SDK release?” and “Are disconnects concentrated by user agent?” That’s data we can pull.

[07:40] Mei Lin: SUP-18703 is that exact thing: streaming disconnects after SDK upgrade. Support was mad because we initially called it “streaming infra issue.”

[07:51] Rafael Mendes: So taxonomy type “Streaming/connection instability.” Subtypes: “gateway resets,” “client/SDK regression,” “upstream network/VPC,” and maybe “backpressure/timeouts.” Owner mapping: Primary Edge/Gateway team—do we have that as Platform?

[08:07] Ethan Park: Gateway is Platform Edge, which is under Platform but different oncall. For the project we can say “Platform Edge/Gateway” as the owning team.

[08:14] Rafael Mendes: We didn’t list a person but that’s fine as team. Still, our docs need to point to an oncall rotation.

[08:21] Mina Haddad: But sometimes routing causes disconnects when we flip models mid-stream. Like fallback changes after token 50.

[08:28] Sofia Ivanova: Also runtime can OOM and kill the worker and the stream ends. Looks like disconnect.

[08:34] Rafael Mendes: Multi-cause rule again: pick the primary mitigation. If we mitigated by rolling back gateway, it’s streaming/gateway. If we mitigated by disabling mid-stream fallback, it’s routing. If we mitigated by rolling back runtime build, it’s runtime regression.

[08:50] Allison Grant: This is the theme. Put it explicitly in the policy.

[08:54] Rafael Mendes: Done.

[08:56] Rafael Mendes: Next disputed: Quota enforcement vs capacity saturation vs “customer bursting.”

[09:03] Vanessa Ortiz: Dedicated burst tickets are always a mess. Customer sees 429, says it’s our fault. Sometimes it’s their configured quota.

[09:11] Ethan Park: Quota and rate limiting are control plane, specifically the quota service and enforcement at gateway. That should be a subtype under “Traffic management / rate limiting.”

[09:21] Rafael Mendes: Do we want a top-level type “Rate limiting / quota enforcement” or fold it into overload?

[09:28] Allison Grant: If we fold, it makes the label ambiguous. Also “429” is not always overload, it’s often intentional.

[09:35] Mina Haddad: Right, and routing sometimes returns 429 when it can’t place requests. But the code path is different.

[09:42] Rafael Mendes: So top-level: “Quota/Rate limiting enforcement.” Owner: Control Plane primary. Secondary: Support consulted because of customer comms. Fleet consulted if it’s misconfigured capacity that triggers more throttling.

[09:58] Dev? (unlabeled speaker): Sorry, can I jump in—

[10:00] Rafael Mendes: Go ahead.

[10:01] Speaker 1: This is Dylan. For bot UX we should keep the label names simple. Like “Rate limiting / quota” and then subtypes “customer quota,” “platform protection,” “bug.” But “platform protection” is like… what does that mean?

[10:15] Ethan Park: It means our global protection rules, but yeah it’s vague.

[10:19] Rafael Mendes: We’ll name it “protective throttling” maybe. But we need crisp definitions.

[10:24] Mei Lin: In the backfill sheet I labeled a bunch as “quota enforcement - customer-configured” versus “quota enforcement - unexpected.” That worked.

[10:32] Rafael Mendes: Great. Use that.

[10:35] Rafael Mendes: Okay, runtime regressions. Sofia, do we need subtypes or keep it one?

[10:42] Sofia Ivanova: If we keep it one, it becomes a dumpster fire. The oncall needs at least: “OOM / memory regression,” “scheduler stall,” “KV cache thrash / fragmentation,” and “model-specific regression (new weights, quant).”

[10:59] Allison Grant: Subtypes are fine as long as it’s bounded. Like 4-6.

[11:04] Rafael Mendes: Owner for runtime regression is Serving Runtime. Secondary: Fleet if it’s tied to specific GPU type. Consult Routing if it’s triggered by fallback causing pathological sequence lengths.

[11:17] Mina Haddad: That last part is real. Fallback to a model with different max context can create load.

[11:22] Sofia Ivanova: Yeah, and then runtime gets blamed for “latency.” It’s not wrong but it’s not the primary cause.

[11:29] Rafael Mendes: Again: primary type based on mitigation. If runtime rollback fixed it, runtime regression.

[11:35] Rafael Mendes: Next: Observability/telemetry blind spots.

[11:40] Allison Grant: Please keep it as a real type. Because we have incidents where SLO alerts go off but we have no per-route breakdown because cardinality got nuked, or metrics pipeline down. That materially harms MTTR.

[11:53] Rafael Mendes: Agree. Type: “Telemetry/observability impairment.” Subtypes: “metrics pipeline outage,” “logging pipeline outage,” “dashboard broken/permissions,” “cardinality / sampling misconfig.” Owner: Observability.

[12:09] Aisha Bello: For dashboard perms, there’s overlap with Internal Support. But owning team is still Observability, we can consult IT if needed.

[12:18] Allison Grant: Yes, but please don’t put “permissions” as a subtype if it explodes. Just “access/permissions issue.”

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Okay.

[12:26] Rafael Mendes: Let’s do one of the real ambiguous examples. Mei, pick one.

[12:31] Mei Lin: The region failover one: intermittent 500s during region failover, elevated latency. Support ticket says they saw both 500 and timeouts. Incident channel debated routing fallback misbehavior vs capacity shortage.

[12:46] Mina Haddad: That’s usually routing. During failover, the router tries to shift, but if health signals lag you can get oscillation and send traffic to a region that’s not ready.

[12:57] Vanessa Ortiz: But sometimes the destination region actually has no headroom, so even correct routing fails.

[13:03] Rafael Mendes: What was the mitigation in that incident?

[13:06] Mei Lin: Mitigation was “pin traffic to primary region + disable automatic cross-region fallback” for 30 minutes, and Fleet started provisioning in the secondary region.

[13:17] Mina Haddad: If we disabled fallback, that’s routing misbehavior / policy.

[13:21] Vanessa Ortiz: But capacity work was also in flight.

[13:24] Rafael Mendes: Primary: routing misbehavior, contributing factor: capacity shortage risk. That matches our rule.

[13:31] Allison Grant: Make sure the postmortem has both fields: incident_type and contributing_systems. Otherwise we lose the nuance.

[13:38] Dylan Brooks: Incident-bot modal can have a required “primary incident type” dropdown and optional “contributing factors” multi-select.

[13:45] Rafael Mendes: Good.

[13:47] Rafael Mendes: Next example: latency regression on model rollout.

[13:52] Sofia Ivanova: That’s a mixed one too. If it’s a new model version, it could be runtime kernel selection changed, or quant profile changed.

[14:02] Mina Haddad: Or routing shifted traffic to the new model incorrectly, like too fast.

[14:07] Rafael Mendes: So we need a top-level “Rollout regression” type? Or keep under latency?

[14:13] Aisha Bello: From reporting, it’s useful to separate “regression from change” vs “random degradation.” Also rollout regressions are actionable: slow down canaries.

[14:24] Allison Grant: But adding too many top-level types is risk. Could be a subtype under “Latency degradation.”

[14:31] Rafael Mendes: Proposal: top-level “Performance degradation (latency/throughput)” with a subtype “rollout-related.” Another subtype “runtime regression,” “infra/network,” “capacity-induced.” But that overlaps.

[14:44] Sofia Ivanova: I’d rather keep “Runtime regression” separate from generic latency. But “rollout-related” is a modifier. You can have runtime regression that is rollout-related.

[14:54] Rafael Mendes: Right. Maybe the taxonomy is: primary type is the technical domain (runtime, routing, capacity, control plane, auth, telemetry, network/private connectivity, etc.) and then we store “trigger” as a field: deploy/rollout, cloud outage, customer change.

[15:10] Allison Grant: That’s cleaner for cardinality. Keep trigger as a freeform or controlled low-card field, but not part of incident_type.

[15:17] Dylan Brooks: But our current plan is only incident_type and subtype as required in bot. Adding trigger adds more UX.

[15:24] Rafael Mendes: For v1, we can put rollout-related as an allowed subtype under each domain? That’s messy.

[15:31] Mei Lin: From analysis, “rollout” is a huge dimension. If we don’t capture it we lose a lot.

[15:38] Rafael Mendes: Okay, compromise: add an optional field “suspected trigger” with a small set: “deploy/rollout,” “traffic spike,” “cloud provider,” “customer change,” “unknown.” Not required for closure.

[15:52] Allison Grant: As long as it’s not a metric label.

[15:55] Rafael Mendes: Not a metric label. Just incident record metadata.

[15:59] Aisha Bello: Works.

[16:01] Rafael Mendes: Now, private deployments. Victor isn’t here, but we have to decide categories. The scope includes private connectivity/config issues.

[16:10] Ethan Park: Private incidents are often DNS/TLS/VPC connectivity. That’s networking plus the Private deployments team.

[16:18] Rafael Mendes: Taxonomy type “Private deployment connectivity/config.” Subtypes: “DNS,” “TLS/certs,” “network routing (VPC/peering),” “upgrade/config regression.” Owner: Private Deployments / Networking. Consult Infra.

[16:32] Allison Grant: Again, team naming matters. Make sure it maps to an oncall schedule.

[16:37] Kira Thompson: I can add runbook links for those. There’s already a private upgrade checklist.

[16:42] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[16:44] Rafael Mendes: Let’s talk about escalation rules briefly: if incident type is unknown during live, what do we do?

[16:52] Dylan Brooks: Bot can allow “Unknown / needs triage” but require that it’s updated within, say, 30 minutes, or before resolve.

[17:00] Rafael Mendes: Policy: IC sets initial type within 15 minutes if possible, can set Unknown. Must be updated before closure. If unknown at closure, SRE review required.

[17:12] Allison Grant: And Unknown should be a single bucket, not 20 variants like “unknown-other.”

[17:17] Rafael Mendes: Yup.

[17:19] Rafael Mendes: Okay, back to owner dispute: Fleet vs Routing for overload. We already agreed on primary based on mitigation. But we also need a default mapping for alert-driven pages.

[17:32] Vanessa Ortiz: Default for “GPU saturation high” should page Fleet. Default for “router fallback rate high” pages Routing.

[17:39] Mina Haddad: Except fallback can be high because of capacity. But yes, page Routing if it’s a routing alert.

[17:46] Rafael Mendes: And for “5xx spike” generic, we page SRE and then the bot prompts for classification and owner.

[17:53] Dylan Brooks: Right, that’s the safe path.

[17:56] Rafael Mendes: Allison, can you speak to the metric label allowlist? People keep asking for incident_type as a label everywhere.

[18:05] Allison Grant: Yeah. We cannot put high-cardinality labels on request metrics. Incident_type is okay if it’s an allowlist and only applied at the incident record level, not per-request. If you try to attach incident_type to every request log, you’ll get cardinality blowups and storage bills. The plan should be: incident records have type; dashboards join incidents to metrics by time window and service.

[18:28] Aisha Bello: For the new dashboard, we’re planning to count incidents by type, not label every request. We can annotate time ranges.

[18:36] Allison Grant: Exactly. Use annotations.

[18:39] Rafael Mendes: Great. So the taxonomy dashboard is incident-based, not metric-label based.

[18:44] Dylan Brooks: The PR we have in flight adds incident_type to alert payload schemas, not to metrics.

[18:50] Allison Grant: That’s fine.

[18:52] Rafael Mendes: Cool. Let’s lock the list of top-level types we think are in v1. I’ll read what I have:

[18:59] Rafael Mendes: 1) Availability / elevated 5xx (maybe too generic?)
2) Latency / performance degradation
3) Streaming / connection instability
4) Routing / fallback misbehavior
5) Capacity / fleet shortage
6) Control plane outage / degradation
7) Quota / rate limiting enforcement
8) AuthN/AuthZ / RBAC failure
9) Telemetry / observability impairment
10) Private deployment connectivity/config
11) Data plane dependency outage (like upstream provider?)
12) Tooling/automation failure (incident-bot, paging) ???

[19:29] Rafael Mendes: That’s 12. Too many? Some of these could be merged.

[19:34] Sofia Ivanova: “Availability 5xx” is a symptom, not a cause. It will overlap with routing/capacity/runtime.

[19:41] Mina Haddad: Same with latency.

[19:44] Rafael Mendes: Right. So maybe top-level types should be cause-domain based: runtime, routing, capacity, control plane, auth, telemetry, gateway/edge, private connectivity, and “external dependency.” Then symptoms are captured elsewhere.

[19:59] Aisha Bello: But then it’s harder for oncall. They start with symptom.

[20:03] Rafael Mendes: Decision tree starts with symptom, taxonomy label is cause-domain.

[20:08] Allison Grant: That’s a good split.

[20:10] Mei Lin: But during live incident you don’t know cause-domain.

[20:14] Rafael Mendes: True. So we allow initial classification by symptom and later refine. But the field is still one thing.

[20:22] Dylan Brooks: From UX standpoint, we can show the dropdown grouped by symptom sections but store as cause-domain. Like: “If you see 5xx, consider these: capacity, routing, runtime, gateway.”

[20:35] Rafael Mendes: Okay, I like that.

[20:37] Kira Thompson: Runbooks can follow that structure too.

[20:41] Rafael Mendes: So we will not have top-level “5xx spike” as a type. We’ll have “Capacity,” “Routing,” “Runtime,” “Edge/Gateway,” etc.

[20:51] Sofia Ivanova: Please include “Serving runtime regression” as a top-level.

[20:54] Rafael Mendes: Yes.

[20:56] Ethan Park: And “Control plane” top-level.

[20:59] Rafael Mendes: Yes.

[21:01] Allison Grant: “Telemetry” top-level.

[21:03] Rafael Mendes: Yes.

[21:04] Rafael Mendes: “External dependency” is tricky. Like cloud provider outage.

[21:09] Vanessa Ortiz: That’s real. If AWS has a regional GPU capacity meltdown, it’s an external dependency.

[21:15] Rafael Mendes: But it still impacts our fleet and routing. If mitigation is failover, it’s routing. If mitigation is wait for AWS, it’s external.

[21:24] Allison Grant: Keep it as a modifier like “suspected trigger = cloud provider.” Don’t make it a top-level.

[21:31] Rafael Mendes: Good point. So no external dependency top-level. Use trigger metadata.

[21:38] Rafael Mendes: Tooling failure? Like incident-bot broken.

[21:41] Dylan Brooks: That happens, but does it count as an incident type? It impacts response but not customer traffic.

[21:47] Rafael Mendes: It’s in-scope for high-sev internal incidents that threaten reliability. But we can treat that as “Telemetry/Tooling impairment.”

[21:55] Allison Grant: No, please don’t put tooling under telemetry. It’s different.

[21:58] Rafael Mendes: Okay, “Incident response tooling impairment” as a small top-level? Or fold into “Control plane”?

[22:06] Dylan Brooks: I’d rather have “Tooling/Automation outage” top-level, owner Tooling (me). But it’ll be low volume.

[22:13] Rafael Mendes: Low volume is okay if it’s stable.

[22:16] Mei Lin: In the last 90 days we had like three: bot modal missing fields, pager routing misconfigured, and dashboard permissions blocking CS.

[22:26] Aisha Bello: That third one is kind of telemetry/access.

[22:29] Allison Grant: Yeah, that’s a telemetry subtype.

[22:33] Rafael Mendes: Hmm. Let’s decide: bot outage and pager routing misconfig belong to “Incident response tooling.” Dashboard perms stays in telemetry.

[22:43] Allison Grant: Fine.

[22:44] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[22:46] Rafael Mendes: So final top-level list is more like:
- Serving runtime regression
- Smart routing / fallback misbehavior
- Fleet / capacity shortage
- Edge / gateway availability (including streaming resets when gateway)
- Control plane outage/degradation
- Quota / rate limiting enforcement
- AuthN/AuthZ / RBAC failure
- Telemetry / observability impairment
- Private deployment connectivity/config
- Incident response tooling impairment

[23:15] Rafael Mendes: That’s 10. Feels right.

[23:18] Mina Haddad: Edge/gateway vs streaming - are we separating?

[23:21] Rafael Mendes: Streaming is a symptom; the cause domain is edge/gateway, routing, or runtime. So type should be edge/gateway when it’s in that domain.

[23:32] Sofia Ivanova: Works.

[23:33] Kira Thompson: For oncall we can still have the decision tree branch “Streaming disconnects?” and then guide to choose which domain.

[23:40] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[23:42] Rafael Mendes: Okay, owner mapping table: We’ll assign one primary team per type.

[23:47] Rafael Mendes: - Serving runtime regression: Serving Runtime (Sofia/Noah)
- Smart routing/fallback misbehavior: Smart Routing (Mina/Ravi)
- Fleet/capacity shortage: Infra/Fleet (Vanessa/Harish)
- Edge/gateway availability: Platform Edge/Gateway (Platform)
- Control plane outage/degradation: Platform Control Plane (Ethan)
- Quota/rate limiting enforcement: Control Plane (Ethan) with Support consulted
- AuthN/AuthZ/RBAC failure: Control Plane primary, Security Ops consulted (Fiona)
- Telemetry/observability impairment: Observability (Allison)
- Private deployment connectivity/config: Private Deployments/Networking (Victor) with Infra consulted
- Incident response tooling impairment: Tooling Integration (Dylan/Devon)

[24:29] Rafael Mendes: Any objections?

[24:31] (crosstalk)

[24:32] Vanessa Ortiz: For edge/gateway, make sure it’s not Fleet by default. We get blamed for load balancer problems.

[24:38] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[24:39] Allison Grant: For auth failures, I want the subtype list to include “certificate/cert rotation” because those show up as authz errors sometimes.

[24:47] Fiona McCarthy: That’s fine, but those are not security incidents per se unless compromised.

[24:52] Rafael Mendes: We’ll capture that in the alignment note.

[24:55] Ethan Park: For quota enforcement, can we clarify that “customer-caused throttling” is still a control plane type? Because it’s not our outage.

[25:03] Rafael Mendes: Yes, it’s still quota enforcement type, but severity might be different or we flag as “customer-caused.”

[25:11] Aisha Bello: That matters for SLA breach calculations.

[25:14] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. We’ll add a flag “customer-caused” that pauses SLA timers, with approval by IC + SRE.

[25:22] Allison Grant: Please document that carefully. People will misuse it.

[25:26] Rafael Mendes: Yep.

[25:28] Rafael Mendes: Let’s talk SLAs quickly. We’re not finalizing numbers today, but we need a direction.

[25:35] Aisha Bello: Generally, Sev0 needs mitigate in minutes, fix in hours/days depending. Dedicated and Private have stricter comms expectations, not always faster fixes.

[25:44] Vanessa Ortiz: For capacity incidents in Dedicated, mitigation can be “add burst capacity” but provisioning takes time. We can’t promise 15 minutes if cloud capacity is constrained.

[25:56] Rafael Mendes: So SLA matrix should be by tier and severity, but with realistic carve-outs.

[26:02] Mina Haddad: Routing mitigations are usually faster, like policy flip, disable fallback, revert change. That can be within 30 minutes.

[26:10] Sofia Ivanova: Runtime fixes are slower, because rollout and validation. Mitigation might be traffic shift or rollback, which is fast-ish, but fix is days.

[26:20] Rafael Mendes: Okay, I’ll stop there. Aisha, you’ll take Dedicated Sev1 overload row and validate with Lauren.

[26:27] Aisha Bello: Yep.

[26:28] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[26:30] Rafael Mendes: Next: how do we handle multi-cause in dashboards? If we pick one primary type, we may undercount contributing systems.

[26:40] Mei Lin: For reporting, primary type is fine, but add “contributing systems” tags in postmortems. For the incident record itself, we can store a list but not as metric labels.

[26:51] Allison Grant: Exactly. Don’t turn contributing systems into Prometheus labels.

[26:56] Dylan Brooks: In the incident database we can store it as an array, then dashboards can count it separately if we want.

[27:03] Rafael Mendes: Nice. But for the doc, v1 requirement is only primary type and owner.

[27:09] Dylan Brooks: We can make contributing factors optional.

[27:12] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[27:14] Rafael Mendes: Let’s do a quick run through the decision tree draft. Step 1: what do you see? 5xx, latency, streaming, auth errors, control plane errors, capacity alerts.

[27:25] Kira Thompson: For each symptom, we list 2-3 checks and which taxonomy type it likely maps to.

[27:30] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[27:32] Allison Grant: And we should explicitly say: “Do not use customer symptoms as taxonomy labels.” Use the domain.

[27:38] Rafael Mendes: Yup.

[27:40] Rafael Mendes: Okay, last contentious point: “Latency/performance degradation” as its own type. Do we keep it?

[27:49] Sofia Ivanova: I’d rather not. Latency is everywhere. If it’s runtime, pick runtime. If it’s routing, pick routing.

[27:56] Mina Haddad: Agree.

[27:58] Vanessa Ortiz: Agree.

[28:00] Aisha Bello: From an SLO viewpoint, latency SLO is separate, but taxonomy doesn’t need latency as a type.

[28:08] Rafael Mendes: Great, removed.

[28:10] Rafael Mendes: What about “Availability” as a type?

[28:13] Allison Grant: Same issue. It’s a symptom.

[28:16] Rafael Mendes: Okay, removed.

[28:18] Rafael Mendes: That simplifies.

[28:20] Rafael Mendes: Dylan, from tooling side, what do you need from us to implement this in bot?

[28:28] Dylan Brooks: Three things: 1) final allowlist of types/subtypes, 2) owner mapping table for type/subtype -> owner team, and 3) runbook links per type. Also some wording: what to do if unknown, and when to update.

[28:46] Rafael Mendes: You’ll get (1) and (2) by end of week, runbook links maybe next.

[28:51] Kira Thompson: I can do initial runbook links for the top three this week.

[28:55] Dylan Brooks: Great.

[28:56] Rafael Mendes: Mei, what about backfill classification guidance? We need examples in the taxonomy doc.

[29:04] Mei Lin: I’ll take ten incidents per confusing bucket and write “why we picked this type.” People learn by examples.

[29:13] Rafael Mendes: Perfect.

[29:15] Fiona McCarthy: For auth failures, include examples where security joined. Like suspicious auth bursts. Otherwise people will forget to loop us in.

[29:23] Rafael Mendes: Yes. We’ll add a “security handoff” section.

[29:27] Rafael Mendes: Okay, we have 30 minutes left; let’s drill into one more edge case: “telemetry false positive.” Like p95 latency alert fired but it was metrics pipeline issue.

[29:38] Allison Grant: That should be “Telemetry impairment.” Subtype: “incorrect alerting / bad SLO calc.” Owner: Observability.

[29:46] Aisha Bello: But the SLO logic lives in slo-toolkit too.

[29:49] Allison Grant: That’s still Observability/SRE. We can consult whoever owns the tool. But primary is Observability.

[29:56] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[29:58] Mina Haddad: Another weird one: routing fallback reasons. We’re adding structured enums: capacity, health, policy, timeout. That helps classification.

[30:07] Rafael Mendes: Great. And we can map “policy” to routing misbehavior, “capacity” to fleet, “health” depends, “timeout” maybe gateway/runtime.

[30:17] Sofia Ivanova: Timeout can be runtime stalls too.

[30:19] Rafael Mendes: Yes, that’s where we need examples.

[30:22] Mei Lin: In the data, “timeout” incidents often end up being “gateway max time” misconfigured.

[30:28] Ethan Park: That’s control plane sometimes, because timeouts are configured in policy.

[30:34] Rafael Mendes: Great. So the fallback reason is a hint, not the taxonomy itself.

[30:40] Dylan Brooks: Bot can suggest “likely type” and say “based on fallback_reason=policy.”

[30:46] Rafael Mendes: Cool.

[30:48] Rafael Mendes: Another: “control plane config write failures” that block mitigation during a live incident. Does that make the primary type control plane even if the underlying issue is fleet?

[31:00] Ethan Park: If we can’t change routing policy because control plane is down, that becomes its own incident, but it’s kind of a secondary incident.

[31:08] Rafael Mendes: In v1, we pick one primary incident type. But we can create a linked incident if needed.

[31:15] Allison Grant: Or treat control plane as contributing factor.

[31:18] Rafael Mendes: Right. Primary remains fleet if customer traffic impacted by capacity. Control plane becomes contributing factor if it slowed mitigation.

[31:27] Ethan Park: Works.

[31:29] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[31:31] Rafael Mendes: Let’s sanity check the owner mapping dispute that came up last week: “overload due to routing policy mis-set.” Who owns?

[31:40] Mina Haddad: If it’s a routing policy issue, routing owns. Even if the symptom is overload.

[31:45] Ethan Park: But if the mis-set came from control plane bug writing wrong value, then it’s control plane.

[31:52] Mina Haddad: That’s fair. But again, primary based on mitigation. If we fixed by editing policy, routing. If we fixed by fixing control plane writer, control plane.

[32:03] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[32:05] Rafael Mendes: Allison, are we okay with subtypes being optional? Because sometimes oncall can pick type but not subtype.

[32:13] Allison Grant: Optional subtype is okay if we don’t lose reporting. But for the bot, maybe make subtype required for certain types only.

[32:22] Dylan Brooks: We can do conditional requiredness. For example, runtime requires subtype because it’s actionable; telemetry maybe optional; private connectivity maybe required because it routes to the right SME.

[32:34] Rafael Mendes: Good. Let’s define which types require subtype:
- Runtime: required
- Routing: required
- Control plane: required
- Private connectivity: required
Others optional.

[32:48] Sofia Ivanova: I support that.

[32:50] Mina Haddad: Same.

[32:52] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[32:54] Rafael Mendes: Okay, quick check on naming. “Smart routing / fallback misbehavior” is a mouthful.

[33:01] Mina Haddad: Can we call it “Routing/fallback” and then subtype “misbehavior”? Because fallback can be normal.

[33:08] Rafael Mendes: But taxonomy type is for incidents; so fallback being normal is not an incident.

[33:13] Allison Grant: Keep it “Routing/fallback misbehavior” and shorten in UI.

[33:17] Dylan Brooks: UI can show “Routing/Fallback.” In description, say “misbehavior.”

[33:22] Rafael Mendes: Done.

[33:24] Rafael Mendes: “Fleet/capacity shortage” is fine.

[33:27] Vanessa Ortiz: Yeah.

[33:28] Rafael Mendes: “Edge/gateway availability” might be “Gateway/Edge.”

[33:32] Ethan Park: That’s fine.

[33:33] Rafael Mendes: “Telemetry/observability impairment” is fine.

[33:36] Allison Grant: Yep.

[33:37] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[33:39] Rafael Mendes: I want to capture the explicit rule for multi-cause incidents. I’ll read it out:

[33:45] Rafael Mendes: “Primary incident type must correspond to the system/team that had the primary mitigation lever used during the incident. Other involved systems must be recorded as contributing factors in the incident record and/or postmortem. If multiple mitigations were used, pick the earliest mitigation that restored customer impact.”

[34:09] Mina Haddad: That last sentence is key. The earliest mitigation is usually the one that mattered.

[34:14] Vanessa Ortiz: Yes.

[34:15] Sofia Ivanova: Yup.

[34:17] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[34:19] Rafael Mendes: Security alignment note: “Auth failures that are suspicious require dual-tracking.” Fiona, what’s “suspicious” in one line?

[34:28] Fiona McCarthy: Sudden auth failure spikes from new IP ranges, brute-force patterns, repeated token validation failures across many accounts, or signs of credential leakage. If you see that, page Security Ops and open a security incident while continuing reliability mitigation.

[34:44] Rafael Mendes: Great, we’ll paste that.

[34:46] Rafael Mendes: Alright, last five minutes: recap action items and decisions.

[34:52] Rafael Mendes: Decisions:
- Taxonomy v1 top-level types are domain-based (runtime/routing/fleet/control plane/etc.), not symptom-based (5xx/latency).
- Primary owner mapping resolved for capacity vs routing vs quota.
- Multi-cause rule: pick mitigation owner as primary type, track others as contributing.
- Add optional “suspected trigger” field (deploy/rollout, traffic spike, cloud provider, customer change, unknown) not used as metric label.
- Conditional subtype requiredness for runtime/routing/control plane/private.

[35:28] Rafael Mendes: Action items I captured; I’ll send them in Slack too.

[35:33] Dylan Brooks: Can you also send the final allowlist as a CSV or something? It’s easier for config.

[35:38] Rafael Mendes: Yes, Ethan and I will sync. Ethan will update schema draft.

[35:44] Ethan Park: Yep.

[35:45] Allison Grant: And please include the note about allowlist and unknown bucket. I don’t want “other-other” creeping in.

[35:52] Rafael Mendes: Will do.

[35:54] Kira Thompson: I’ll start runbook mapping for overload, auth, and routing.

[35:59] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[36:01] Aisha Bello: I’ll draft the Dedicated Sev1 overload SLA row and send it to Rafael and Lauren.

[36:06] Rafael Mendes: Perfect.

[36:08] Rafael Mendes: Any last objections?

[36:10] (silence)

[36:12] Rafael Mendes: Cool. Ending recording. Thanks all.

[36:16] Fireflies.ai: Recording stopped.

Note: Transcript ends early due to a recording interruption; remaining 22 minutes continued off-record in Zoom chat according to attendees. Key decisions captured above were reiterated and no additional taxonomy categories were added in the off-record portion.

title: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
preview: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.
text: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.

source.title: Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
topics: Launch KPI recap: traffic, signup conversion, activation, demo requests, SQLs, influenced pipeline
Channel performance: email, social, community, partners, analyst pre-briefs, webinar
What worked: interactive table, methodology credibility, sales enablement, press/analyst narrative
What didn’t: launch-day cache miss storm/timeouts, metric naming confusion, region/residency questions, follow-up latency
Sales/support feedback and objection patterns (fairness, variance, caching/batching, workload fit)
Experiments and follow-ups: retargeting, nurture, webinar recording, replication guide, in-product surfaces
Benchmark refresh cadence: what triggers a republish vs addendum, ownership and run scheduling
source.summary: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.
questions: What is the minimum dataset refresh cadence that stays credible without becoming an ongoing program?
Do we publish competitor context at all in v1.1, or keep it purely Redwood numbers + methodology?
How do we define “material change” (model version, kernel/runtime change, region capacity change) that triggers a republish?
Can we surface benchmark results inside Console for logged-in users without creating support expectations?
action_items: Tyler Benson - Publish final campaign dashboard snapshot (source breakdown + assisted pipeline definitions) to GTM drive - Due 2025-07-18
Ben Carter - Launch retargeting + nurture sequence v2 (include webinar recording + methodology explainer) - Due 2025-07-22
Ethan Wallace - Draft v1.1 addendum blog outline: reliability learnings + how to interpret variance + metric naming clarification - Due 2025-07-23
Jonas Weber + Claire Dubois - Propose refresh cadence + material-change thresholds (runtime/model/region) + sign-off workflow - Due 2025-07-25
Sean Gallagher - Write up launch incident postmortem summary for marketing-facing stakeholders + preventive controls checklist - Due 2025-07-19
Felix Schneider - Ticket/PR plan: pre-warm cache + safer default TTLs for benchmark JSON + CDN protection for spikes - Due 2025-07-24
Markus Klein - Create 1-page “How to replicate in your PoC” worksheet (inputs, configs, caveats) and share with AEs/SEs - Due 2025-07-26
Jasmine Liu - Update Support macro responses (caching/batching, variance, region coverage, residency language) and link canonical FAQ - Due 2025-07-21
Lukas Meyer + Stephanie Nguyen - Send top 5 objections heard in calls this week + suggested answers to PMM - Due 2025-07-18
next_steps: Run a 2-week post-launch follow-up sprint: retargeting, webinar recording distribution, and high-intent SDR routing
Ship clarifications: rename/tooltip the latency/$ metric, add “how to read this chart” section, and highlight confidence/variance notes
Operational hardening: cache warm-up, rate-limit guardrails, and monitoring playbook specifically for marketing launches
Align on benchmark refresh: quarterly default, with an “on-demand” refresh when a material change threshold is hit
source.transcript:
Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
Recorded: 2025-07-16 10:04 AM PT
Duration: 53 minutes
Attendees: Soojin Lee, Camila Reyes, Ethan Wallace, Ben Carter, Tyler Benson, Jonas Weber, Claire Dubois, Sean Gallagher, Felix Schneider, Alicia Perez, Markus Klein, Stephanie Nguyen, Lukas Meyer, Mei Lin, Isabelle Laurent, Jasmine Liu

---

Soojin Lee (10:04:12)
Alright, we’ll start. This is the post-launch retro for the benchmark report campaign. Goal is candid: KPI review, what worked, what didn’t, and then a concrete plan for follow-ups and refresh cadence. I’ll keep us moving.

Camila Reyes (10:04:32)
And we’re recording, right?

Soojin Lee (10:04:34)
Yeah, recording is on.

Tyler Benson (10:04:37)
Yep.

Soojin Lee (10:04:39)
Okay. Tyler, can you do the KPI run-through first? Then Ben for channel + funnel, Ethan for content, Markus for field feedback, Jonas/Claire for methodology reactions and refresh, and Sean/Felix for incident and technical learnings.

Tyler Benson (10:04:58)
Cool. Sharing numbers verbally. I’ll post the dashboard screenshot after.

Tyler Benson (10:05:04)
So in the first 7 days post-launch: 118k sessions to the benchmark landing page. About 71% new users. Primary sources: 34% organic/social combined, 22% direct, 18% email, 13% community referrals, 8% partner placements, and the remainder from press/analyst backlinks.

Ben Carter (10:05:31)
Quick clarify, community referrals meaning Hacker News/Reddit and a couple Discords, right?

Tyler Benson (10:05:36)
Yeah, that bucket’s basically HN, Reddit, a few newsletters, and some “unknown ref” where we see UTM-less but the timing matches.

Tyler Benson (10:05:47)
Conversion: overall signup conversion from that page to create-account was 3.8%. That’s higher than our typical blog posts, which are like 1.5 to 2.1. We got 1,420 net new signups attributed as last-touch or first-touch to benchmark assets, depending on the model.

Isabelle Laurent (10:06:07)
Just to avoid confusion later: 1,420 is last-touch for self-serve signups. First-touch is higher, around 2,050, but we didn’t want to over-credit.

Tyler Benson (10:06:16)
Exactly. Activation: 41% of those signups made at least one API call within 72 hours. That’s decent. However, only 19% reached the “10k tokens generated” milestone in week one. That’s the same gap we usually see when the acquisition is technical-curious but not yet building.

Camila Reyes (10:06:36)
So activation is fine, depth is the issue, not the top.

Tyler Benson (10:06:41)
Yeah. Now enterprise: demo request form submissions from the benchmark page and PDF were 182. Of those, 66 were routed to AEs as “high intent” based on company size/ICP. 46 became SQLs within 10 days. Early days but trending good.

Stephanie Nguyen (10:07:03)
Anecdotally, those were high quality. Less “what is Redwood” and more “we need dedicated capacity, what’s the process.”

Tyler Benson (10:07:10)
Pipeline influence: using our standard “campaign member + engaged + meeting held” definition, influenced pipeline is $1.9M so far. That’s not all net new, some acceleration. We have 7 new Dedicated opportunities that specifically referenced the report. Two Private/VPC ones, both regulated.

Jasmine Liu (10:07:30)
Support saw the regulated ones asking for security review links right away.

Tyler Benson (10:07:34)
Webinar: 820 registrations, 410 live attendees, 260 watched recording in first 5 days. Demo conversion from webinar attendees: 5.6% submitted a “talk to us” CTA.

Ben Carter (10:07:52)
That’s quite high for us.

Tyler Benson (10:07:54)
Yes. But there’s also a caveat: launch day incident created a dip in time-on-page and a spike in bounce. We’ll get into it.

Soojin Lee (10:08:04)
Thanks. Ben, channel and funnel learnings.

Ben Carter (10:08:09)
Okay. Email performed well. We ran two variants: one more “results headline” subject line, one more “methodology transparency.” The methodology subject line had lower opens but higher click-to-signup. So: hype draws attention, but credibility gets the conversion.

Ethan Wallace (10:08:29)
That matches the comments we saw. People were basically like “show me your work.”

Ben Carter (10:08:34)
Exactly. Paid retargeting was minimal at launch; we intentionally didn’t do a heavy paid push until we validated stability. In hindsight, we probably could have turned it on 24 hours later. For community, HN was a big spike; we got a top thread for a few hours, then the incident hit and people started reporting timeouts. That hurt sentiment for a day.

Sean Gallagher (10:08:57)
Yeah, I saw that thread. A couple folks assumed it was “marketing page can’t handle traffic, so inference probably can’t.” Which is unfair but that’s the perception.

Ben Carter (10:09:07)
Partner placements: the cloud partner newsletter slot drove fewer clicks than expected but higher demo intent. Those were bigger companies.

Lukas Meyer (10:09:18)
EMEA leads from the partner slot had a lot of region questions. Like “which EU region exactly,” and “is data staying in the EU.”

Ben Carter (10:09:26)
Right. That’s one of the “didn’t work” items. People hit the benchmark table and they’re like, “ok but where is eu-west actually, and is that dedicated or hosted.” We need clearer labeling.

Alicia Perez (10:09:39)
We can add a small “deployment mode” toggle or at least a tag, but we have to avoid making the table a monster.

Ben Carter (10:09:47)
Funnel friction: we saw a decent number of export clicks—CSV and PDF. Those had a 2x demo-request rate after export. But we didn’t have enough “next step” on the export moment. Like after you export, it should say: “Want us to run this on your workload? Dedicated sizing call.”

Soojin Lee (10:10:06)
Yep. That’s a good follow-up experiment.

Ben Carter (10:10:09)
Also, nurture. People sign up, they poke, but then stall. We need a “replicate this result” template—like a cookbook. Markus has been asking for that for the field.

Markus Klein (10:10:21)
Yeah, the number one ask from prospects was, “Cool chart, how do I reproduce that on my prompts?”

Soojin Lee (10:10:26)
We’ll get there. Ethan, content.

Ethan Wallace (10:10:30)
So blog + PDF. The blog drove more top-of-funnel, but the PDF drove more enterprise. People forwarded the PDF inside their orgs. We saw a ton of “/benchmark.pdf” direct hits from corporate networks.

Mei Lin (10:10:46)
Also from the logs, the PDF was often downloaded after someone spent time in the interactive table. So the table is like the hook, PDF is the internal share artifact.

Ethan Wallace (10:10:56)
What worked: the methodology section was long and a little nerdy, but people actually read it. Average scroll depth on methodology page was higher than on the “results” page, which is not typical.

Camila Reyes (10:11:10)
That’s great because it validates our thesis: trust and defensibility.

Ethan Wallace (10:11:15)
What didn’t: the metric label “latency/$” was confusing in the first 48 hours. People asked if it meant “latency divided by dollars” or “dollars per latency.” Which is… yes, but not intuitive.

Soojin Lee (10:11:29)
We tried “latency per dollar” and “latency per $1” and “latency/$” all over the place.

Ethan Wallace (10:11:35)
We should standardize to “Latency per $ (lower is better)” and show a tooltip. Also: a few folks latched onto a single model number and assumed it applied to their workload, ignoring context length and concurrency. We need a stronger “results depend on workload” callout, without making it sound like we’re hedging too hard.

Claire Dubois (10:11:58)
Yeah, and we have the supporting metrics—p50, p95, throughput—but they’re kind of hidden behind toggles.

Alicia Perez (10:12:05)
The toggles were a compromise for page performance and readability. But maybe a small “Recommended view” default that shows p95 and cost per 1M tokens in a mini panel.

Ethan Wallace (10:12:16)
Other content gap: we didn’t have a good public response draft for the “fairness” critique until it was already circulating.

Soojin Lee (10:12:25)
Right. We ended up writing the response in Slack and then posting after the fact. That’s a process lesson.

Soojin Lee (10:12:33)
Markus, field feedback.

Markus Klein (10:12:36)
Okay. Overall, it’s been a great asset. AEs are using it in outbound sequences and in live calls. The interactive table is the thing—they can filter to region and model and then it becomes a “show, don’t tell.”

Stephanie Nguyen (10:12:53)
We used it in two discovery calls this week. Both times, the prospect asked about p95 and whether caching was on. Once we answered, they were like “okay, that’s actually better than I expected.”

Markus Klein (10:13:05)
Top objections:
1) “Is this cherry-picked?”
2) “Does this apply to my workload? We’re streaming, or we’re short prompts, or we have tool calls.”
3) “Are you comparing to AWS Bedrock or OpenAI?” They want competitor context but also don’t trust it.
4) “What about dedicated vs hosted?” People assume the chart is hosted-only.

Lukas Meyer (10:13:28)
EMEA-specific: “Where is the data processed” and “are you storing prompts.” A lot of them didn’t read the security section.

Jasmine Liu (10:13:36)
Support got that too. People saw “benchmarks” and assumed we captured their prompts. Different audiences.

Markus Klein (10:13:43)
Also: the “replicate this” ask. Prospects want a recipe: give me your runner, give me the flags, tell me what hardware. Even if we can’t disclose all details, we can provide a safe PoC playbook.

Jonas Weber (10:13:59)
We can share the Docker runner and the manifest format. That’s already in the repo; we just need to make sure the public docs are clear and don’t leak internal IDs.

Soojin Lee (10:14:08)
Let’s park the doc angle. Sean and Felix, incident and reliability lessons.

Sean Gallagher (10:14:13)
So launch day, we had a cache miss storm. The benchmark JSON endpoint got hammered, CDN didn’t cache as expected due to a header mismatch, and every request went origin. Origin was fine, but it was doing a bunch of computation and auth checks, so it slowed down. We saw increased page latency, then timeouts.

Felix Schneider (10:14:37)
Also the table component was re-fetching on some filter interactions, more than we intended. So one user could generate multiple origin hits in a few seconds.

Sean Gallagher (10:14:48)
We mitigated by forcing a longer TTL and by serving a static fallback for the most common default view. After that, page stabilized.

Camila Reyes (10:15:00)
How long was the impact window?

Sean Gallagher (10:15:03)
Roughly 47 minutes of elevated latency and about 18 minutes of real user-visible timeouts. We also saw a brief 429 spike when rate limiting kicked in—separate from inference rate limiting, but the site service.

Ben Carter (10:15:18)
The problem is the optics: people screenshot 429 and post it.

Sean Gallagher (10:15:22)
Yep. We should treat marketing launches like product launches: load test the landing page, pre-warm cache, and have a rollback plan.

Felix Schneider (10:15:33)
Agreed. We can do a “cache warm” job at publish time. Also we need safer defaults: always cache the JSON artifact at the edge, because it’s versioned. We were being too conservative.

Soojin Lee (10:15:45)
Okay. Jonas and Claire: methodology feedback from outside and the refresh cadence question.

Jonas Weber (10:15:52)
Externally, the feedback we saw was mostly positive. People liked that we published configs, prompt set description, and stability checks. The critique was “does this reflect my workload,” which is fair. We can’t cover every workload in v1.

Claire Dubois (10:16:08)
A couple people also asked about streaming metrics specifically. We had that debate internally, and we ended up not emphasizing streaming because it complicates the latency/$ definition. But streaming is very common, so we should at least add a section: “How streaming changes what you should measure.”

Ethan Wallace (10:16:28)
Even if it’s just guidance, not new charts.

Jonas Weber (10:16:31)
Refresh cadence: my proposal is a quarterly refresh as default, plus an “on-demand” refresh when there’s a material change: new model version added to catalog, new runtime kernel path that affects p95 materially, new region, or price change that affects $/token.

Isabelle Laurent (10:16:52)
We need that “material change” definition to be crisp because it impacts legal and the PDF versioning.

Claire Dubois (10:16:58)
Right. Material could be: any change >7% in latency/$ for a headline configuration, or any change >10% in p95 latency for a region-model pair. Also any change that flips ordering between top contenders.

Ben Carter (10:17:12)
But if we tie ourselves to a numeric threshold, do we create obligations?

Soojin Lee (10:17:16)
Legal might push back, yeah.

Camila Reyes (10:17:19)
We can define internal triggers without promising public cadence beyond “we’ll refresh periodically.”

Jonas Weber (10:17:26)
Internally we need triggers so we don’t argue every time. Publicly we can say “data as of [date], see artifacts.”

Mei Lin (10:17:35)
Also from a data pipeline standpoint, quarterly is manageable. Monthly becomes a program, which was out of scope.

Soojin Lee (10:17:43)
Let’s do: quarterly target, on-demand if major model release or major runtime change. We’ll capture the thresholds as internal.

Soojin Lee (10:17:52)
Now, I want to go around for “what worked / what didn’t” quickly, then we’ll lock follow-up experiments.

Alicia Perez (10:17:59)
What worked: the design was scannable. People understood “filter model/region, toggle p95.” Also the disclaimers being near the table helped, even though some folks still missed them.

Alicia Perez (10:18:12)
What didn’t: on mobile the filters were a bit cramped, and the disclaimer collapsed. That likely contributed to confusion.

Felix Schneider (10:18:22)
What worked: export flows. People used them. What didn’t: cache configuration and the re-fetch behavior.

Jasmine Liu (10:18:31)
Support: what worked is having a single canonical methodology page we can link. What didn’t: we didn’t have macros updated ahead of time, so agents were improvising for the first day.

Stephanie Nguyen (10:18:44)
Sales: worked—one-slide summary and talk track. Didn’t—region clarity. People asked “Is this on Dedicated?” and we were like, “It’s on our platform,” which is correct but not satisfying.

Lukas Meyer (10:18:58)
EMEA: add explicit region names and residency notes. “EU” is not a region.

Ethan Wallace (10:19:06)
Content: worked—methodology depth. Didn’t—metric naming. Also, we may have underplayed reliability story. People care about p95 and variance.

Sean Gallagher (10:19:18)
Agree. If we say “production economics,” we need to show we handled variability. Even our stability gates story is strong.

Soojin Lee (10:19:27)
Okay. Follow-up experiments. Ben, you mentioned export CTA and nurture.

Ben Carter (10:19:33)
Yes. I propose three experiments:
1) Retargeting ads to benchmark visitors with two creative angles: “replicate on your workload” and “Dedicated capacity sizing.”
2) A 3-email technical nurture for new signups from the campaign: (a) how to interpret metrics, (b) how to run a quick benchmark with our SDK, (c) link to webinar recording + book a consult.
3) Improve on-page CTAs: after export, show a “Send me a Dedicated sizing plan” CTA.

Tyler Benson (10:20:10)
We should also add analytics events for “export completed” vs “export clicked,” because click isn’t always successful if popup blocked.

Felix Schneider (10:20:19)
We can instrument that.

Soojin Lee (10:20:22)
Markus, what’s the simplest “replicate this” asset?

Markus Klein (10:20:26)
A one-pager PDF or doc with:
- What to measure (p50/p95, throughput, $/1M tokens)
- Example workloads (short chat vs long context vs batch embeddings)
- Exact flags we used in the runner that are safe to share
- Common gotchas: caching, warmup, concurrency
- And a disclaimer: results vary, but this gives comparability.

Claire Dubois (10:20:54)
We can provide a “starter manifest” with placeholders. That’s very actionable.

Soojin Lee (10:21:00)
Ethan, v1.1 content update?

Ethan Wallace (10:21:03)
I’d do a small addendum blog rather than republishing the whole PDF immediately. Addendum can cover: what we learned from launch traffic, clarify metric naming, and a section “how to read and apply this to your workload.” Then later, in the next refresh, we roll those edits into PDF.

Camila Reyes (10:21:24)
I like addendum. Keeps momentum and doesn’t trigger a big legal cycle.

Isabelle Laurent (10:21:30)
Still needs review, but lighter.

Soojin Lee (10:21:33)
Sean/Felix: preventive checklist.

Sean Gallagher (10:21:36)
We’ll write a marketing-launch readiness checklist: cache warm, load test, CDN config, rate-limit thresholds, on-call coverage, and a static fallback mode.

Felix Schneider (10:21:49)
And we’ll change default caching headers for versioned artifacts so this doesn’t happen again.

Soojin Lee (10:21:55)
Now refresh cadence and ownership. Jonas, Claire, can you own a proposal doc? Include “material change” internal thresholds and the run scheduling. Also note any dependencies on capacity reservations.

Jonas Weber (10:22:09)
Yes. We need Fleet involved because region sweeps require reserved capacity. But we can propose a lighter refresh: only update deltas for new models/regions, not full matrix.

Mei Lin (10:22:23)
From a data standpoint, we should still run a stability sample across the matrix, otherwise people will compare new rows to old rows and assume everything is contemporaneous.

Claire Dubois (10:22:33)
We can do: full matrix quarterly, partial monthly internally, and publish only when full matrix is rerun. That keeps comparability.

Ben Carter (10:22:44)
And in between, for big announcements, we can do an “addendum” that clearly states “this is an additional datapoint” rather than “updated report.”

Camila Reyes (10:22:55)
Yes, and keep the “data as of” timestamp prominent.

Soojin Lee (10:23:01)
Okay. Let’s talk about the external fairness critique. What should we do differently next time?

Ethan Wallace (10:23:06)
We should pre-write a public Q&A response: “What did we measure, why, and what we didn’t measure.” Then when someone says “this is biased,” we link the same response.

Claire Dubois (10:23:20)
Also, we should be explicit that we optimized the Redwood stack, not someone else’s stack. People assume we tuned Redwood and left competitors untuned.

Soojin Lee (10:23:30)
But we didn’t even publish competitor numbers.

Claire Dubois (10:23:33)
True, but people project. Even without competitor numbers, they think “you picked something favorable.” The defense is transparency: publish the harness, prompt sets, configs, and the variance checks.

Jonas Weber (10:23:46)
We could add a section: “If you want to run this on your stack, here’s how to approximate the workload.” Not endorsing results, just enabling.

Ben Carter (10:23:56)
And keep language conservative. No “fastest in the world.”

Camila Reyes (10:24:00)
Yes. Legal was very clear about that.

Soojin Lee (10:24:03)
Sales enablement: what updates do you need?

Stephanie Nguyen (10:24:07)
We need a short region/residency script, especially for EU. Like: “These results were run in eu-west; for data residency, Hosted can pin region; Dedicated/Private offers stronger controls.” Something like that.

Lukas Meyer (10:24:24)
And a “what’s supported vs experimental” list. Prospects ask about an APAC region that’s not in the table and then assume we don’t support APAC at all.

Soojin Lee (10:24:36)
We can update the FAQ and the talk track.

Jasmine Liu (10:24:40)
Support macros too.

Soojin Lee (10:24:43)
Okay. Let’s do a short round on “biggest surprise.”

Tyler Benson (10:24:48)
Surprise: methodology content overperformed. People actually engaged with the deep technical stuff.

Alicia Perez (10:24:55)
Surprise: exports were used way more than we expected.

Sean Gallagher (10:24:59)
Surprise: marketing traffic can create incident-level load. We treated it like a static page, but it’s effectively an API-backed app.

Ethan Wallace (10:25:09)
Surprise: the metric naming confusion. I didn’t anticipate “latency/$” would be that ambiguous.

Markus Klein (10:25:16)
Surprise: the report shortened sales cycles for some. People came in pre-sold on the value prop, just wanting proof and process.

Camila Reyes (10:25:25)
Okay. Next: concrete plan for the next two weeks.

Soojin Lee (10:25:30)
I’m proposing: a “post-launch sprint.” Ben owns campaigns, Ethan owns addendum outline, Markus owns PoC replication sheet, Tyler owns final reporting snapshot and definitions, Sean/Felix own the technical checklist and caching fixes, Jonas/Claire own refresh proposal.

Ben Carter (10:25:51)
Works.

Jonas Weber (10:25:52)
Yep.

Sean Gallagher (10:25:53)
Yep.

Soojin Lee (10:25:55)
Before we close: anything we should stop doing?

Camila Reyes (10:25:59)
Stop letting metric naming drift across assets. One phrase, one definition, one tooltip.

Ethan Wallace (10:26:05)
Stop shipping without a “critique response” plan. It’s not if, it’s when.

Felix Schneider (10:26:11)
Stop assuming CDN defaults are correct. We need explicit caching for versioned assets.

Jasmine Liu (10:26:18)
Stop launching without Support macros ready. Even if it’s just five.

Soojin Lee (10:26:24)
Got it.

Soojin Lee (10:26:26)
Last topic: refresh cadence decision. I want alignment now, even if we refine.

Soojin Lee (10:26:32)
Proposal: publish “data as of” date. Commit internally to quarterly full refresh. Allow addendum posts for major model releases, but not update the PDF unless full refresh. On-demand refresh if we make a major runtime or pricing change that affects headline claims.

Isabelle Laurent (10:26:52)
From an ops/legal standpoint, that’s manageable.

Jonas Weber (10:26:56)
From technical standpoint, yes.

Claire Dubois (10:26:58)
Yes.

Camila Reyes (10:27:00)
Marketing standpoint, yes.

Soojin Lee (10:27:03)
Okay. We’ll document it.

Soojin Lee (10:27:06)
We’re at time but we have a few minutes. Any final notes?

Lukas Meyer (10:27:10)
Small thing: please don’t say “EU numbers” in emails. Say “Frankfurt” or “eu-west” and add the residency line. People are sensitive.

Soojin Lee (10:27:22)
Noted.

Stephanie Nguyen (10:27:24)
For NA, we should push the “Dedicated sizing” CTA harder. Those prospects are ready.

Ben Carter (10:27:30)
We can segment by company size and do that.

Ethan Wallace (10:27:34)
Also: we should highlight the stability gates story. It’s a differentiator: we rerun on variance thresholds. That’s not common.

Jonas Weber (10:27:44)
Yes. It’s actually one of the strongest credibility points.

Soojin Lee (10:27:48)
Alright. I’ll send notes with action items. Thanks everyone.

Camila Reyes (10:27:52)
Good work, team.

---
End of transcript.

title: Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
preview: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.
text: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.
