source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-07-08
Time: 3:00pm–3:58pm PT
Duration: 58 min
Title: Benchmark report launch webinar rehearsal (run of show + demo + Q&A)

Attendees
- Noura El-Khoury (producer)
- Soojin Lee (PMM / host)
- Ethan Wallace (content)
- Jonas Weber (Applied ML / performance)
- Claire Dubois (Applied ML / methodology)
- Alicia Perez (design)
- Felix Schneider (web engineering)
- Ben Carter (growth)
- Wei Chen (docs / copy)
- Markus Klein (solutions engineering)

---

Summary (auto)
- Run through the webinar agenda and tighten speaker transitions.
- Validate the live demo flow for the interactive benchmark page.
- Practice answering common “fairness” and “can you replicate this?” questions.
- Capture last-minute edits for slides, copy, and disclaimers.

Questions (auto)
- Are we saying “latency per dollar” or “latency / $” out loud?
- How do we explain caching/batching without sounding like we tuned for the benchmark?
- What do we do if the landing page is slow or 429s?
- How do we avoid competitor callouts but still contextualize?

---

[00:00] Noura El-Khoury: Alright, cool. Recording is on. This is our rehearsal for next week’s benchmark report launch webinar. Goal today: timing, speaker handoffs, demo run-through, and we’ll do a Q and A bank at the end.

[00:15] Soojin Lee: Yep. And reminder, this is the public one, so anything we say should match the final copy and legal-safe language. No “best in the world”, none of that.

[00:24] Ethan Wallace: I wrote that on a sticky note. No absolutes.

[00:28] Noura El-Khoury: Great. I’ll be keeping time and I’ll interrupt if we drift. We’re aiming for 30 minutes of content, 10 minutes live demo, 15 Q&A, plus buffer.

[00:39] Noura El-Khoury: Soojin, you’re the host. You kick. Ethan does the narrative setup. Jonas and Claire split “what we measured.” Alicia + Felix cover demo. Then Soojin closes with CTA and Ben’s follow-up. Sound right?

[00:52] Soojin Lee: Yep.

[00:54] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, let’s do a dry run. Soojin, go.

[01:00] Soojin Lee: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. I’m Soojin, product marketing at Redwood Inference. Today we’re sharing our new benchmark report focused on production inference economics: latency and cost efficiency, or what we call latency per dollar—

[01:13] Wei Chen: Quick interruption, we decided to say “latency per dollar” aloud and show “latency/$” in the UI, right?

[01:18] Soojin Lee: Yes. Thanks. Latency per dollar. We’ll show the metric as latency slash dollar in the table.

[01:25] Soojin Lee: —and why we think measuring just tokens per second isn’t enough for production teams. We’ll cover how we measured, what we found across models and regions, and how you can use the interactive table and download the PDF.

[01:39] Noura El-Khoury: Good. Keep that to about 90 seconds in the real thing.

[01:43] Soojin Lee: Got it. Then I’ll hand to Ethan.

[01:46] Soojin Lee: Ethan, over to you.

[01:48] Ethan Wallace: Cool. So the “why” here: when teams ship LLM features, performance isn’t just raw speed. It’s speed under load, consistency across regions, and the cost you pay to hit your SLOs. We built this report because people ask, “What model is fastest?” and that’s not the right question. The right question is: for my workload, in my region, what’s the best tradeoff between latency, reliability, and cost.

[02:14] Jonas Weber: Can we say “best” there?

[02:16] Ethan Wallace: Right, sorry. “What’s the right tradeoff” between latency and cost.

[02:21] Soojin Lee: That’s the pattern: “in our tests,” “for the workloads measured,” “depending on constraints.”

[02:27] Ethan Wallace: Yep. In our tests, across a curated set of models and regions, we saw meaningful differences in cost efficiency that don’t show up if you only look at throughput.

[02:39] Noura El-Khoury: Nice. Ethan, you’re at ~50 seconds. In the live, you have 4 minutes total, so you can include the “production economics” framing and the quick caveat about variance.

[02:49] Ethan Wallace: Got it.

[02:51] Noura El-Khoury: Next, Jonas + Claire: methodology section. Keep it crisp and don’t go too deep on internals.

[02:58] Jonas Weber: Okay. I’ll do the “what is latency per dollar” and Claire does “how we ran it.”

[03:03] Claire Dubois: Sure.

[03:05] Jonas Weber: Latency per dollar is our normalized metric that combines the latency distribution—p50 and p95—and the cost to generate tokens for a defined workload. The idea is not that one number replaces everything; it’s a way to compare cost efficiency while keeping latency honest.

[03:24] Markus Klein: We should say “cost per 1M tokens” too, because prospects ask for that.

[03:28] Jonas Weber: Yes. We also provide cost per one million output tokens and throughput, and we always show p50 and p95 latency.

[03:37] Soojin Lee: Minor: “output tokens” specifically, so no confusion.

[03:41] Jonas Weber: Right, cost per 1M output tokens.

[03:44] Claire Dubois: On methodology: we used our prompt set v3, designed to reflect a production mix—some prefill-heavy prompts, some decode-heavy. We ran at defined concurrency levels, did warmups, and we applied stability gates. If p95 latency variance exceeded a threshold, we reran automatically.

[04:06] Felix Schneider: Claire, should you mention caching? It’s one of the top questions.

[04:10] Claire Dubois: Yes. We used Redwood’s default production serving settings for each deployment mode tested. We did not create special one-off tuning for the benchmark. Some optimizations like continuous batching are part of standard serving; caching policy is described in the methodology section.

[04:26] Wei Chen: Careful: “default” can be interpreted. Maybe “standard recommended configuration for this workload class” or “documented configuration.”

[04:33] Claire Dubois: Good point. We used documented, reproducible configurations, and we publish the configuration details and caveats.

[04:42] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Jonas + Claire, in the real webinar you have 6 minutes combined. Keep those four bullets: prompt mix, concurrency + warmups, stability rerun gates, and where to find the full methodology.

[04:55] Soojin Lee: Then I come back for a quick “what you’ll see in the table” bridge, right?

[04:59] Noura El-Khoury: Yep. Do that and then hand to demo.

[05:03] Soojin Lee: Okay: “Now we’ll show the interactive page where you can filter by model and region, compare p95 latency, and export data.”

[05:11] Soojin Lee: Alicia, Felix, take it away.

[05:14] Alicia Perez: Cool. So on screen, we have the landing page. Above the fold is the key chart and below is the interactive table. I’ll scroll quickly and highlight the three main controls: model filter, region filter, and context length / workload preset.

[05:29] Felix Schneider: I’ll drive the demo live. Just flagging: the current build has the “latency/$” label with a little info icon, but the tooltip copy is still draft.

[05:38] Noura El-Khoury: We’ll capture that as an action item.

[05:41] Felix Schneider: Demo flow: first, show “All models, NA region.” Then click to EU West. Show that p95 changes. Then switch to a longer context bucket and point out “results vary by workload.” Then click “Export CSV” and show it downloads.

[05:59] Alicia Perez: And I’ll call out the footnote/disclaimer region: “Measured on Redwood infrastructure, time window, configs, and rerun gates.”

[06:08] Soojin Lee: We should make sure the disclaimer is readable and not tiny.

[06:12] Alicia Perez: Yep. I increased font size but it might still be small on webinar video.

[06:17] Noura El-Khoury: On that: assume some people watch on 720p. If text isn’t readable, say it out loud and point to “Methodology” link.

[06:25] Felix Schneider: Also, contingency: if the page is slow, we can use the static screenshots in the deck. I have those.

[06:31] Noura El-Khoury: Perfect. We’ll do a “plan B” slide. I’ll add that to the run-of-show.

[06:36] Ben Carter: Small request: when you show Export, pause two seconds so folks see the CTA tracking. It’s silly but we need it.

[06:42] Felix Schneider: Yeah, I can do that.

[06:45] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, let’s simulate the demo quickly. Felix, do the clicks verbally.

[06:50] Felix Schneider: Alright. I’m selecting Region: us-east. Then I toggle to eu-west. You see the table reranks by latency per dollar. Now I select “Long context” bucket. The p95 column updates. And then Export CSV—download.

[07:10] Jonas Weber: We should be careful saying “reranks by latency per dollar,” because it implies the “best” top row. Maybe say “sort by.”

[07:17] Felix Schneider: Good catch. “Sort by.”

[07:19] Soojin Lee: Also do not say “top model” or “winner.”

[07:24] Felix Schneider: Got it.

[07:26] Noura El-Khoury: Great. That’s demo. Now we do the Q&A practice. I’ll throw questions that we expect live. Everyone answer as if it’s public.

[07:36] Noura El-Khoury: Q1: “Is latency per dollar a made-up metric to make you look good?” Jonas?

[07:42] Jonas Weber: We’re transparent that it’s a composite metric, and we publish the components alongside it: p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost per 1M output tokens. The reason for a composite is teams have both latency SLOs and budget constraints. We also share the methodology so you can compute other views from the exported data.

[08:03] Claire Dubois: And we include caveats: results vary by workload, region, and model version. That’s why the table has filters and we publish the prompt set definition.

[08:12] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Q2: “Did you enable caching and batching? Isn’t that unfair?” Claire.

[08:18] Claire Dubois: We used reproducible, documented serving configurations. Modern production inference typically includes optimizations like continuous batching. We don’t hide that—we describe it. Importantly, the benchmark is not a microbenchmark of a single kernel; it’s a production-style measurement under load, with warmups and stability checks.

[08:39] Markus Klein: Add: “If you have a different caching posture, your results may differ.” Prospects appreciate that.

[08:45] Claire Dubois: Yes, and if you want to replicate with your own traffic shape, we can share a replication guide and you can compare.

[08:53] Noura El-Khoury: Q3: “Are these numbers stable? I’ve seen cloud regions be noisy.” Jonas.

[08:58] Jonas Weber: We expect variance, which is why we run repeated sweeps and apply stability gates. If p95 is out of family beyond a threshold, we rerun. We also timestamp the run window and publish the reporting period so you know when it was measured.

[09:15] Soojin Lee: And we should say we reserved capacity for the runs, so we weren’t competing with random background load.

[09:20] Jonas Weber: Right, we reserved capacity as part of the methodology.

[09:24] Noura El-Khoury: Q4: “Are you comparing to AWS/Bedrock/Together/etc?” Soojin.

[09:31] Soojin Lee: The report is primarily Redwood results across models and regions. Where we provide external context, we’re very explicit about scope and caveats. We’re not making broad claims about every provider or every workload. We encourage readers to use our methodology and reproduce for their own use case.

[09:52] Wei Chen: Important: don’t name a competitor unless asked. Keep it general.

[09:56] Soojin Lee: Yep.

[09:57] Noura El-Khoury: Q5: “How do I use this to decide Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private?” Markus.

[10:04] Markus Klein: Hosted is best for quick start and variable usage. Dedicated is for predictable throughput and isolation—if you have strict p95 SLOs or you want reserved capacity. Private is for regulated or data residency requirements, VPC controls, KMS/HSM integrations, and audit logs. The benchmark can help you pick a starting model/region, then we help you validate under your traffic and constraints.

[10:28] Ben Carter: And the CTA should route accordingly: devs to try the API, enterprises to sizing call.

[10:33] Noura El-Khoury: Good. Q6: “Can I get the raw data?” Felix.

[10:38] Felix Schneider: The interactive table supports export, and the report includes a downloadable dataset view. We redact anything sensitive; what you’ll get is aggregated benchmark results and the manifest metadata needed to interpret them.

[10:52] Jonas Weber: And we keep raw logs internally per retention policy, but for public we publish what’s safe.

[10:58] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Now last-minute edits. I captured a few, but let’s do quick round.

[11:04] Alicia Perez: Slide 6: the screenshot is outdated. The region dropdown is different now. I’ll update. Also, the disclaimer font needs to be larger.

[11:13] Wei Chen: Landing page copy: we still have one line that says “most cost-effective across providers” in the draft. That needs to be removed or softened to “cost-efficient in our tests on Redwood.”

[11:27] Soojin Lee: Yep, delete “across providers.”

[11:30] Ethan Wallace: In the script, I had “winner” once. I’ll remove.

[11:35] Jonas Weber: Also be consistent on “p95.” Sometimes we say “P ninety five.” Pick one.

[11:40] Noura El-Khoury: Say “p95” and if you need to clarify, “95th percentile latency.”

[11:46] Ben Carter: CTA slide: we should add both: “Try the interactive benchmark” and “Request Dedicated sizing.” But not too many links.

[11:55] Soojin Lee: We can do one primary CTA and one secondary.

[11:59] Noura El-Khoury: Works.

[12:01] Felix Schneider: One more: the tooltip on “latency/$” should define what the dollar is. It’s basically cost per output token in USD under the measured config. We should avoid confusion like “customer spend” vs “infrastructure cost.”

[12:16] Wei Chen: Yeah. We should say “estimated usage cost” or “usage cost at Redwood list price” depending on what it is.

[12:23] Jonas Weber: It is usage cost based on the pricing schedule for that deployment mode, yes.

[12:27] Soojin Lee: Then it’s “usage cost” not “infra cost.”

[12:31] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I’ll put that in the action items: define tooltip text and confirm the pricing reference.

[12:38] Noura El-Khoury: Time check. We’re good. Now I want to do a quick run from the end: closing and follow-up. Soojin, your close.

[12:47] Soojin Lee: Thanks everyone. The report and interactive table are live, and you can download the PDF. If you’re building on Hosted, start with the models and regions that fit your latency and cost constraints and try it with your prompts. If you need predictable throughput or data isolation, we can help you size Dedicated. And if you have VPC or on-prem requirements, talk to us about Private.

[13:12] Ben Carter: Then I say: you’ll get a follow-up email with links, the recording, and a short “how we measured” explainer.

[13:18] Noura El-Khoury: Nice. Also include: “post questions in chat” and “we’ll publish an FAQ.”

[13:25] Wei Chen: We have that FAQ page drafted.

[13:28] Soojin Lee: Great.

[13:30] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I think we’re done. I’ll read back the key action items.

[13:35] Noura El-Khoury: One, Felix: add tooltip/disclaimer next to latency slash dollar and link to methodology. Two, Wei: fix any “best/cheapest/across providers” language on landing page and confirm “usage cost” wording. Three, Alicia: update slide screenshots and bump disclaimer font for 720p readability. Four, Jonas and Claire: send me and Ethan a short methodology blurb we can repeat verbatim. Five, Ben: finalize follow-up emails and UTM tracking.

[14:05] Noura El-Khoury: Anything else before we stop recording?

[14:08] Ethan Wallace: Just one: I want a short line to address “you can reproduce it.” Like, “We’ll publish configs.”

[14:14] Claire Dubois: I can draft a sentence: “We publish the prompt mix, concurrency, warmup, and rerun rules; you can export the dataset and validate against your own workload.”

[14:24] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[14:26] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Stopping.

(Recording ends)

Producer note: The call continued for a few minutes off-record to coordinate calendar holds and confirm rehearsal deck versioning.

[03:44] Claire Dubois: On methodology: we used our prompt set v3, designed to reflect a production mix—some prefill-heavy prompts, some decode-heavy. [04:06] Felix Schneider: Claire, should you mention caching? It’s one of the top questions.

[07:19] Soojin Lee: Also do not say “top model” or “winner.”

[07:24] Felix Schneider: Got it.

The reason for a composite is teams have both latency SLOs and budget constraints. We also share the methodology so you can compute other views from the exported data.

[08:03] Claire Dubois: And we include caveats: results vary by workload, region, and model version. That’s why the table has filters and we publish the prompt set definition.

[08:12] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Q2: “Did you enable caching and batching? Isn’t that unfair?” Claire.

[08:18] Claire Dubois: We used reproducible, documented serving configurations. Modern production inference typically includes optimizations like continuous batching. We don’t hide that—we describe it. Importantly, the benchmark is not a microbenchmark of a single kernel; it’s a production-style measurement under load, with warmups and stability checks.

[08:39] Markus Klein: Add: “If you have a different caching posture, your results may differ.” Prospects appreciate that.

[08:45] Claire Dubois: Yes, and if you want to replicate with your own traffic shape, we can share a replication guide and you can compare.

[08:53] Noura El-Khoury: Q3: “Are these numbers stable? I’ve seen cloud regions be noisy.” Jonas.

[09:15] Soojin Lee: And we should say we reserved capacity for the runs, so we weren’t competing with random background load.

[09:20] Jonas Weber: Right, we reserved capacity as part of the methodology.

[09:56] Soojin Lee: Yep.

[03:05] Jonas Weber: Latency per dollar is our normalized metric that combines the latency distribution—p50 and p95—and the cost to generate tokens for a defined workload. The idea is not that one number replaces everything; it’s a way to compare cost efficiency while keeping latency honest.

[03:24] Markus Klein: We should say “cost per 1M tokens” too, because prospects ask for that.

[03:28] Jonas Weber: Yes. We also provide cost per one million output tokens and throughput, and we always show p50 and p95 latency.

[03:37] Soojin Lee: Minor: “output tokens” specifically, so no confusion.

[03:41] Jonas Weber: Right, cost per 1M output tokens.

[03:44] Claire Dubois: On methodology: we used our prompt set v3, designed to reflect a production mix—some prefill-heavy prompts, some decode-heavy. We ran at defined concurrency levels, did warmups, and we applied stability gates. If p95 latency variance exceeded a threshold, we reran automatically.

[04:06] Felix Schneider: Claire, should you mention caching? It’s one of the top questions.

[04:10] Claire Dubois: Yes. We used Redwood’s default production serving settings for each deployment mode tested. We did not create special one-off tuning for the benchmark. Some optimizations like continuous batching are part of standard serving; caching policy is described in the methodology section.

[04:26] Wei Chen: Careful: “default” can be interpreted. Maybe “standard recommended configuration for this workload class” or “documented configuration.”

[04:33] Claire Dubois: Good point. We used documented, reproducible configurations, and we publish the configuration details and caveats.

[04:42] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Jonas + Claire, in the real webinar you have 6 minutes combined. Keep those four bullets: prompt mix, concurrency + warmups, stability rerun gates, and where to find the full methodology.

[04:55] Soojin Lee: Then I come back for a quick “what you’ll see in the table” bridge, right?

[04:59] Noura El-Khoury: Yep. Do that and then hand to demo.

[05:03] Soojin Lee: Okay: “Now we’ll show the interactive page where you can filter by model and region, compare p95 latency, and export data.”

[05:11] Soojin Lee: Alicia, Felix, take it away.

[05:14] Alicia Perez: Cool. So on screen, we have the landing page. Above the fold is the key chart and below is the interactive table. I’ll scroll quickly and highlight the three main controls: model filter, region filter, and context length / workload preset.

[05:29] Felix Schneider: I’ll drive the demo live. Just flagging: the current build has the “latency/$” label with a little info icon, but the tooltip copy is still draft.

[05:38] Noura El-Khoury: We’ll capture that as an action item.

[05:41] Felix Schneider: Demo flow: first, show “All models, NA region.” Then click to EU West. Show that p95 changes. Then switch to a longer context bucket and point out “results vary by workload.” Then click “Export CSV” and show it downloads.

[05:59] Alicia Perez: And I’ll call out the footnote/disclaimer region: “Measured on Redwood infrastructure, time window, configs, and rerun gates.”

[06:08] Soojin Lee: We should make sure the disclaimer is readable and not tiny.

[06:12] Alicia Perez: Yep. I increased font size but it might still be small on webinar video.

[06:17] Noura El-Khoury: On that: assume some people watch on 720p. If text isn’t readable, say it out loud and point to “Methodology” link.

[06:25] Felix Schneider: Also, contingency: if the page is slow, we can use the static screenshots in the deck. I have those.

[06:31] Noura El-Khoury: Perfect. We’ll do a “plan B” slide. I’ll add that to the run-of-show.

[06:36] Ben Carter: Small request: when you show Export, pause two seconds so folks see the CTA tracking. It’s silly but we need it.

[06:42] Felix Schneider: Yeah, I can do that.

[06:45] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, let’s simulate the demo quickly. Felix, do the clicks verbally.

[06:50] Felix Schneider: Alright. I’m selecting Region: us-east. Then I toggle to eu-west. You see the table reranks by latency per dollar. Now I select “Long context” bucket. The p95 column updates. And then Export CSV—download.

[07:10] Jonas Weber: We should be careful saying “reranks by latency per dollar,” because it implies the “best” top row. Maybe say “sort by.”

[07:17] Felix Schneider: Good catch. “Sort by.”

[07:19] Soojin Lee: Also do not say “top model” or “winner.”

[07:24] Felix Schneider: Got it.

[07:26] Noura El-Khoury: Great. That’s demo. Now we do the Q&A practice. I’ll throw questions that we expect live. Everyone answer as if it’s public.

[07:36] Noura El-Khoury: Q1: “Is latency per dollar a made-up metric to make you look good?” Jonas?

[07:42] Jonas Weber: We’re transparent that it’s a composite metric, and we publish the components alongside it: p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost per 1M output tokens. The reason for a composite is teams have both latency SLOs and budget constraints. We also share the methodology so you can compute other views from the exported data.

[08:03] Claire Dubois: And we include caveats: results vary by workload, region, and model version. That’s why the table has filters and we publish the prompt set definition.

[08:12] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Q2: “Did you enable caching and batching? Isn’t that unfair?” Claire.

[08:18] Claire Dubois: We used reproducible, documented serving configurations. Modern production inference typically includes optimizations like continuous batching. We don’t hide that—we describe it. Importantly, the benchmark is not a microbenchmark of a single kernel; it’s a production-style measurement under load, with warmups and stability checks.

[08:39] Markus Klein: Add: “If you have a different caching posture, your results may differ.” Prospects appreciate that.

[08:45] Claire Dubois: Yes, and if you want to replicate with your own traffic shape, we can share a replication guide and you can compare.

[08:53] Noura El-Khoury: Q3: “Are these numbers stable? I’ve seen cloud regions be noisy.” Jonas.

[08:58] Jonas Weber: We expect variance, which is why we run repeated sweeps and apply stability gates. If p95 is out of family beyond a threshold, we rerun. We also timestamp the run window and publish the reporting period so you know when it was measured.

[09:15] Soojin Lee: And we should say we reserved capacity for the runs, so we weren’t competing with random background load.

[09:20] Jonas Weber: Right, we reserved capacity as part of the methodology.

[09:24] Noura El-Khoury: Q4: “Are you comparing to AWS/Bedrock/Together/etc?” Soojin.

[09:31] Soojin Lee: The report is primarily Redwood results across models and regions. Where we provide external context, we’re very explicit about scope and caveats. We’re not making broad claims about every provider or every workload. We encourage readers to use our methodology and reproduce for their own use case.

[09:52] Wei Chen: Important: don’t name a competitor unless asked. Keep it general.

[09:56] Soojin Lee: Yep.

[09:57] Noura El-Khoury: Q5: “How do I use this to decide Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private?” Markus.

[10:04] Markus Klein: Hosted is best for quick start and variable usage. Dedicated is for predictable throughput and isolation—if you have strict p95 SLOs or you want reserved capacity. Private is for regulated or data residency requirements, VPC controls, KMS/HSM integrations, and audit logs. The benchmark can help you pick a starting model/region, then we help you validate under your traffic and constraints.

[10:28] Ben Carter: And the CTA should route accordingly: devs to try the API, enterprises to sizing call.

[10:33] Noura El-Khoury: Good. Q6: “Can I get the raw data?” Felix.

[10:38] Felix Schneider: The interactive table supports export, and the report includes a downloadable dataset view. We redact anything sensitive; what you’ll get is aggregated benchmark results and the manifest metadata needed to interpret them.

[10:52] Jonas Weber: And we keep raw logs internally per retention policy, but for public we publish what’s safe.

[10:58] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Now last-minute edits. I captured a few, but let’s do quick round.

[11:04] Alicia Perez: Slide 6: the screenshot is outdated. The region dropdown is different now. I’ll update. Also, the disclaimer font needs to be larger.

[11:13] Wei Chen: Landing page copy: we still have one line that says “most cost-effective across providers” in the draft. That needs to be removed or softened to “cost-efficient in our tests on Redwood.”

[11:27] Soojin Lee: Yep, delete “across providers.”

[11:30] Ethan Wallace: In the script, I had “winner” once. I’ll remove.

[11:35] Jonas Weber: Also be consistent on “p95.” Sometimes we say “P ninety five.” Pick one.

[11:40] Noura El-Khoury: Say “p95” and if you need to clarify, “95th percentile latency.”

[11:46] Ben Carter: CTA slide: we should add both: “Try the interactive benchmark” and “Request Dedicated sizing.” But not too many links.

[11:55] Soojin Lee: We can do one primary CTA and one secondary.

[11:59] Noura El-Khoury: Works.

[12:01] Felix Schneider: One more: the tooltip on “latency/$” should define what the dollar is. It’s basically cost per output token in USD under the measured config. We should avoid confusion like “customer spend” vs “infrastructure cost.”

[12:16] Wei Chen: Yeah. We should say “estimated usage cost” or “usage cost at Redwood list price” depending on what it is.

[12:23] Jonas Weber: It is usage cost based on the pricing schedule for that deployment mode, yes.

[12:27] Soojin Lee: Then it’s “usage cost” not “infra cost.”

[12:31] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I’ll put that in the action items: define tooltip text and confirm the pricing reference.

[12:38] Noura El-Khoury: Time check. We’re good. Now I want to do a quick run from the end: closing and follow-up. Soojin, your close.

[12:47] Soojin Lee: Thanks everyone. The report and interactive table are live, and you can download the PDF. If you’re building on Hosted, start with the models and regions that fit your latency and cost constraints and try it with your prompts. If you need predictable throughput or data isolation, we can help you size Dedicated. And if you have VPC or on-prem requirements, talk to us about Private.

[13:12] Ben Carter: Then I say: you’ll get a follow-up email with links, the recording, and a short “how we measured” explainer.

[13:18] Noura El-Khoury: Nice. Also include: “post questions in chat” and “we’ll publish an FAQ.”

[13:25] Wei Chen: We have that FAQ page drafted.

[13:28] Soojin Lee: Great.

[13:30] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I think we’re done. I’ll read back the key action items.

[13:35] Noura El-Khoury: One, Felix: add tooltip/disclaimer next to latency slash dollar and link to methodology. Two, Wei: fix any “best/cheapest/across providers” language on landing page and confirm “usage cost” wording. Three, Alicia: update slide screenshots and bump disclaimer font for 720p readability. Four, Jonas and Claire: send me and Ethan a short methodology blurb we can repeat verbatim. Five, Ben: finalize follow-up emails and UTM tracking.

[14:05] Noura El-Khoury: Anything else before we stop recording?

[14:08] Ethan Wallace: Just one: I want a short line to address “you can reproduce it.” Like, “We’ll publish configs.”

[14:14] Claire Dubois: I can draft a sentence: “We publish the prompt mix, concurrency, warmup, and rerun rules; you can export the dataset and validate against your own workload.”

[14:24] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[14:26] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Stopping.

(Recording ends)

Producer note: The call continued for a few minutes off-record to coordinate calendar holds and confirm rehearsal deck versioning.

source.body:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Title: Benchmark report kickoff — latency/$ economics (internal)
Date: 2025-06-03
Time: 10:00–10:56 PT (recorded)
Duration: 56 min
Attendees (Redwood): Soojin Lee, Ethan Wallace, Ben Carter, Jonas Weber, Claire Dubois, Rina Kobayashi, Mateo Alvarez, Vanessa Ortiz, Neha Kapoor, Elliot Price, Wei Chen, Tyler Benson, Talia Benson

Summary (auto-generated):
- Team aligned on goals for a public benchmark report focused on production inference economics (latency per dollar) with reproducible methodology.
- Discussed scope: models/regions shortlist, workload design (prefill/decode mix), caching/batching policy, stability checks, and how to present variance.
- Confirmed launch assets: interactive web table, downloadable PDF, blog post, sales enablement kit, webinar.
- Identified legal/safe-claims constraints and the need for clear caveats vs competitor baselines.
- Assigned owners and immediate action items.

Topics (auto-generated):
1) What we’re publishing + why now
2) Methodology and fairness (workloads, prompt sets, caching/batching)
3) Regions, capacity reservations, run logistics
4) Messaging, disclaimers, and competitive language
5) Launch plan + funnel instrumentation

Action items (auto-generated):
- Share methodology v0.9 and prompt set proposal
- Draft capacity reservation plan
- Draft report outline and figure requirements
- Confirm artifact export + redaction requirements
- Create analytics event list and campaign tagging

Transcript:

[00:00] Soojin Lee: Alright, I think we’re at time. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the kickoff for the benchmark report—public-facing, focused on production inference economics, so like latency per dollar, not just raw tokens per second. Goal today is align on scope, owners, timeline, and like, what we will and won’t say.

[00:20] Talia Benson: Quick note, I’m recording and I’ll drop notes after. If anything sensitive comes up, call it out and we’ll flag for redaction.

[00:27] Ethan Wallace: Cool. And I’ll be annoying about narrative. Just warning everybody.

[00:31] Jonas Weber: Please be annoying, that’s helpful.

[00:34] Soojin Lee: Perfect. Let’s do a quick round of what each team needs out of this. I’ll start: PMM needs a credible, defensible story that’s consistent with what we can substantiate. Also Sales needs a talk track that doesn’t overclaim.

[00:48] Ben Carter: Growth perspective: we want this to drive signups and demo requests. So we need a landing page that converts, and instrumentation so we can attribute pipeline. Also, we should know what the “aha” is—like, what are we claiming Redwood is better at.

[01:02] Jonas Weber: Applied ML/perf side: we need methodology locked early. If we do “latency/$” it has to be well defined. Otherwise we’ll get roasted.

[01:11] Claire Dubois: Yup. And we need to make sure workloads reflect production distributions—prefill heavy vs decode heavy. Not just one synthetic prompt.

[01:18] Rina Kobayashi: From harness side: region sweeps are doable but capacity variability is real. We need reserved slots. Also we need to decide what artifacts are publishable—manifests, configs, etc.

[01:30] Mateo Alvarez: Runtime: we’re supportive, but I want to be clear: we are not going to ship kernel changes just to look good in a benchmark. Small guardrails or telemetry, fine. But not “benchmark-ware.”

[01:42] Neha Kapoor: Reliability: launch week traffic is a risk, plus we’ll get people hammering the page and maybe the API. So we need rate limit and caching plan.

[01:50] Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet: I need dates and region list. If you want NA, EU, APAC, I can try to reserve, but some regions are tight. We have to pick what’s realistic.

[02:01] Wei Chen: Docs/web copy: give me the disclaimers early. Also methodology summary needs to be readable, not a paper.

[02:09] Elliot Price: I’m here to help with the method write-up and make it consistent with how we talk about caching, batching, and what “typical” means.

[02:16] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s anchor on the “why now” and the deliverables. Ethan, can you summarize what’s shipping?

[02:24] Ethan Wallace: Sure. My current mental package is: 1) interactive table on the site—filter by model, region, context length maybe; 2) PDF report with narrative, charts, and a methodology section; 3) blog post that’s more digestible; 4) enablement kit—FAQ, talk track, maybe a one slide summary; and 5) webinar to walk through results and answer “is this fair.”

[02:47] Soojin Lee: Yes. And timeline wise, we’ve got like ~8 weeks in the strawman. We can adjust, but we should assume legal review and reviews will take time.

[03:00] Jonas Weber: Can I ask—are we planning any competitor baselines? Because that changes legal risk and methodology.

[03:06] Soojin Lee: Good question. Tentatively: we can include context, but we have to be cautious. We can say “here’s Redwood on X hardware with Y config.” If we do competitor numbers, it needs caveats and preferably from public sources or clearly stated measurement conditions.

[03:21] Ethan Wallace: I’d rather keep competitor references minimal in the first version. The story can be “this is what production economics look like; here’s how to evaluate.” But I know Sales will want comparison.

[03:34] Mateo Alvarez: Also competitor numbers are hard to make apples-to-apples. If someone runs AWS Bedrock with default settings vs our tuned runtime, people will argue.

[03:42] Claire Dubois: The Internet will argue regardless.

[03:44] (crosstalk)

[03:45] Ben Carter: If we don’t compare, do we still get pickup?

[03:48] Soojin Lee: We can still get pickup if the metric is novel and the methodology is strong. “Latency per dollar” and regional consistency is a good hook.

[03:56] Jonas Weber: On the technical side, we should publish enough artifacts so a skeptical engineer can reproduce. That’s the defense.

[04:06] Rina Kobayashi: +1, but we should plan redaction. Manifests can’t leak internal hostnames or request IDs.

[04:12] Wei Chen: And it has to be presented in a way that doesn’t scare off normal devs. Like “here’s the summary; here’s the full method if you want it.”

[04:21] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s talk methodology. Claire, can you walk through what you think “latency/$” should be?

[04:28] Claire Dubois: Yeah. So, we need to define the unit: dollars per something and latency per something. Usually we can compute cost per 1M tokens given our pricing or underlying GPU cost for dedicated. But since this is public and spans hosted, maybe we compute using published hosted price per token. Then “latency/$” could be like (tokens per second) divided by ($ per second) effectively, but that gets confusing. Better: “time to generate a fixed output length” and “cost for that generation.” Then we can do “seconds per $” or invert to “outputs per $.”

[05:05] Jonas Weber: Right now internally we’ve been doing something like: latency per request (p50, p95) and cost per 1M tokens, and then a combined metric we call latency-per-dollar as a normalized score. But we need to define it precisely.

[05:18] Mateo Alvarez: Please no “score” that looks like we’re cooking numbers.

[05:22] Claire Dubois: Agree. We should avoid a single magic score unless it’s literally a ratio with clear units.

[05:28] Ben Carter: Marketing-wise, units are good. “p95 latency at $X per 1M tokens” is understandable.

[05:35] Ethan Wallace: Could be a table that toggles: show p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost, then let people decide. Then we highlight a couple insights.

[05:46] Soojin Lee: Ok. Key methodology questions that keep coming up: caching/batching, concurrency, prompt sets, and warmups. Jonas, where are we right now?

[05:56] Jonas Weber: For a production-like benchmark, we should enable the things customers actually use. So continuous batching is on. For caching, it depends. Prefix caching can dramatically change economics for repeated prompts. If we enable it, we need to say under what cache hit rate. If we disable it, people will say we’re not showing real world savings.

[06:20] Elliot Price: I’ve seen this go sideways. We should separate: baseline run with cache off (to show raw serving performance) and an optional scenario with cache on with a defined synthetic hit rate or a defined workload that naturally has repeats.

[06:33] Claire Dubois: Another option: keep caching policy fixed—like “KV cache reuse off; standard attention KV cache obviously exists,” but no prefix reuse across requests. Then we can have a section: “if your workload has repeats, caching can improve cost/latency; here’s how to estimate.”

[06:51] Rina Kobayashi: That’s easiest to implement and defend. If we do cross-request caching, we need to model user behavior.

[06:58] Ben Carter: From funnel perspective, I’d rather not bury the lead in nuance, but I get it.

[07:05] Soojin Lee: Legal will also prefer less “it depends” but we have to be honest. We can message: “Benchmark uses conservative assumptions; your workload may see better results with caching.”

[07:17] Mateo Alvarez: Also, concurrency. If we run at low concurrency, we’ll look good on p50 but not throughput. If we run at high concurrency, we might degrade p95. We should pick something realistic.

[07:30] Claire Dubois: I propose we define multiple load points: concurrency 1 (single user), concurrency 8/16 (small production), and maybe 64 for stress. But that’s a lot of runs.

[07:43] Vanessa Ortiz: That is a lot of GPU hours.

[07:46] Jonas Weber: We can limit to two concurrencies: one “interactive” and one “service” mode. E.g., C=1 and C=16.

[07:55] Neha Kapoor: And we should run repeats across time windows to catch variance. Otherwise a region with a noisy neighbor day will look bad.

[08:05] Rina Kobayashi: We can add stability gates. Like if p95 variance > threshold across 3 runs, auto-rerun.

[08:13] Mateo Alvarez: That’s fine. But it adds time.

[08:16] Soojin Lee: Better time than a PR crisis.

[08:19] (laughter)

[08:22] Ethan Wallace: On prompt sets, what’s the plan? Because for narrative I need to say “we used a production mix.”

[08:29] Claire Dubois: We have an internal prompt set v2, but it’s a bit too “eval-y.” I want to update to something like v3 that includes: short chat turns, long context summarization, RAG-ish prompts, and structured output. Also include prefill-heavy tasks and decode-heavy tasks.

[08:50] Jonas Weber: And we should publish the categories and distributions, not necessarily the raw prompts if there’s any IP concerns.

[08:58] Wei Chen: If we don’t publish prompts, people may say it’s not reproducible.

[09:03] Elliot Price: We can publish hashed prompts or a synthetic open set. Or publish the generator code and category definitions.

[09:10] Rina Kobayashi: We can publish prompt IDs and lengths, and a small sample set. Full set maybe in a GitHub repo with review.

[09:20] Soojin Lee: Let’s park exact publication details, but align that we need reproducibility artifacts.

[09:26] Tyler Benson: Can I jump in on measurement outputs? For the web page, we need a stable schema. Model, region, context length bucket, concurrency, p50, p95, throughput, cost per 1M tokens, and a timestamp / run id. Also needs “methodology version.”

[09:44] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, we can do JSON + CSV export. But please decide early what dimensions we’re exposing because it affects data volume.

[09:53] Ben Carter: Also, for the interactive page, we want filters but not 20 knobs. Too many knobs hurts conversion.

[10:01] Ethan Wallace: Agree. We can default to the “recommended” workload and let advanced users toggle.

[10:06] Soojin Lee: Ok. Regions and capacity. Vanessa, can you outline constraints?

[10:12] Vanessa Ortiz: Sure. For NA we’re good: us-east and us-west are fine. For EU, eu-west is usually okay but we have some reserved commitments. APAC is the tricky one. ap-southeast sometimes has limited availability depending on instance type. If we want consistent results, we should reserve blocks and run all models in a tight window.

[10:34] Neha Kapoor: We also need to avoid running during known maintenance windows.

[10:38] Vanessa Ortiz: Right. And we need to choose instance types—like are we standardizing on one GPU class or are we “best available per region”? Because that affects fairness.

[10:50] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, we control the fleet but it’s not always identical across regions. For a benchmark, maybe we pin to a specific GPU for all runs, even if it’s not our default in one region.

[11:03] Jonas Weber: If we change fleet config just for the benchmark, it’s not representative.

[11:08] Mateo Alvarez: Not change permanently, but reserve identical slices.

[11:12] Soojin Lee: This is one of those tradeoffs. We want representative but also comparable. Maybe we present “this is what customers see in region X as-of date,” and we list the hardware. That’s honest.

[11:28] Claire Dubois: Yes. Publish the hardware profile per region. Then no one can say we hid it.

[11:34] Ethan Wallace: But does that create confusion? “Why is region A faster? Because different GPUs.”

[11:40] Ben Carter: That might be okay. People care about what they will experience.

[11:45] Wei Chen: If we do that, we need a tooltip that explains “hardware may differ by region.”

[11:51] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s shift to messaging and claims. I know Legal isn’t on this call, but we should plan the review. The big risk: “fastest” claims. We should avoid absolutes unless we can substantiate across all workloads.

[12:09] Ethan Wallace: So the core narrative could be: 1) production inference economics are multi-dimensional; 2) Redwood provides a consistent, optimized runtime; 3) here’s what we measured and how; 4) key insights: regional consistency, p95 stability, and cost efficiency.

[12:28] Jonas Weber: Also include reliability. Not just speed. People care about tail latency.

[12:33] Neha Kapoor: Yes please. p95 and error rates. We can report 5xx/429 rates during runs maybe, or at least note we excluded failed runs.

[12:42] Claire Dubois: If we include error rates, we need to define exclusion criteria. Like if a run has more than X% errors, discard and rerun.

[12:50] Rina Kobayashi: We already have rerun logic we can add. Or manual gates.

[12:55] Soojin Lee: Ok. On competitive, we can say “compared to common managed endpoints, you should evaluate with these factors.” But we shouldn’t say “2x faster than OpenAI” unless we have very strong evidence and permission.

[13:10] Ben Carter: Can we at least mention “AWS Bedrock” as an example of “general managed endpoints”?

[13:15] Soojin Lee: Mentioning as category is okay. Specific numerical comparisons are where we need legal sign-off.

[13:22] Wei Chen: For disclaimers, we should include: results depend on workload, concurrency, region, time of day, and model version. Also “as of date.”

[13:34] Elliot Price: And “we used Redwood-optimized configs.” People will ask if they can get the same. We should say these settings are available to customers (batching, etc.) and we’ll link to docs.

[13:46] Mateo Alvarez: As long as that’s true. Some configs might be internal defaults.

[13:50] Jonas Weber: We should only publish numbers for configurations that customers can actually select or that are default behavior.

[13:57] Soojin Lee: Great constraint: publish only supported configs.

[14:01] Talia Benson: I’m noting: “supported vs experimental” needs definition. We should align with Product.

[14:07] Soojin Lee: Yes, Henry isn’t here but we’ll pull him in for model/region “supported” list.

[14:14] Ben Carter: On the funnel, I want to propose CTAs per segment. For self-serve, “Get API key + credits.” For enterprise, “Talk to us about Dedicated/Private.” The benchmark page should route to both.

[14:28] Ethan Wallace: And we need a “how we measured” explainer that’s short, so people don’t bounce.

[14:33] Wei Chen: I can draft that as a collapsible section.

[14:36] Tyler Benson: Analytics events I’ll need: filter changes, export clicks, PDF download, CTA clicks, time on page, and scroll depth maybe. Also UTM capture.

[14:49] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s discuss timeline. Strawman: next 2 weeks we lock methodology and implement any required instrumentation, weeks 3-5 run sweeps, week 6 content, week 7 reviews, week 8 launch. Is that feasible?

[15:07] Rina Kobayashi: Feasible if we don’t have a million workload variants.

[15:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Feasible if you give me region list this week.

[15:14] Jonas Weber: Feasible if methodology is frozen by end of next week. Otherwise it slips.

[15:20] Ethan Wallace: I need early data to draft charts. Even partial results.

[15:25] Claire Dubois: We can do an early “pilot sweep” with 2 models, 2 regions, to validate harness and produce initial charts.

[15:34] Mateo Alvarez: Good. That will also reveal runtime regressions.

[15:39] Neha Kapoor: And it helps SRE plan for launch week traffic—what endpoints, caching, etc.

[15:45] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s list decisions we need this week.

[15:49] (pause)

[15:51] Soojin Lee: 1) Model shortlist. Jonas/Claire, can you propose? Keep it curated.

[15:58] Jonas Weber: We should include at least one small, one mid, one large model. Maybe a couple popular open weights. We should also consider embeddings? Or is it just generation?

[16:09] Ethan Wallace: For narrative, keep it to generation in v1, or it becomes too big.

[16:14] Ben Carter: Agree. Embeddings is a separate report.

[16:18] Claire Dubois: Ok, generation only. Proposed: a 7–8B class, a 13–14B class, a 70B-ish, and maybe one MoE if we support it. And we should pin model versions.

[16:33] Mateo Alvarez: Make sure they’re actually stable in production.

[16:36] Soojin Lee: 2) Region list: likely us-east, us-west, eu-west, and one APAC. Vanessa will advise.

[16:44] Vanessa Ortiz: I’d say ap-southeast if possible, or ap-northeast depending on capacity. I’ll confirm.

[16:51] Soojin Lee: 3) Workloads: at least two context length buckets, and two concurrencies. Claire to propose.

[16:58] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[17:00] Soojin Lee: 4) Caching stance: baseline without cross-request prefix caching; continuous batching on; publish that.

[17:08] Jonas Weber: Works. We should be explicit: we’re not modeling cache hit rates in the primary metric.

[17:14] Elliot Price: And we can add a sidebar: “If you have repeated prefixes, here’s how caching changes the math.”

[17:20] Soojin Lee: Great.

[17:22] (audio glitch)

[17:25] Ben Carter: One more: naming. Are we calling it “latency per dollar” or “latency/$” or “throughput per dollar”?

[17:33] Ethan Wallace: “Latency per dollar” is catchy but could be misunderstood. “Better latency at lower cost” is safer.

[17:41] Soojin Lee: We’ll test terms. But for now internally call it latency/$.

[17:47] Tyler Benson: If we’re using a combined metric, we need to decide if it’s ratio or two-axis plots.

[17:53] Jonas Weber: I’d prefer a frontier chart: latency vs cost. Then we can say “efficient frontier.”

[18:01] Ethan Wallace: Frontier chart is cool but maybe too nerdy. We can do both—frontier in PDF, table in web.

[18:10] Wei Chen: On web, keep it simple. Table + a couple charts.

[18:16] Soojin Lee: Ok. Let’s talk objections we anticipate. This will inform FAQ. Markus isn’t here, but we should seed it. What will engineers complain about?

[18:27] Jonas Weber: “Your workload isn’t my workload.”

[18:30] Claire Dubois: “You cherry-picked prompts.”

[18:33] Mateo Alvarez: “You tuned your runtime but not others.”

[18:36] Neha Kapoor: “Regions vary day to day.”

[18:39] Rina Kobayashi: “You hid errors and retries.”

[18:42] Ben Carter: “This doesn’t match what I see.”

[18:45] Soojin Lee: Great. So we need: clear methodology, versioning, run dates, hardware, and stability notes.

[18:55] Elliot Price: Also define how we treat streaming. Are we measuring time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token? People care.

[19:02] Claire Dubois: We should capture TTFT and TPOT maybe. But that multiplies metrics.

[19:09] Jonas Weber: At minimum we should report TTFT and end-to-end latency for a fixed token output. TTFT is key for chat.

[19:18] Mateo Alvarez: Streaming instrumentation exists; Omar can help, but not in this call.

[19:24] Soojin Lee: Let’s include TTFT + end-to-end p95 as core.

[19:29] Wei Chen: If we add TTFT, we need definitions on the page or people will misread.

[19:34] Ethan Wallace: That’s fine. We can do tooltips.

[19:38] Soojin Lee: Ok, I want to make sure we have clear owners. Jonas/Claire/Rina own benchmark design and data. Mateo/Neha/Vanessa own runtime+infra support and capacity. Ethan owns narrative. Ben+Tyler own funnel and analytics. Wei+Elliot own docs/methodology summary. I own the launch plan, message house, and sales enablement.

[20:04] Talia Benson: Captured.

[20:06] Soojin Lee: Now, open questions.

[20:09] Ben Carter: Question: Are we gating launch on an interactive page? Could we launch with PDF and add interactive later?

[20:17] Wei Chen: If we don’t have interactive, conversion might be lower.

[20:20] Ethan Wallace: Also press likes interactive.

[20:23] Jonas Weber: For credibility, interactive isn’t required. But it’s nice for transparency.

[20:30] Soojin Lee: We will plan for interactive as part of launch, but we can have a contingency: PDF + blog if web slips.

[20:39] Neha Kapoor: Please have contingency.

[20:42] (laughter)

[20:45] Rina Kobayashi: Another question: Do we include Dedicated and Private? Or just Hosted API? Because numbers differ.

[20:52] Soojin Lee: Good point. For v1, it’s easiest to benchmark Hosted because it’s consistent. But we should message “these optimizations also apply to Dedicated and Private” and Sales can use it as proof of runtime. We can add a section on how Dedicated/Private differ (isolation, residency, SLA).

[21:12] Mateo Alvarez: I agree. Benchmark Hosted, then position Dedicated/Private as deployment options with similar performance characteristics, but don’t publish those numbers unless we can standardize hardware.

[21:23] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[21:25] Ethan Wallace: For story, we can frame: “Same runtime across hosted/dedicated/private; benchmarks shown on hosted; ask us for dedicated sizing.”

[21:36] Ben Carter: That gives us enterprise CTA.

[21:39] Soojin Lee: Exactly.

[21:41] Tyler Benson: For attribution: we should define what counts as influenced pipeline. We’ll need campaign tag in HubSpot. I’ll file a request.

[21:49] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[21:52] (short pause)

[21:54] Claire Dubois: One more technical detail: warmup. We should standardize: discard first N requests or run for X minutes before recording. Otherwise cold starts will bias.

[22:04] Jonas Weber: Yes. Warmup + steady state. And note that we are measuring steady state, not cold start. We can mention cold start separately.

[22:14] Neha Kapoor: And maybe note autoscaling effects. But that’s complicated.

[22:18] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, autoscaling is part of reality. But for benchmark, we should fix capacity so queueing doesn’t dominate.

[22:27] Rina Kobayashi: Runner can pin capacity and then run a closed-loop load to keep concurrency constant.

[22:34] Claire Dubois: Great. Then we can attribute p95 to serving, not queue spikes.

[22:40] Soojin Lee: All right. Let’s close with concrete next steps and dates.

[22:45] Soojin Lee: Claire, you’ll circulate workload definition and prompt set plan by Friday.

[22:49] Claire Dubois: Yes.

[22:51] Soojin Lee: Rina, confirm runner + manifest fields, especially what we can publish.

[22:56] Rina Kobayashi: Yep.

[22:58] Soojin Lee: Vanessa, region capacity plan and proposed reservation windows.

[23:02] Vanessa Ortiz: Yep.

[23:04] Soojin Lee: Ethan, outline and figure list.

[23:07] Ethan Wallace: Yep, I’ll send a doc.

[23:09] Soojin Lee: Ben and Tyler, KPI plan and analytics events.

[23:13] Ben Carter: Yes.

[23:14] Tyler Benson: Yep.

[23:16] Soojin Lee: Wei and Elliot, draft methodology summary/disclaimers (v0) for the landing page.

[23:21] Wei Chen: Sounds good.

[23:22] Elliot Price: Yup.

[23:24] Soojin Lee: And I’ll schedule legal review and sales enablement review. Also I’ll set up a recurring benchmark standup Tue/Thu.

[23:33] Talia Benson: I’ll send calendar invite.

[23:36] Soojin Lee: Before we end—any blockers anyone wants to raise?

[23:41] Neha Kapoor: Just reiterating: launch traffic. Put SRE on the plan early. Also, if the interactive page pulls JSON, it needs caching and rate limiting.

[23:52] Mateo Alvarez: And from runtime: we’ll support but please avoid last-minute methodology changes.

[23:57] Claire Dubois: Understood.

[24:00] Soojin Lee: Great. Thanks all. I’ll send notes with owners and action items. Meeting adjourned.

[24:06] (Recording continues briefly; side chatter)

[24:08] Ethan Wallace: Jonas, can you sanity check the frontier chart idea with me?

[24:11] Jonas Weber: Yeah, ping me. Also we need to decide if we’re using p50 or p95 in that chart.

[24:15] (end)

Note: Fireflies transcript includes partial audio after the meeting ended; remaining recording was mostly silence and was truncated.

title: Benchmark report kickoff — latency/$ economics (internal)
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Title: Benchmark report kickoff — latency/$ economics (internal)
Date: 2025-06-03
Time: 10:00–10:56 PT (recorded)
Duration: 56 min
Attendees (Redwood): Soojin Lee, Ethan Wallace, Ben Carter, Jonas Weber, Claire Dubois, Rina Kobayashi, Mateo Alvarez, Vanessa Ortiz, Neha Kapoor, Elliot Price, Wei Chen, Tyler Benson, Talia Benson

Summary (auto-generated):
- Team aligned on goals for a public benchmark report focused on production inference economics (latency per dollar) with reproducible methodology.
- Discussed scope: models/regions shortlist, workload design (prefill/decode mix), caching/batching policy, stability checks, and how to present variance.
- Confirmed launch assets: interactive web table, downloadable PDF, blog post, sales enablement kit, webinar.
- Identified legal/safe-claims constraints and the need for clear caveats vs competitor baselines.
- Assigned owners and immediate action items.

Topics (auto-generated):
1) What we’re publishing + why now
2) Methodology and fairness (workloads, prompt sets, caching/batching)
3) Regions, capacity reservations, run logistics
4) Messaging, disclaimers, and competitive language
5) Launch plan + funnel instrumentation

Action items (auto-generated):
- Share methodology v0.9 and prompt set proposal
- Draft capacity reservation plan
- Draft report outline and figure requirements
- Confirm artifact export + redaction requirements
- Create analytics event list and campaign tagging

Transcript:

[00:00] Soojin Lee: Alright, I think we’re at time. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the kickoff for the benchmark report—public-facing, focused on production inference economics, so like latency per dollar, not just raw tokens per second. Goal today is align on scope, owners, timeline, and like, what we will and won’t say.

[00:20] Talia Benson: Quick note, I’m recording and I’ll drop notes after. If anything sensitive comes up, call it out and we’ll flag for redaction.

[00:27] Ethan Wallace: Cool. And I’ll be annoying about narrative. Just warning everybody.

[00:31] Jonas Weber: Please be annoying, that’s helpful.

[00:34] Soojin Lee: Perfect. Let’s do a quick round of what each team needs out of this. I’ll start: PMM needs a credible, defensible story that’s consistent with what we can substantiate. Also Sales needs a talk track that doesn’t overclaim.

[00:48] Ben Carter: Growth perspective: we want this to drive signups and demo requests. So we need a landing page that converts, and instrumentation so we can attribute pipeline. Also, we should know what the “aha” is—like, what are we claiming Redwood is better at.

[01:02] Jonas Weber: Applied ML/perf side: we need methodology locked early. If we do “latency/$” it has to be well defined. Otherwise we’ll get roasted.

[01:11] Claire Dubois: Yup. And we need to make sure workloads reflect production distributions—prefill heavy vs decode heavy. Not just one synthetic prompt.

[01:18] Rina Kobayashi: From harness side: region sweeps are doable but capacity variability is real. We need reserved slots. Also we need to decide what artifacts are publishable—manifests, configs, etc.

[01:30] Mateo Alvarez: Runtime: we’re supportive, but I want to be clear: we are not going to ship kernel changes just to look good in a benchmark. Small guardrails or telemetry, fine. But not “benchmark-ware.”

[01:42] Neha Kapoor: Reliability: launch week traffic is a risk, plus we’ll get people hammering the page and maybe the API. So we need rate limit and caching plan.

[01:50] Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet: I need dates and region list. If you want NA, EU, APAC, I can try to reserve, but some regions are tight. We have to pick what’s realistic.

[02:01] Wei Chen: Docs/web copy: give me the disclaimers early. Also methodology summary needs to be readable, not a paper.

[02:09] Elliot Price: I’m here to help with the method write-up and make it consistent with how we talk about caching, batching, and what “typical” means.

[02:16] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s anchor on the “why now” and the deliverables. Ethan, can you summarize what’s shipping?

[02:24] Ethan Wallace: Sure. My current mental package is: 1) interactive table on the site—filter by model, region, context length maybe; 2) PDF report with narrative, charts, and a methodology section; 3) blog post that’s more digestible; 4) enablement kit—FAQ, talk track, maybe a one slide summary; and 5) webinar to walk through results and answer “is this fair.”

[02:47] Soojin Lee: Yes. And timeline wise, we’ve got like ~8 weeks in the strawman. We can adjust, but we should assume legal review and reviews will take time.

[03:00] Jonas Weber: Can I ask—are we planning any competitor baselines? Because that changes legal risk and methodology.

[03:06] Soojin Lee: Good question. Tentatively: we can include context, but we have to be cautious. We can say “here’s Redwood on X hardware with Y config.” If we do competitor numbers, it needs caveats and preferably from public sources or clearly stated measurement conditions.

[03:21] Ethan Wallace: I’d rather keep competitor references minimal in the first version. The story can be “this is what production economics look like; here’s how to evaluate.” But I know Sales will want comparison.

[03:34] Mateo Alvarez: Also competitor numbers are hard to make apples-to-apples. If someone runs AWS Bedrock with default settings vs our tuned runtime, people will argue.

[03:42] Claire Dubois: The Internet will argue regardless.

[03:44] (crosstalk)

[03:45] Ben Carter: If we don’t compare, do we still get pickup?

[03:48] Soojin Lee: We can still get pickup if the metric is novel and the methodology is strong. “Latency per dollar” and regional consistency is a good hook.

[03:56] Jonas Weber: On the technical side, we should publish enough artifacts so a skeptical engineer can reproduce. That’s the defense.

[04:06] Rina Kobayashi: +1, but we should plan redaction. Manifests can’t leak internal hostnames or request IDs.

[04:12] Wei Chen: And it has to be presented in a way that doesn’t scare off normal devs. Like “here’s the summary; here’s the full method if you want it.”

[04:21] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s talk methodology. Claire, can you walk through what you think “latency/$” should be?

[04:28] Claire Dubois: Yeah. So, we need to define the unit: dollars per something and latency per something. Usually we can compute cost per 1M tokens given our pricing or underlying GPU cost for dedicated. But since this is public and spans hosted, maybe we compute using published hosted price per token. Then “latency/$” could be like (tokens per second) divided by ($ per second) effectively, but that gets confusing. Better: “time to generate a fixed output length” and “cost for that generation.” Then we can do “seconds per $” or invert to “outputs per $.”

[05:05] Jonas Weber: Right now internally we’ve been doing something like: latency per request (p50, p95) and cost per 1M tokens, and then a combined metric we call latency-per-dollar as a normalized score. But we need to define it precisely.

[05:18] Mateo Alvarez: Please no “score” that looks like we’re cooking numbers.

[05:22] Claire Dubois: Agree. We should avoid a single magic score unless it’s literally a ratio with clear units.

[05:28] Ben Carter: Marketing-wise, units are good. “p95 latency at $X per 1M tokens” is understandable.

[05:35] Ethan Wallace: Could be a table that toggles: show p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost, then let people decide. Then we highlight a couple insights.

[05:46] Soojin Lee: Ok. Key methodology questions that keep coming up: caching/batching, concurrency, prompt sets, and warmups. Jonas, where are we right now?

[05:56] Jonas Weber: For a production-like benchmark, we should enable the things customers actually use. So continuous batching is on. For caching, it depends. Prefix caching can dramatically change economics for repeated prompts. If we enable it, we need to say under what cache hit rate. If we disable it, people will say we’re not showing real world savings.

[06:20] Elliot Price: I’ve seen this go sideways. We should separate: baseline run with cache off (to show raw serving performance) and an optional scenario with cache on with a defined synthetic hit rate or a defined workload that naturally has repeats.

[06:33] Claire Dubois: Another option: keep caching policy fixed—like “KV cache reuse off; standard attention KV cache obviously exists,” but no prefix reuse across requests. Then we can have a section: “if your workload has repeats, caching can improve cost/latency; here’s how to estimate.”

[06:51] Rina Kobayashi: That’s easiest to implement and defend. If we do cross-request caching, we need to model user behavior.

[06:58] Ben Carter: From funnel perspective, I’d rather not bury the lead in nuance, but I get it.

[07:05] Soojin Lee: Legal will also prefer less “it depends” but we have to be honest. We can message: “Benchmark uses conservative assumptions; your workload may see better results with caching.”

[07:17] Mateo Alvarez: Also, concurrency. If we run at low concurrency, we’ll look good on p50 but not throughput. If we run at high concurrency, we might degrade p95. We should pick something realistic.

[07:30] Claire Dubois: I propose we define multiple load points: concurrency 1 (single user), concurrency 8/16 (small production), and maybe 64 for stress. But that’s a lot of runs.

[07:43] Vanessa Ortiz: That is a lot of GPU hours.

[07:46] Jonas Weber: We can limit to two concurrencies: one “interactive” and one “service” mode. E.g., C=1 and C=16.

[07:55] Neha Kapoor: And we should run repeats across time windows to catch variance. Otherwise a region with a noisy neighbor day will look bad.

[08:05] Rina Kobayashi: We can add stability gates. Like if p95 variance > threshold across 3 runs, auto-rerun.

[08:13] Mateo Alvarez: That’s fine. But it adds time.

[08:16] Soojin Lee: Better time than a PR crisis.

[08:19] (laughter)

[08:22] Ethan Wallace: On prompt sets, what’s the plan? Because for narrative I need to say “we used a production mix.”

[08:29] Claire Dubois: We have an internal prompt set v2, but it’s a bit too “eval-y.” I want to update to something like v3 that includes: short chat turns, long context summarization, RAG-ish prompts, and structured output. Also include prefill-heavy tasks and decode-heavy tasks.

[08:50] Jonas Weber: And we should publish the categories and distributions, not necessarily the raw prompts if there’s any IP concerns.

[08:58] Wei Chen: If we don’t publish prompts, people may say it’s not reproducible.

[09:03] Elliot Price: We can publish hashed prompts or a synthetic open set. Or publish the generator code and category definitions.

[09:10] Rina Kobayashi: We can publish prompt IDs and lengths, and a small sample set. Full set maybe in a GitHub repo with review.

[09:20] Soojin Lee: Let’s park exact publication details, but align that we need reproducibility artifacts.

[09:26] Tyler Benson: Can I jump in on measurement outputs? For the web page, we need a stable schema. Model, region, context length bucket, concurrency, p50, p95, throughput, cost per 1M tokens, and a timestamp / run id. Also needs “methodology version.”

[09:44] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, we can do JSON + CSV export. But please decide early what dimensions we’re exposing because it affects data volume.

[09:53] Ben Carter: Also, for the interactive page, we want filters but not 20 knobs. Too many knobs hurts conversion.

[10:01] Ethan Wallace: Agree. We can default to the “recommended” workload and let advanced users toggle.

[10:06] Soojin Lee: Ok. Regions and capacity. Vanessa, can you outline constraints?

[10:12] Vanessa Ortiz: Sure. For NA we’re good: us-east and us-west are fine. For EU, eu-west is usually okay but we have some reserved commitments. APAC is the tricky one. ap-southeast sometimes has limited availability depending on instance type. If we want consistent results, we should reserve blocks and run all models in a tight window.

[10:34] Neha Kapoor: We also need to avoid running during known maintenance windows.

[10:38] Vanessa Ortiz: Right. And we need to choose instance types—like are we standardizing on one GPU class or are we “best available per region”? Because that affects fairness.

[10:50] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, we control the fleet but it’s not always identical across regions. For a benchmark, maybe we pin to a specific GPU for all runs, even if it’s not our default in one region.

[11:03] Jonas Weber: If we change fleet config just for the benchmark, it’s not representative.

[11:08] Mateo Alvarez: Not change permanently, but reserve identical slices.

[11:12] Soojin Lee: This is one of those tradeoffs. We want representative but also comparable. Maybe we present “this is what customers see in region X as-of date,” and we list the hardware. That’s honest.

[11:28] Claire Dubois: Yes. Publish the hardware profile per region. Then no one can say we hid it.

[11:34] Ethan Wallace: But does that create confusion? “Why is region A faster? Because different GPUs.”

[11:40] Ben Carter: That might be okay. People care about what they will experience.

[11:45] Wei Chen: If we do that, we need a tooltip that explains “hardware may differ by region.”

[11:51] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s shift to messaging and claims. I know Legal isn’t on this call, but we should plan the review. The big risk: “fastest” claims. We should avoid absolutes unless we can substantiate across all workloads.

[12:09] Ethan Wallace: So the core narrative could be: 1) production inference economics are multi-dimensional; 2) Redwood provides a consistent, optimized runtime; 3) here’s what we measured and how; 4) key insights: regional consistency, p95 stability, and cost efficiency.

[12:28] Jonas Weber: Also include reliability. Not just speed. People care about tail latency.

[12:33] Neha Kapoor: Yes please. p95 and error rates. We can report 5xx/429 rates during runs maybe, or at least note we excluded failed runs.

[12:42] Claire Dubois: If we include error rates, we need to define exclusion criteria. Like if a run has more than X% errors, discard and rerun.

[12:50] Rina Kobayashi: We already have rerun logic we can add. Or manual gates.

[12:55] Soojin Lee: Ok. On competitive, we can say “compared to common managed endpoints, you should evaluate with these factors.” But we shouldn’t say “2x faster than OpenAI” unless we have very strong evidence and permission.

[13:10] Ben Carter: Can we at least mention “AWS Bedrock” as an example of “general managed endpoints”?

[13:15] Soojin Lee: Mentioning as category is okay. Specific numerical comparisons are where we need legal sign-off.

[13:22] Wei Chen: For disclaimers, we should include: results depend on workload, concurrency, region, time of day, and model version. Also “as of date.”

[13:34] Elliot Price: And “we used Redwood-optimized configs.” People will ask if they can get the same. We should say these settings are available to customers (batching, etc.) and we’ll link to docs.

[13:46] Mateo Alvarez: As long as that’s true. Some configs might be internal defaults.

[13:50] Jonas Weber: We should only publish numbers for configurations that customers can actually select or that are default behavior.

[13:57] Soojin Lee: Great constraint: publish only supported configs.

[14:01] Talia Benson: I’m noting: “supported vs experimental” needs definition. We should align with Product.

[14:07] Soojin Lee: Yes, Henry isn’t here but we’ll pull him in for model/region “supported” list.

[14:14] Ben Carter: On the funnel, I want to propose CTAs per segment. For self-serve, “Get API key + credits.” For enterprise, “Talk to us about Dedicated/Private.” The benchmark page should route to both.

[14:28] Ethan Wallace: And we need a “how we measured” explainer that’s short, so people don’t bounce.

[14:33] Wei Chen: I can draft that as a collapsible section.

[14:36] Tyler Benson: Analytics events I’ll need: filter changes, export clicks, PDF download, CTA clicks, time on page, and scroll depth maybe. Also UTM capture.

[14:49] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s discuss timeline. Strawman: next 2 weeks we lock methodology and implement any required instrumentation, weeks 3-5 run sweeps, week 6 content, week 7 reviews, week 8 launch. Is that feasible?

[15:07] Rina Kobayashi: Feasible if we don’t have a million workload variants.

[15:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Feasible if you give me region list this week.

[15:14] Jonas Weber: Feasible if methodology is frozen by end of next week. Otherwise it slips.

[15:20] Ethan Wallace: I need early data to draft charts. Even partial results.

[15:25] Claire Dubois: We can do an early “pilot sweep” with 2 models, 2 regions, to validate harness and produce initial charts.

[15:34] Mateo Alvarez: Good. That will also reveal runtime regressions.

[15:39] Neha Kapoor: And it helps SRE plan for launch week traffic—what endpoints, caching, etc.

[15:45] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s list decisions we need this week.

[15:49] (pause)

[15:51] Soojin Lee: 1) Model shortlist. Jonas/Claire, can you propose? Keep it curated.

[15:58] Jonas Weber: We should include at least one small, one mid, one large model. Maybe a couple popular open weights. We should also consider embeddings? Or is it just generation?

[16:09] Ethan Wallace: For narrative, keep it to generation in v1, or it becomes too big.

[16:14] Ben Carter: Agree. Embeddings is a separate report.

[16:18] Claire Dubois: Ok, generation only. Proposed: a 7–8B class, a 13–14B class, a 70B-ish, and maybe one MoE if we support it. And we should pin model versions.

[16:33] Mateo Alvarez: Make sure they’re actually stable in production.

[16:36] Soojin Lee: 2) Region list: likely us-east, us-west, eu-west, and one APAC. Vanessa will advise.

[16:44] Vanessa Ortiz: I’d say ap-southeast if possible, or ap-northeast depending on capacity. I’ll confirm.

[16:51] Soojin Lee: 3) Workloads: at least two context length buckets, and two concurrencies. Claire to propose.

[16:58] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[17:00] Soojin Lee: 4) Caching stance: baseline without cross-request prefix caching; continuous batching on; publish that.

[17:08] Jonas Weber: Works. We should be explicit: we’re not modeling cache hit rates in the primary metric.

[17:14] Elliot Price: And we can add a sidebar: “If you have repeated prefixes, here’s how caching changes the math.”

[17:20] Soojin Lee: Great.

[17:22] (audio glitch)

[17:25] Ben Carter: One more: naming. Are we calling it “latency per dollar” or “latency/$” or “throughput per dollar”?

[17:33] Ethan Wallace: “Latency per dollar” is catchy but could be misunderstood. “Better latency at lower cost” is safer.

[17:41] Soojin Lee: We’ll test terms. But for now internally call it latency/$.

[17:47] Tyler Benson: If we’re using a combined metric, we need to decide if it’s ratio or two-axis plots.

[17:53] Jonas Weber: I’d prefer a frontier chart: latency vs cost. Then we can say “efficient frontier.”

[18:01] Ethan Wallace: Frontier chart is cool but maybe too nerdy. We can do both—frontier in PDF, table in web.

[18:10] Wei Chen: On web, keep it simple. Table + a couple charts.

[18:16] Soojin Lee: Ok. Let’s talk objections we anticipate. This will inform FAQ. Markus isn’t here, but we should seed it. What will engineers complain about?

[18:27] Jonas Weber: “Your workload isn’t my workload.”

[18:30] Claire Dubois: “You cherry-picked prompts.”

[18:33] Mateo Alvarez: “You tuned your runtime but not others.”

[18:36] Neha Kapoor: “Regions vary day to day.”

[18:39] Rina Kobayashi: “You hid errors and retries.”

[18:42] Ben Carter: “This doesn’t match what I see.”

[18:45] Soojin Lee: Great. So we need: clear methodology, versioning, run dates, hardware, and stability notes.

[18:55] Elliot Price: Also define how we treat streaming. Are we measuring time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token? People care.

[19:02] Claire Dubois: We should capture TTFT and TPOT maybe. But that multiplies metrics.

[19:09] Jonas Weber: At minimum we should report TTFT and end-to-end latency for a fixed token output. TTFT is key for chat.

[19:18] Mateo Alvarez: Streaming instrumentation exists; Omar can help, but not in this call.

[19:24] Soojin Lee: Let’s include TTFT + end-to-end p95 as core.

[19:29] Wei Chen: If we add TTFT, we need definitions on the page or people will misread.

[19:34] Ethan Wallace: That’s fine. We can do tooltips.

[19:38] Soojin Lee: Ok, I want to make sure we have clear owners. Jonas/Claire/Rina own benchmark design and data. Mateo/Neha/Vanessa own runtime+infra support and capacity. Ethan owns narrative. Ben+Tyler own funnel and analytics. Wei+Elliot own docs/methodology summary. I own the launch plan, message house, and sales enablement.

[20:04] Talia Benson: Captured.

[20:06] Soojin Lee: Now, open questions.

[20:09] Ben Carter: Question: Are we gating launch on an interactive page? Could we launch with PDF and add interactive later?

[20:17] Wei Chen: If we don’t have interactive, conversion might be lower.

[20:20] Ethan Wallace: Also press likes interactive.

[20:23] Jonas Weber: For credibility, interactive isn’t required. But it’s nice for transparency.

[20:30] Soojin Lee: We will plan for interactive as part of launch, but we can have a contingency: PDF + blog if web slips.

[20:39] Neha Kapoor: Please have contingency.

[20:42] (laughter)

[20:45] Rina Kobayashi: Another question: Do we include Dedicated and Private? Or just Hosted API? Because numbers differ.

[20:52] Soojin Lee: Good point. For v1, it’s easiest to benchmark Hosted because it’s consistent. But we should message “these optimizations also apply to Dedicated and Private” and Sales can use it as proof of runtime. We can add a section on how Dedicated/Private differ (isolation, residency, SLA).

[21:12] Mateo Alvarez: I agree. Benchmark Hosted, then position Dedicated/Private as deployment options with similar performance characteristics, but don’t publish those numbers unless we can standardize hardware.

[21:23] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[21:25] Ethan Wallace: For story, we can frame: “Same runtime across hosted/dedicated/private; benchmarks shown on hosted; ask us for dedicated sizing.”

[21:36] Ben Carter: That gives us enterprise CTA.

[21:39] Soojin Lee: Exactly.

[21:41] Tyler Benson: For attribution: we should define what counts as influenced pipeline. We’ll need campaign tag in HubSpot. I’ll file a request.

[21:49] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[21:52] (short pause)

[21:54] Claire Dubois: One more technical detail: warmup. We should standardize: discard first N requests or run for X minutes before recording. Otherwise cold starts will bias.

[22:04] Jonas Weber: Yes. Warmup + steady state. And note that we are measuring steady state, not cold start. We can mention cold start separately.

[22:14] Neha Kapoor: And maybe note autoscaling effects. But that’s complicated.

[22:18] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, autoscaling is part of reality. But for benchmark, we should fix capacity so queueing doesn’t dominate.

[22:27] Rina Kobayashi: Runner can pin capacity and then run a closed-loop load to keep concurrency constant.

[22:34] Claire Dubois: Great. Then we can attribute p95 to serving, not queue spikes.

[22:40] Soojin Lee: All right. Let’s close with concrete next steps and dates.

[22:45] Soojin Lee: Claire, you’ll circulate workload definition and prompt set plan by Friday.

[22:49] Claire Dubois: Yes.

[22:51] Soojin Lee: Rina, confirm runner + manifest fields, especially what we can publish.

[22:56] Rina Kobayashi: Yep.

[22:58] Soojin Lee: Vanessa, region capacity plan and proposed reservation windows.

[23:02] Vanessa Ortiz: Yep.

[23:04] Soojin Lee: Ethan, outline and figure list.

[23:07] Ethan Wallace: Yep, I’ll send a doc.

[23:09] Soojin Lee: Ben and Tyler, KPI plan and analytics events.

[23:13] Ben Carter: Yes.

[23:14] Tyler Benson: Yep.

[23:16] Soojin Lee: Wei and Elliot, draft methodology summary/disclaimers (v0) for the landing page.

[23:21] Wei Chen: Sounds good.

[23:22] Elliot Price: Yup.

[23:24] Soojin Lee: And I’ll schedule legal review and sales enablement review. Also I’ll set up a recurring benchmark standup Tue/Thu.

[23:33] Talia Benson: I’ll send calendar invite.

[23:36] Soojin Lee: Before we end—any blockers anyone wants to raise?

[23:41] Neha Kapoor: Just reiterating: launch traffic. Put SRE on the plan early. Also, if the interactive page pulls JSON, it needs caching and rate limiting.

[23:52] Mateo Alvarez: And from runtime: we’ll support but please avoid last-minute methodology changes.

[23:57] Claire Dubois: Understood.

[24:00] Soojin Lee: Great. Thanks all. I’ll send notes with owners and action items. Meeting adjourned.

[24:06] (Recording continues briefly; side chatter)

[24:08] Ethan Wallace: Jonas, can you sanity check the frontier chart idea with me?

[24:11] Jonas Weber: Yeah, ping me. Also we need to decide if we’re using p50 or p95 in that chart.

[24:15] (end)

Note: Fireflies transcript includes partial audio after the meeting ended; remaining recording was mostly silence and was truncated.
text:
